The euro is accepted in [Campione d'Italia](/wiki/Campione_d%27Italia "Campione d'Italia"), but its official currency is the [Swiss franc](/wiki/Swiss_franc "Swiss franc").[[11]](#cite_note-11) 3. To call Campione d'Italia, it is necessary to use the Swiss code [+41](/wiki/%2B41 "+41"). | |

**Italy**,[[a]](#cite_note-12) officially the **Italian Republic**,[[b]](#cite_note-13) is a country in [Southern](/wiki/Southern_Europe "Southern Europe") and [Western Europe](/wiki/Western_Europe "Western Europe").[[c]](#cite_note-WE-15) It consists of [a peninsula](/wiki/Italian_Peninsula "Italian Peninsula") that extends into the [Mediterranean Sea](/wiki/Mediterranean_Sea "Mediterranean Sea"), with the [Alps](/wiki/Alps "Alps") on its northern land border, as well as [nearly 800 islands](/wiki/List_of_islands_of_Italy "List of islands of Italy"), notably [Sicily](/wiki/Sicily "Sicily") and [Sardinia](/wiki/Sardinia "Sardinia"). Italy shares land borders with [France](/wiki/France "France") to the west; [Switzerland](/wiki/Switzerland "Switzerland") and [Austria](/wiki/Austria "Austria") to the north; [Slovenia](/wiki/Slovenia "Slovenia") to the east; and the two [enclaves](/wiki/Enclave "Enclave") of [Vatican City](/wiki/Vatican_City "Vatican City") and [San Marino](/wiki/San_Marino "San Marino"). It is the [tenth-largest country in Europe by area](/wiki/List_of_European_countries_by_area "List of European countries by area"), covering 301,340 km2 (116,350 sq mi), and the third-most populous [member state of the European Union](/wiki/Member_state_of_the_European_Union "Member state of the European Union"), with nearly 59 million inhabitants. Italy's capital and [largest city](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_Italy "List of cities in Italy") is [Rome](/wiki/Rome "Rome"); other major cities include [Milan](/wiki/Milan "Milan") (the largest [metropolitan area](/wiki/Metropolitan_area "Metropolitan area") in the country), [Naples](/wiki/Naples "Naples"), [Turin](/wiki/Turin "Turin"), [Palermo](/wiki/Palermo "Palermo"), [Bologna](/wiki/Bologna "Bologna"), [Florence](/wiki/Florence "Florence"), [Genoa](/wiki/Genoa "Genoa"), and [Venice](/wiki/Venice "Venice").

The [history of Italy](/wiki/History_of_Italy "History of Italy") goes back to numerous [Italic peoples](/wiki/List_of_ancient_peoples_of_Italy "List of ancient peoples of Italy"), notably including the [ancient Romans](/wiki/Ancient_Romans "Ancient Romans"), who conquered the Mediterranean world during the [Roman Republic](/wiki/Roman_Republic "Roman Republic") and ruled it for centuries during the [Roman Empire](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire"). With the spread of Christianity, Rome became the seat of the [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") and the [Papacy](/wiki/Papacy "Papacy"). [Barbarian invasions](/wiki/Barbarian_invasions "Barbarian invasions") and other factors led to the decline and [fall of the Western Roman Empire](/wiki/Fall_of_the_Western_Roman_Empire "Fall of the Western Roman Empire") between [late antiquity](/wiki/Late_antiquity "Late antiquity") and the [Early Middle Ages](/wiki/Early_Middle_Ages "Early Middle Ages"). By the 11th century, [Italian city-states](/wiki/Italian_city-states "Italian city-states") and [maritime republics](/wiki/Maritime_republics "Maritime republics") expanded, bringing renewed prosperity through commerce and laying the groundwork for modern capitalism. The [Italian Renaissance](/wiki/Italian_Renaissance "Italian Renaissance") flourished during the 15th and 16th centuries and [spread to the rest of Europe](/wiki/Renaissance#Spread "Renaissance"). Italian explorers discovered new routes to the Far East and the [New World](/wiki/New_World "New World"), contributing significantly to the [Age of Discovery](/wiki/Age_of_Discovery "Age of Discovery").

After centuries of political and territorial divisions, the [Kingdom of Italy](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Italy "Kingdom of Italy") was established in 1861, following [wars of independence](/wiki/Italian_War_of_Independence "Italian War of Independence") and the [Expedition of the Thousand](/wiki/Expedition_of_the_Thousand "Expedition of the Thousand"). From the late 19th to the early 20th century, [Italy industrialised](/wiki/Industrialization_in_Italy "Industrialization in Italy") and acquired [a colonial empire](/wiki/Italian_Empire "Italian Empire"), while [the south](/wiki/Southern_Italy "Southern Italy") remained largely impoverished, fuelling [a large immigrant diaspora](/wiki/Italian_diaspora "Italian diaspora") to the Americas. From 1915 to 1918, [Italy took part](/wiki/Military_history_of_Italy_during_World_War_I "Military history of Italy during World War I") in [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I") with the [Entente](/wiki/Allies_of_World_War_I "Allies of World War I") against the [Central Powers](/wiki/Central_Powers "Central Powers"). In 1922, the [Italian fascist](/wiki/Italian_fascist "Italian fascist") dictatorship was established. [During World War II](/wiki/Military_history_of_Italy_during_World_War_II "Military history of Italy during World War II"), Italy was first part of the [Axis](/wiki/Axis_powers "Axis powers") until [an armistice](/wiki/Armistice_of_Cassibile "Armistice of Cassibile") with the [Allied powers](/wiki/Allies_of_World_War_II "Allies of World War II") (1940–1943), then a co-belligerent of the Allies during the [Italian resistance](/wiki/Italian_resistance "Italian resistance") and [liberation of Italy](/wiki/Liberation_of_Italy "Liberation of Italy") from German occupation and the collaborationist [RSI](/wiki/Italian_Social_Republic "Italian Social Republic") (1943–1945). In 1946, the monarchy [was replaced by a republic](/wiki/1946_Italian_institutional_referendum "1946 Italian institutional referendum") and the country made [a strong recovery](/wiki/Italian_economic_miracle "Italian economic miracle").

A [developed country](/wiki/Developed_country "Developed country") with [an advanced economy](/wiki/Economy_of_Italy "Economy of Italy"), Italy has the [eighth-largest nominal GDP](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_GDP_(nominal) "List of countries by GDP (nominal)") in the world, the [second-largest manufacturing sector](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_manufacturing_output "List of countries by manufacturing output") in Europe, and [plays a significant role in regional](/wiki/Regional_power "Regional power") and – [to a lesser extent](/wiki/Least_of_the_great_powers "Least of the great powers") – global economic, military, cultural, and political affairs. It is [a founding](/wiki/Inner_Six "Inner Six") and [leading member](/wiki/EU_three "EU three") of the European Union, and [is part of numerous other international organisations and forums](/wiki/Foreign_relations_of_Italy#International_institutions "Foreign relations of Italy"). As a [cultural superpower](/wiki/Cultural_superpower "Cultural superpower"), Italy has long been a renowned global centre [of art](/wiki/Italian_art "Italian art"), [music](/wiki/Music_of_Italy "Music of Italy"), [literature](/wiki/Italian_literature "Italian literature"), [cuisine](/wiki/Italian_cuisine "Italian cuisine"), [fashion](/wiki/Italian_fashion "Italian fashion"), [science and technology](/wiki/Science_and_technology_in_Italy "Science and technology in Italy"), and the source of [multiple inventions and discoveries](/wiki/List_of_Italian_inventions_and_discoveries "List of Italian inventions and discoveries"). It has the [highest number](/wiki/World_Heritage_Sites_by_country "World Heritage Sites by country") of [World Heritage Sites](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site "World Heritage Site") ([61](/wiki/List_of_World_Heritage_Sites_in_Italy "List of World Heritage Sites in Italy")) and is the [fifth-most visited country](/wiki/World_Tourism_rankings "World Tourism rankings") in the world.

## Name

Main article: [Name of Italy](/wiki/Name_of_Italy "Name of Italy")

Hypotheses for the etymology of *Italia* are numerous.[[13]](#cite_note-16) One theory suggests it originated from an [Ancient Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek "Ancient Greek") term for the land of the *Italói*, a tribe that resided in the region now known as [Calabria](/wiki/Calabria "Calabria"). Originally thought to be named *Vituli*, some scholars suggest their [totemic](/wiki/Totem "Totem") animal to be the calf ([Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin"): *vitulus*; [Umbrian](/wiki/Umbrian "Umbrian"): *vitlo*; [Oscan](/wiki/Oscan "Oscan"): *Víteliú*).[[14]](#cite_note-17) Several ancient authors said it was named after a local ruler [Italus](/wiki/Italus "Italus").[[15]](#cite_note-18)

The ancient Greek term for Italy initially referred only to the south of the [Bruttium](/wiki/Calabria "Calabria") peninsula and parts of [Catanzaro](/wiki/Catanzaro "Catanzaro") and [Vibo Valentia](/wiki/Vibo_Valentia "Vibo Valentia"). The larger concept of [Oenotria](/wiki/Oenotria "Oenotria") and "Italy" became synonymous, and the name applied to most of [Lucania](/wiki/Lucania "Lucania") as well. Before the Roman Republic's expansion, the name was used by Greeks for the land between the [strait of Messina](/wiki/Strait_of_Messina "Strait of Messina") and the line connecting the [gulfs of Salerno](/wiki/Gulf_of_Salerno "Gulf of Salerno") and [Taranto](/wiki/Gulf_of_Taranto "Gulf of Taranto"), corresponding to Calabria. The Greeks came to apply "Italia" to a larger region.[[16]](#cite_note-19) In addition to the "[Greek Italy](/wiki/Greek_Italy "Greek Italy")" in the south, historians have suggested the existence of an "Etruscan Italy", which consisted of areas of central Italy.[[17]](#cite_note-20)

The borders of [Roman Italy](/wiki/Roman_Italy "Roman Italy"), *Italia*, are better established. Cato's *[Origines](/wiki/Origines "Origines")* describes Italy as the entire peninsula south of the Alps.[[18]](#cite_note-21) In 264 BC, Roman Italy extended from the [Arno](/wiki/Arno "Arno") and [Rubicon](/wiki/Rubicon "Rubicon") rivers of the centre-north to the entire south. The northern area, [Cisalpine Gaul](/wiki/Cisalpine_Gaul "Cisalpine Gaul"), considered geographically part of Italy, was occupied by Rome in the 220s BC,[[19]](#cite_note-22) but remained politically separated. It was legally merged into the administrative unit of Italy in 42 BC.[[20]](#cite_note-23) Sardinia, [Corsica](/wiki/Corsica "Corsica"), Sicily, and [Malta](/wiki/Malta "Malta") were added to Italy by [Diocletian](/wiki/Diocletian "Diocletian") in 292 AD,[[21]](#cite_note-24) which made late-ancient Italy coterminous with the modern [Italian geographical region](/wiki/Italian_geographical_region "Italian geographical region").[[22]](#cite_note-25)

The Latin *Italicus* was used to describe "a man of Italy" as opposed to a *provincial*, or one from the [Roman province](/wiki/Roman_province "Roman province").[[23]](#cite_note-26) The adjective *italianus*, from which *Italian* was derived, is from [medieval Latin](/wiki/Medieval_Latin "Medieval Latin") and was used alternatively with *Italicus* during the [early modern period](/wiki/Early_modern_period "Early modern period").[[24]](#cite_note-27) After the [fall of the Western Roman Empire](/wiki/Fall_of_the_Western_Roman_Empire "Fall of the Western Roman Empire"), the [Ostrogothic Kingdom](/wiki/Ostrogothic_Kingdom "Ostrogothic Kingdom") of Italy was created. After the [Lombard](/wiki/Lombards "Lombards") invasions, *Italia* was retained as the name for their kingdom, and its [successor kingdom](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Italy_(Holy_Roman_Empire) "Kingdom of Italy (Holy Roman Empire)") within the [Holy Roman Empire](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Empire "Holy Roman Empire").[[25]](#cite_note-28)

## History

Main article: [History of Italy](/wiki/History_of_Italy "History of Italy")

### Prehistory and antiquity

Main articles: [Prehistoric Italy](/wiki/Prehistoric_Italy "Prehistoric Italy"), [Italic peoples](/wiki/Italic_peoples "Italic peoples"), [Etruscan civilisation](/wiki/Etruscan_civilisation "Etruscan civilisation"), [Greek colonisation](/wiki/Greek_colonisation "Greek colonisation"), and [Magna Graecia](/wiki/Magna_Graecia "Magna Graecia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/75/Etruscan_Painting_1.jpg/250px-Etruscan_Painting_1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Etruscan_Painting_1.jpg)

[Etruscan](/wiki/Etruscan_civilization "Etruscan civilization") fresco in the [Monterozzi necropolis](/wiki/Monterozzi_necropolis "Monterozzi necropolis"), 5th century BC

[Lower Paleolithic](/wiki/Lower_Paleolithic "Lower Paleolithic") artefacts, dating back 850,000 years, have been recovered from [Monte Poggiolo](/wiki/Monte_Poggiolo "Monte Poggiolo").[[26]](#cite_note-29) Excavations throughout Italy revealed a [Neanderthal](/wiki/Neanderthal "Neanderthal") presence in the Middle Palaeolithic period 200,000 years ago,[[27]](#cite_note-30) while [modern humans](/wiki/Early_modern_human "Early modern human") appeared about 40,000 years ago at [Riparo Mochi](/wiki/Riparo_Mochi "Riparo Mochi").[[28]](#cite_note-31)

The [ancient peoples](/wiki/Ancient_peoples_of_Italy "Ancient peoples of Italy") of pre-Roman Italy were [Indo-European](/wiki/Proto-Indo-Europeans "Proto-Indo-Europeans"), specifically the [Italic peoples](/wiki/Italic_peoples "Italic peoples"). The main historic peoples of possible non-Indo-European or [pre-Indo-European](/wiki/Pre-Indo-European_languages "Pre-Indo-European languages") heritage include the [Etruscans](/wiki/Etruscans "Etruscans"), the [Elymians](/wiki/Elymians "Elymians") and [Sicani](/wiki/Sicani "Sicani") of Sicily, and the prehistoric [Sardinians](/wiki/Sardinians "Sardinians"), who gave birth to the [Nuragic civilisation](/wiki/Nuragic_civilisation "Nuragic civilisation"). Other ancient populations include the [Rhaetian people](/wiki/Rhaetian_people "Rhaetian people") and [Camunni](/wiki/Camunni "Camunni"), known for their [rock drawings in Valcamonica](/wiki/Rock_drawings_in_Valcamonica "Rock drawings in Valcamonica").[[29]](#cite_note-32) A natural mummy, [Ötzi](/wiki/%C3%96tzi "Ötzi"), dated 3400–3100 BC, was discovered in the [Similaun](/wiki/Similaun "Similaun") glacier in 1991.[[30]](#cite_note-33)

The first colonisers were the [Phoenicians](/wiki/Phoenicia "Phoenicia"), who established [emporiums](/wiki/Emporium_(antiquity) "Emporium (antiquity)") on the coasts of Sicily and Sardinia. Some became small urban centres and developed parallel to [Greek colonies](/wiki/Greek_colonies "Greek colonies").[[31]](#cite_note-34) During the 8th and 7th centuries, Greek colonies were established at [Pithecusae](/wiki/Pithecusae "Pithecusae"), eventually extending along the south of the Italian Peninsula and the coast of Sicily, an area later known as [Magna Graecia](/wiki/Magna_Graecia "Magna Graecia").[[32]](#cite_note-35) [Ionians](/wiki/Ionians "Ionians"), [Doric](/wiki/Doric_Greek "Doric Greek") colonists, [Syracusans](/wiki/Syracusan "Syracusan"), and the [Achaeans](/wiki/Achaeans_(tribe) "Achaeans (tribe)") founded various cities. [Greek colonisation](/wiki/Greek_colonization_of_Italy "Greek colonization of Italy") placed the [Italic peoples](/wiki/Italic_peoples "Italic peoples") in contact with democratic forms of government and high artistic and cultural expressions.[[33]](#cite_note-36)

### Ancient Rome

Main articles: [Ancient Rome](/wiki/Ancient_Rome "Ancient Rome"), [Roman expansion in Italy](/wiki/Roman_expansion_in_Italy "Roman expansion in Italy"), and [Roman Italy](/wiki/Roman_Italy "Roman Italy")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/de/Colosseo_2020.jpg/250px-Colosseo_2020.jpg)](/wiki/File:Colosseo_2020.jpg)

The [Colosseum](/wiki/Colosseum "Colosseum"), widely considered one of the greatest works of architecture and engineering of ancient history

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/00/Roman_Empire_Trajan_117AD.png/250px-Roman_Empire_Trajan_117AD.png)](/wiki/File:Roman_Empire_Trajan_117AD.png)

The [Roman Empire](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire") in AD 117 at its greatest extent[[34]](#cite_note-37)

[Vassal states](/wiki/Vassal_state "Vassal state")

Italy's history goes back to numerous [Italic peoples](/wiki/List_of_ancient_peoples_of_Italy "List of ancient peoples of Italy") – notably including the [ancient Romans](/wiki/Ancient_Romans "Ancient Romans"), who conquered the Mediterranean world during the [Roman Republic](/wiki/Roman_Republic "Roman Republic") and ruled it for centuries during the [Roman Empire](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire").[[35]](#cite_note-38)

Ancient Rome, a settlement on the [River Tiber](/wiki/River_Tiber "River Tiber") in central Italy, [founded](/wiki/Founding_of_Rome "Founding of Rome") in 753 BC, was ruled for 244 years by a monarchical system.[[36]](#cite_note-39) In 509 BC, the Romans, favouring a government of the Senate and the People ([SPQR](/wiki/SPQR "SPQR")), [expelled the monarchy](/wiki/Overthrow_of_the_Roman_monarchy "Overthrow of the Roman monarchy") and established an oligarchic republic. [*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The Italian Peninsula, named *Italia*, was consolidated into a unified entity during Roman expansion, the conquest of new territories often at the expense of the [other Italic tribes](/wiki/Samnite_Wars "Samnite Wars"), [Etruscans](/wiki/Roman%E2%80%93Etruscan_Wars "Roman–Etruscan Wars"), [Celts](/wiki/Roman%E2%80%93Gallic_wars "Roman–Gallic wars"), and [Greeks](/wiki/Pyrrhic_War "Pyrrhic War"). A permanent association, with most of the local tribes and cities, was formed, and Rome began the conquest of Western Europe, North Africa, and the [Middle East](/wiki/History_of_the_Middle_East#Greek_and_Roman_Empire "History of the Middle East"). In the wake of [Julius Caesar](/wiki/Julius_Caesar "Julius Caesar")'s assassination in 44 BC, Rome grew into a massive empire stretching from [Britain](/wiki/Roman_Britain "Roman Britain") to the borders of [Persia](/wiki/Mesopotamia_(Roman_province) "Mesopotamia (Roman province)"), engulfing the whole Mediterranean basin, in which Greek, Roman, and other cultures merged into a powerful civilisation. The long reign of the first emperor, [Augustus](/wiki/Augustus "Augustus"), began an age of peace and prosperity. Roman Italy remained the [metropole](/wiki/Metropole "Metropole") of the empire, homeland of the Romans and territory of the capital.[[37]](#cite_note-40)

The [first two centuries of the empire](/wiki/History_of_the_Roman_Empire "History of the Roman Empire") saw a period of unprecedented stability known as the [Pax Romana](/wiki/Pax_Romana "Pax Romana") (lit. 'Roman Peace'). Rome reached its [greatest territorial extent](/wiki/Borders_of_the_Roman_Empire "Borders of the Roman Empire") under [Trajan](/wiki/Trajan "Trajan") (r. 98–117 AD), but a period of increasing trouble and decline began under [Commodus](/wiki/Commodus "Commodus") (r. 180–192).[[38]](#cite_note-41)[[39]](#cite_note-42) The [Migration Period](/wiki/Migration_Period "Migration Period"), involving [large invasions by Germanic peoples](/wiki/Germanic%E2%80%93Roman_contacts "Germanic–Roman contacts"), led to the decline of the Roman Empire.

The Roman Empire was among the largest in history, wielding great economical, cultural, political, and military power.[[40]](#cite_note-43) At its greatest extent, it had an area of 5 million square kilometres (1.9 million square miles).[[41]](#cite_note-44) The [Roman legacy](/wiki/Legacy_of_the_Roman_Empire "Legacy of the Roman Empire") has deeply influenced Western civilisation shaping the modern world. The widespread use of [Romance languages](/wiki/Romance_languages "Romance languages") derived from Latin, [numerical system](/wiki/Roman_numerals "Roman numerals"), modern Western alphabet and calendar, and the emergence of Christianity as a world religion, are among the many legacies of Roman dominance.[[42]](#cite_note-45)

### Middle Ages

Main article: [Italy in the Middle Ages](/wiki/Italy_in_the_Middle_Ages "Italy in the Middle Ages")

After the [fall of the Western Roman Empire](/wiki/Fall_of_the_Western_Roman_Empire "Fall of the Western Roman Empire"), Italy fell under the [Odoacer's kingdom](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Italy_(476%E2%80%93493) "Kingdom of Italy (476–493)"), and was seized by the [Ostrogoths](/wiki/Ostrogoths "Ostrogoths").[[43]](#cite_note-46) Invasions resulted in a chaotic succession of kingdoms and the supposed "[Dark Ages](/wiki/Dark_Ages_(historiography) "Dark Ages (historiography)")". The invasion of another [Germanic tribe](/wiki/Germanic_peoples "Germanic peoples") in the 6th century, the [Lombards](/wiki/Lombards "Lombards"), reduced Byzantine presence and ended the political unity of the peninsula. The north formed the [Lombard kingdom](/wiki/Lombard_kingdom "Lombard kingdom"), central-south was also controlled by the Lombards, and other parts remained Byzantine.[[44]](#cite_note-47)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b0/Marco_Polo_Mosaic_from_Palazzo_Tursi.jpg/250px-Marco_Polo_Mosaic_from_Palazzo_Tursi.jpg)](/wiki/File:Marco_Polo_Mosaic_from_Palazzo_Tursi.jpg)

[Marco Polo](/wiki/Marco_Polo "Marco Polo"), 13th-century explorer

The Lombard kingdom was absorbed into [Francia](/wiki/Francia "Francia") by [Charlemagne](/wiki/Charlemagne "Charlemagne") in the late 8th century and became the Kingdom of Italy.[[45]](#cite_note-48) The Franks helped form the [Papal States](/wiki/Papal_States "Papal States"). Until the 13th century, politics was dominated by relations between the [Holy Roman Emperors](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Emperor "Holy Roman Emperor") and the Papacy, with city-states siding with the former ([Ghibellines](/wiki/Ghibellines "Ghibellines")) or with the latter ([Guelphs](/wiki/Guelphs "Guelphs")) for momentary advantage.[[46]](#cite_note-49) The Germanic emperor and Roman pontiff became the [universal powers](/wiki/Universal_power "Universal power") of medieval Europe. However, conflict over the [Investiture controversy](/wiki/Investiture_controversy "Investiture controversy") and between Guelphs and Ghibellines ended the imperial-feudal system in the north, where cities gained independence.[[47]](#cite_note-50) In 1176, the [Lombard League](/wiki/Lombard_League "Lombard League") of city-states defeated Holy Roman Emperor [Frederick Barbarossa](/wiki/Frederick_Barbarossa "Frederick Barbarossa"), ensuring their independence.

City-states – e.g. [Milan](/wiki/Milan "Milan"), [Florence](/wiki/Florence "Florence"), Venice – played a crucially innovative role in financial development by devising banking practices, and enabling new forms of social organisation.[[48]](#cite_note-51) In coastal and southern areas, maritime republics dominated the Mediterranean and monopolised trade to the Orient. They were independent [thalassocratic](/wiki/Thalassocratic "Thalassocratic") city-states, in which merchants had considerable power. Although oligarchical, the relative political freedom they afforded was conducive to academic and artistic advancement.[[49]](#cite_note-Lane-52) The best-known maritime republics were Venice, [Genoa](/wiki/Republic_of_Genoa "Republic of Genoa"), [Pisa](/wiki/Republic_of_Pisa "Republic of Pisa"), and [Amalfi](/wiki/Duchy_of_Amalfi "Duchy of Amalfi").[[50]](#cite_note-53) Each had dominion over overseas lands, islands, lands on the Adriatic, Aegean, and Black seas, and commercial colonies in the Near East and North Africa.[[51]](#cite_note-54)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/50/Naval_jack_of_Italy.svg/120px-Naval_jack_of_Italy.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Naval_jack_of_Italy.svg)

[![Map](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/23/Republik_Venedig_Handelswege01-IT.png/250px-Republik_Venedig_Handelswege01-IT.png)](/wiki/File:Republik_Venedig_Handelswege01-IT.png)

Left: flag of the [Italian Navy](/wiki/Italian_Navy "Italian Navy"). Clockwise, from upper left: the coat of arms of [Venice](/wiki/Republic_of_Venice "Republic of Venice"), [Genoa](/wiki/Republic_of_Genoa "Republic of Genoa"), [Pisa](/wiki/Republic_of_Pisa "Republic of Pisa") and [Amalfi](/wiki/Duchy_of_Amalfi "Duchy of Amalfi").  
Right: trade routes, colonies of the [Genoa](/wiki/Genoese_colonies "Genoese colonies") and [Venice](/wiki/Stato_da_M%C3%A0r "Stato da Màr").

Venice and Genoa were Europe's gateways to the East, and producers of fine glass, while Florence was a centre of silk, wool, banking, and jewellery. The wealth generated enabled the commissioning of large public and private artistic projects. The republics participated in the [Crusades](/wiki/Crusades "Crusades"), providing support and transport but primarily pursuing political and commercial opportunities.[[49]](#cite_note-Lane-52) Italy first felt the economic changes which led to the [commercial revolution](/wiki/Commercial_revolution "Commercial revolution"): Venice was able to [sack Byzantine's capital](/wiki/Sack_of_Constantinople "Sack of Constantinople") and finance [Marco Polo](/wiki/Marco_Polo "Marco Polo")'s voyages to Asia; the first universities were formed in Italian cities, and scholars such as [Aquinas](/wiki/Aquinas "Aquinas") obtained international fame; capitalism and banking families emerged in Florence, where [Dante](/wiki/Dante "Dante") and [Giotto](/wiki/Giotto "Giotto") were active around 1300.[[52]](#cite_note-See-55) In the south, Sicily had become an [Arab Islamic emirate](/wiki/Emirate_of_Sicily "Emirate of Sicily") in the 9th century, thriving until the [Italo-Normans](/wiki/Italo-Normans "Italo-Normans") conquered it in the late 11th century, together with most of the Lombard and Byzantine principalities of southern Italy.[[53]](#cite_note-56) The region was subsequently divided between the [Kingdom of Sicily](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Sicily "Kingdom of Sicily") and [Kingdom of Naples](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Naples "Kingdom of Naples").[[d]](#cite_note-57)[[54]](#cite_note-58) The [Black Death](/wiki/Black_Death "Black Death") of 1348 killed perhaps a third of Italy's population.[[55]](#cite_note-59)

### Early modern period

Main articles: [Italian Renaissance](/wiki/Italian_Renaissance "Italian Renaissance") and [History of early modern Italy](/wiki/History_of_early_modern_Italy "History of early modern Italy")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/aa/Italy_1494.svg/250px-Italy_1494.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Italy_1494.svg)

[Italian states](/wiki/List_of_historic_states_of_Italy#Late_Middle_Ages "List of historic states of Italy") before the [Italian Wars](/wiki/Italian_Wars "Italian Wars") in 1494

During the 1400s and 1500s, Italy was the birthplace and heart of the [Renaissance](/wiki/Renaissance "Renaissance"). This era marked the transition from the medieval period to the modern age and was fostered by the wealth accumulated by merchant cities and the patronage of dominant families.[[56]](#cite_note-strathern-60) Italian polities were now regional states effectively ruled by princes, in control of trade and administration, and their courts became centres of the arts and sciences. These princedoms were led by political dynasties and merchant families, such as the [Medici](/wiki/Medici "Medici") of Florence. After the end of the [Western Schism](/wiki/Western_Schism "Western Schism"), newly elected [Pope Martin V](/wiki/Pope_Martin_V "Pope Martin V") returned to the [Papal States](/wiki/Papal_States "Papal States") and restored Italy as the sole centre of Western Christianity. The [Medici Bank](/wiki/Medici_Bank "Medici Bank") was made the credit institution of the Papacy, and significant ties were established between the Church and new political dynasties.[[56]](#cite_note-strathern-60)[[57]](#cite_note-61)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/ba/Leonardo_self.jpg/250px-Leonardo_self.jpg)](/wiki/File:Leonardo_self.jpg)

[Leonardo da Vinci](/wiki/Leonardo_da_Vinci "Leonardo da Vinci"), quintessential [Renaissance man](/wiki/Renaissance_man "Renaissance man"), in a self-portrait (c. 1512)

In 1453, despite activity by [Pope Nicholas V](/wiki/Pope_Nicholas_V "Pope Nicholas V") to support the Byzantines, the city of [Constantinople](/wiki/Constantinople "Constantinople") fell to the [Ottomans](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire"). This led to the migration of [Greek scholars](/wiki/Greek_scholars_in_the_Renaissance "Greek scholars in the Renaissance") and texts to Italy, fuelling the rediscovery of Greek [humanism](/wiki/Humanism "Humanism").[[58]](#cite_note-62) Humanist rulers such as [Federico da Montefeltro](/wiki/Federico_da_Montefeltro "Federico da Montefeltro") and [Pope Pius II](/wiki/Pope_Pius_II "Pope Pius II") worked to establish [ideal cities](/wiki/Ideal_city "Ideal city"), founding [Urbino](/wiki/Urbino "Urbino") and [Pienza](/wiki/Pienza "Pienza"). [Pico della Mirandola](/wiki/Pico_della_Mirandola "Pico della Mirandola") wrote the *[Oration on the Dignity of Man](/wiki/Oration_on_the_Dignity_of_Man "Oration on the Dignity of Man")*, considered the manifesto of the Renaissance. In the arts, the Italian Renaissance exercised a dominant influence on European art for centuries, with artists such as [Leonardo da Vinci](/wiki/Leonardo_da_Vinci "Leonardo da Vinci"), [Botticelli](/wiki/Botticelli "Botticelli"), [Michelangelo](/wiki/Michelangelo "Michelangelo"), [Raphael](/wiki/Raphael "Raphael"), [Giotto](/wiki/Giotto "Giotto"), [Donatello](/wiki/Donatello "Donatello"), and [Titian](/wiki/Titian "Titian"), and architects such as [Filippo Brunelleschi](/wiki/Filippo_Brunelleschi "Filippo Brunelleschi"), [Andrea Palladio](/wiki/Andrea_Palladio "Andrea Palladio"), and [Donato Bramante](/wiki/Donato_Bramante "Donato Bramante"). Italian [explorers](/wiki/List_of_Italian_explorers "List of Italian explorers") and navigators from the maritime republics, eager to find an alternative route to the Indies to bypass the Ottomans, offered their services to monarchs of Atlantic countries and played a key role in ushering the [Age of Discovery](/wiki/Age_of_Discovery "Age of Discovery") and colonisation of the Americas. The most notable were: [Christopher Columbus](/wiki/Christopher_Columbus "Christopher Columbus"), who opened the Americas for conquest by Europeans;[[59]](#cite_note-63) [John Cabot](/wiki/John_Cabot "John Cabot"), the first European to explore North America since the [Norse](/wiki/Norsemen "Norsemen");[[60]](#cite_note-64) and [Amerigo Vespucci](/wiki/Amerigo_Vespucci "Amerigo Vespucci"), for whom the continent of [America](/wiki/Naming_of_the_Americas "Naming of the Americas") is named.[[61]](#cite_note-65)[[62]](#cite_note-66)

A defensive alliance known as the [Italic League](/wiki/Italic_League "Italic League") was formed between Venice, Naples, Florence, Milan, and the Papacy. [Lorenzo *the Magnificent* de Medici](/wiki/Lorenzo_de%27_Medici "Lorenzo de' Medici") was the Renaissance's greatest patron, his support allowed the League to [abort invasion](/wiki/Siege_of_Otranto "Siege of Otranto") by the Turks. The alliance, however, collapsed in the 1490s; the invasion of [Charles VIII of France](/wiki/Charles_VIII_of_France "Charles VIII of France") initiated a series of wars in the peninsula. During the [High Renaissance](/wiki/High_Renaissance "High Renaissance"), popes such as [Julius II](/wiki/Julius_II "Julius II") (1503–1513) fought for control of Italy against foreign monarchs; [Paul III](/wiki/Paul_III "Paul III") (1534–1549) preferred to mediate between the European powers to secure peace. In the middle of such conflicts, the Medici popes [Leo X](/wiki/Leo_X "Leo X") (1513–1521) and [Clement VII](/wiki/Clement_VII "Clement VII") (1523–1534) faced the [Protestant Reformation](/wiki/Protestant_Reformation "Protestant Reformation") in Germany, England and elsewhere.

In 1559, at the end of the [Italian wars](/wiki/Italian_wars "Italian wars") between France and the Habsburgs, about half of Italy (the southern Kingdoms of [Naples](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Naples "Kingdom of Naples"), [Sicily](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Sicily "Kingdom of Sicily"), [Sardinia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Sardinia "Kingdom of Sardinia"), and the [Duchy of Milan](/wiki/Duchy_of_Milan "Duchy of Milan")) was under Spanish rule, while the other half remained independent (many states continued to be formally part of the Holy Roman Empire). The Papacy launched the [Counter-Reformation](/wiki/Counter-Reformation "Counter-Reformation"), whose key events include: the [Council of Trent](/wiki/Council_of_Trent "Council of Trent") (1545–1563); adoption of the [Gregorian calendar](/wiki/Gregorian_calendar "Gregorian calendar"); the [Jesuit China mission](/wiki/Jesuit_China_mission "Jesuit China mission"); the [French Wars of Religion](/wiki/French_Wars_of_Religion "French Wars of Religion"); end of the [Thirty Years' War](/wiki/Thirty_Years%27_War "Thirty Years' War") (1618–1648); and the [Great Turkish War](/wiki/Great_Turkish_War "Great Turkish War"). The Italian economy declined in the 1600s and 1700s.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/03/Flag_of_the_Cispadane_Republic.svg/250px-Flag_of_the_Cispadane_Republic.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Flag_of_the_Cispadane_Republic.svg)

Flag of the [Cispadane Republic](/wiki/Cispadane_Republic "Cispadane Republic"), the first [Italian tricolour](/wiki/Italian_tricolour "Italian tricolour") adopted by a sovereign Italian state (1797)

During the [War of the Spanish Succession](/wiki/War_of_the_Spanish_Succession "War of the Spanish Succession") (1700–1714), Austria acquired most of the Spanish domains in Italy, namely Milan, Naples and Sardinia; the latter was given to the House of Savoy in exchange for Sicily in 1720. Later, a branch of the Bourbons ascended to the throne of Sicily and Naples. During the [Napoleonic Wars](/wiki/Napoleonic_Wars "Napoleonic Wars"), north and central Italy were reorganised as [Sister Republics](/wiki/Sister_Republics "Sister Republics") of France and, later, as a [Kingdom of Italy](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Italy_(Napoleonic) "Kingdom of Italy (Napoleonic)").[[63]](#cite_note-67) The south was administered by [Joachim Murat](/wiki/Joachim_Murat "Joachim Murat"), Napoleon's brother-in-law. 1814's [Congress of Vienna](/wiki/Congress_of_Vienna "Congress of Vienna") restored the situation of the late 18th century, but the ideals of the [French Revolution](/wiki/French_Revolution "French Revolution") could not be eradicated, and re-surfaced during the [political upheavals](/wiki/Political_upheaval "Political upheaval") that characterised the early 19th century. The first adoption of the [Italian tricolour](/wiki/Italian_tricolour "Italian tricolour") by an Italian state, the [Cispadane Republic](/wiki/Cispadane_Republic "Cispadane Republic"), occurred during [Napoleonic Italy](/wiki/Flags_of_Napoleonic_Italy "Flags of Napoleonic Italy"), following the French Revolution, which advocated national [self-determination](/wiki/Self-determination "Self-determination").[[64]](#cite_note-68) This event is celebrated by [Tricolour Day](/wiki/Tricolour_Day "Tricolour Day").[[65]](#cite_note-69)

### Unification

Main article: [Unification of Italy](/wiki/Unification_of_Italy "Unification of Italy")

The [birth of the Kingdom of Italy](/wiki/Birth_of_the_Kingdom_of_Italy "Birth of the Kingdom of Italy") was the result of efforts of Italian nationalists and monarchists loyal to the [House of Savoy](/wiki/House_of_Savoy "House of Savoy") to establish a united kingdom encompassing the entire [Italian Peninsula](/wiki/Italian_Peninsula "Italian Peninsula"). By the mid-19th century, rising [Italian nationalism](/wiki/Italian_nationalism "Italian nationalism") led to revolution.[[66]](#cite_note-70) Following the [Congress of Vienna](/wiki/Congress_of_Vienna "Congress of Vienna") in 1815, the political and social Italian unification movement, or [Risorgimento](/wiki/Risorgimento "Risorgimento"), emerged to unite Italy by consolidating the states and liberating them from foreign control. A radical figure was the patriotic journalist [Giuseppe Mazzini](/wiki/Giuseppe_Mazzini "Giuseppe Mazzini"), founder of the political movement [Young Italy](/wiki/Young_Italy "Young Italy") in the 1830s, who favoured a unitary republic and advocated a broad nationalist movement. 1847 saw the first public performance of "[Il Canto degli Italiani](/wiki/Il_Canto_degli_Italiani "Il Canto degli Italiani")", which became the national anthem in 1946.[[67]](#cite_note-71)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1a/Giuseppe_Mazzini.jpg/250px-Giuseppe_Mazzini.jpg)](/wiki/File:Giuseppe_Mazzini.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5e/Giuseppe_Garibaldi_1861.jpg/250px-Giuseppe_Garibaldi_1861.jpg)](/wiki/File:Giuseppe_Garibaldi_1861.jpg)

[Giuseppe Mazzini](/wiki/Giuseppe_Mazzini "Giuseppe Mazzini") (left), highly influential leader of the Italian revolutionary movement; and [Giuseppe Garibaldi](/wiki/Giuseppe_Garibaldi "Giuseppe Garibaldi") (right), celebrated as one of the greatest generals of modern times[[68]](#cite_note-scholar_and_patriot-72) and as the "Hero of the Two Worlds" because of his military enterprises in South America and Europe,[[69]](#cite_note-73) who fought in many military campaigns that led to [Italian unification](/wiki/Italian_unification "Italian unification")

The most famous member of Young Italy was the revolutionary and general [Giuseppe Garibaldi](/wiki/Giuseppe_Garibaldi "Giuseppe Garibaldi")[[70]](#cite_note-74) who led the republican drive for unification in southern Italy. However, the Italian monarchy of the House of Savoy, in the [Kingdom of Sardinia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Sardinia_(1720%E2%80%931861) "Kingdom of Sardinia (1720–1861)"), whose government was led by [Camillo Benso, Count of Cavour](/wiki/Camillo_Benso,_Count_of_Cavour "Camillo Benso, Count of Cavour"), also had ambitions of establishing a united Italian state. In the context of the [1848 liberal revolutions](/wiki/1848_liberal_revolutions "1848 liberal revolutions") that swept Europe, an unsuccessful [First Italian War of Independence](/wiki/First_Italian_War_of_Independence "First Italian War of Independence") was declared against [Austria](/wiki/Austria-Hungary "Austria-Hungary"). In 1855, Sardinia became an ally of Britain and France in the [Crimean War](/wiki/Crimean_War "Crimean War").[[71]](#cite_note-75) Sardinia fought the Austrian Empire in the [Second Italian War of Independence](/wiki/Second_Italian_War_of_Independence "Second Italian War of Independence") of 1859, with the aid of France, resulting in liberating [Lombardy](/wiki/Lombardy "Lombardy"). On the basis of the [Plombières Agreement](/wiki/Plombi%C3%A8res_Agreement "Plombières Agreement"), the Sardinia ceded [Savoy](/wiki/Savoy "Savoy") and [Nice](/wiki/Nice "Nice") to France, an event that caused the [Niçard exodus](/wiki/Ni%C3%A7ard_exodus "Niçard exodus").[[72]](#cite_note-76)

In 1860–1861, Garibaldi led the drive for unification in Naples and Sicily.[[73]](#cite_note-77) [Teano](/wiki/Teano "Teano") was the site of a famous meeting between Garibaldi and [Victor Emmanuel II](/wiki/Victor_Emmanuel_II "Victor Emmanuel II"), the last king of Sardinia, during which Garibaldi shook Victor Emanuel's hand and hailed him as [King of Italy](/wiki/King_of_Italy "King of Italy"). Cavour agreed to include Garibaldi's southern Italy in a union with the Kingdom of Sardinia in 1860. This allowed the Sardinian government to [declare a united Italian kingdom](/wiki/Proclamation_of_the_Kingdom_of_Italy "Proclamation of the Kingdom of Italy") on 17 March 1861,[[74]](#cite_note-78) with Victor Emmanuel II as its first king. In 1865, the kingdom's capital was moved from Turin to Florence. In 1866, Victor Emmanuel II, allied with [Prussia](/wiki/Prussia "Prussia") during the [Austro-Prussian War](/wiki/Austro-Prussian_War "Austro-Prussian War"), waged the [Third Italian War of Independence](/wiki/Third_Italian_War_of_Independence "Third Italian War of Independence"), which resulted in Italy annexing [Venetia](/wiki/Veneto "Veneto"). Finally, in 1870, as France abandoned Rome during the [Franco-Prussian War](/wiki/Franco-Prussian_War "Franco-Prussian War"), the Italians [captured the Papal States](/wiki/Capture_of_Rome "Capture of Rome"), unification was completed, and the capital moved to Rome.[[68]](#cite_note-scholar_and_patriot-72)

### Liberal period

Main articles: [Kingdom of Italy](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Italy "Kingdom of Italy"), [Italian diaspora](/wiki/Italian_diaspora "Italian diaspora"), [Italian Empire](/wiki/Italian_Empire "Italian Empire"), and [Military history of Italy during World War I](/wiki/Military_history_of_Italy_during_World_War_I "Military history of Italy during World War I")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dc/VictorEmmanuel2.jpg/250px-VictorEmmanuel2.jpg)](/wiki/File:VictorEmmanuel2.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c2/Camillo_Benso_Cavour_di_Ciseri.jpg/250px-Camillo_Benso_Cavour_di_Ciseri.jpg)](/wiki/File:Camillo_Benso_Cavour_di_Ciseri.jpg)

[Victor Emmanuel II](/wiki/Victor_Emmanuel_II "Victor Emmanuel II") (left) and [Camillo Benso, Count of Cavour](/wiki/Camillo_Benso,_Count_of_Cavour "Camillo Benso, Count of Cavour") (right), leading figures in unification, became respectively the first King and prime minister of unified Italy.

Sardinia's constitution was extended to all of Italy in 1861, and provided basic freedoms for the new state; but electoral laws excluded the non-propertied classes. The new kingdom was governed by a parliamentary constitutional monarchy dominated by liberals. As northern Italy quickly industrialised, southern and northern rural areas remained underdeveloped and overpopulated, forcing millions to migrate and fuelling [a large and influential diaspora](/wiki/Italian_diaspora "Italian diaspora"). The [Italian Socialist Party](/wiki/Italian_Socialist_Party "Italian Socialist Party") increased in strength, challenging the traditional liberal and conservative establishment. In the last two decades of the 19th century, Italy developed into [a colonial power](/wiki/Italian_Empire "Italian Empire")[[75]](#cite_note-79) by subjugating [Eritrea](/wiki/Italian_Eritrea "Italian Eritrea"), [Somalia](/wiki/Italian_Somalia "Italian Somalia"), [Tripolitania](/wiki/Italian_Tripolitania "Italian Tripolitania"), and [Cyrenaica](/wiki/Italian_Cyrenaica "Italian Cyrenaica") in Africa.[[76]](#cite_note-80) In 1913, male universal suffrage was adopted. The pre-[World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I") period was dominated by [Giovanni Giolitti](/wiki/Giovanni_Giolitti "Giovanni Giolitti"), prime minister five times between 1892 and 1921.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f2/Trento_3_novembre_1918.jpg/250px-Trento_3_novembre_1918.jpg)](/wiki/File:Trento_3_novembre_1918.jpg)

Italian cavalry in [Trento](/wiki/Trento "Trento") on 3 November 1918, after the victorious [Battle of Vittorio Veneto](/wiki/Battle_of_Vittorio_Veneto "Battle of Vittorio Veneto")

[Italy entered into the First World War](/wiki/Italian_entry_into_World_War_I "Italian entry into World War I") in 1915 with the aim of completing national unity, so it is also considered the Fourth Italian War of Independence,[[77]](#cite_note-81) from a historiographical perspective, as the conclusion of the [unification of Italy](/wiki/Unification_of_Italy "Unification of Italy").[[78]](#cite_note-82) Italy, nominally allied with the [German](/wiki/German_Empire "German Empire") and [Austro-Hungarian](/wiki/Austro-Hungarian "Austro-Hungarian") empires in the [Triple Alliance](/wiki/Triple_Alliance_(1882) "Triple Alliance (1882)"), in 1915 joined the [Allies](/wiki/Allies_of_World_War_I "Allies of World War I"), entering World War I with a [promise](/wiki/Treaty_of_London_(1915) "Treaty of London (1915)") of substantial territorial gains that included west [Inner Carniola](/wiki/Inner_Carniola "Inner Carniola"), the former [Austrian Littoral](/wiki/Austrian_Littoral "Austrian Littoral"), and [Dalmatia](/wiki/Dalmatia "Dalmatia"), as well as parts of the [Ottoman Empire](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire"). The country's contribution to the Allied victory earned it a place as one of the "[Big Four](/wiki/The_Big_Four_(World_War_I) "The Big Four (World War I)")" powers. Reorganisation of the army and conscription led to Italian victories. In October 1918, the Italians launched a massive offensive, culminating in victory at the [Battle of Vittorio Veneto](/wiki/Battle_of_Vittorio_Veneto "Battle of Vittorio Veneto").[[79]](#cite_note-83) This marked the end of war on the Italian Front, secured dissolution of the Austro-Hungarian Empire, and was instrumental in [ending](/wiki/Armistice_with_Germany "Armistice with Germany") the war less than two weeks later.

During the war, more than 650,000 Italian soldiers and as many civilians died,[[80]](#cite_note-84) and the kingdom was on the brink of bankruptcy. The [Treaty of Saint-Germain-en-Laye](/wiki/Treaty_of_Saint-Germain-en-Laye_(1919) "Treaty of Saint-Germain-en-Laye (1919)") (1919) and [Treaty of Rapallo](/wiki/Treaty_of_Rapallo_(1920) "Treaty of Rapallo (1920)") (1920) allowed for annexation of [Trentino](/wiki/Trentino "Trentino") and [South Tyrol](/wiki/South_Tyrol "South Tyrol"), the [Julian March](/wiki/Julian_March "Julian March"), [Istria](/wiki/Istria "Istria"), the [Kvarner Gulf](/wiki/Kvarner_Gulf "Kvarner Gulf"), and the Dalmatian city of [Zara](/wiki/Zadar "Zadar"). The subsequent [Treaty of Rome](/wiki/Treaty_of_Rome_(1924) "Treaty of Rome (1924)") (1924) led to annexation of [Fiume](/wiki/Fiume "Fiume") by Italy. Italy did not receive other territories promised by the Treaty of London, so this outcome was denounced as a "[mutilated victory](/wiki/Mutilated_victory "Mutilated victory")", by [Benito Mussolini](/wiki/Benito_Mussolini "Benito Mussolini"), which helped lead to the [rise of Italian fascism](/wiki/Fascist_Italy_(1922%E2%80%931943) "Fascist Italy (1922–1943)"). Historians regard "mutilated victory" as a "political myth", used by fascists to fuel [Italian imperialism](/wiki/Italian_imperialism "Italian imperialism").[[81]](#cite_note-85) Italy gained a permanent seat in the [League of Nations](/wiki/League_of_Nations "League of Nations")'s executive council.

### Fascist regime and World War II

Main articles: [Fascist Italy](/wiki/Fascist_Italy "Fascist Italy"), [Military history of Italy during World War II](/wiki/Military_history_of_Italy_during_World_War_II "Military history of Italy during World War II"), [Italian campaign (World War II)](/wiki/Italian_campaign_(World_War_II) "Italian campaign (World War II)"), and [The Holocaust in Italy](/wiki/The_Holocaust_in_Italy "The Holocaust in Italy")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8a/Mussolini_mezzobusto.jpg/250px-Mussolini_mezzobusto.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mussolini_mezzobusto.jpg)

The fascist dictator [Benito Mussolini](/wiki/Benito_Mussolini "Benito Mussolini") titled himself *[Duce](/wiki/Duce "Duce")* and ruled the country from 1922 until [his overthrow](/wiki/Fall_of_the_Fascist_regime_in_Italy "Fall of the Fascist regime in Italy") in 1943.

The [socialist agitations](/wiki/Biennio_Rosso "Biennio Rosso") that followed the devastation of the Great War, inspired by the [Russian Revolution](/wiki/Russian_Revolution "Russian Revolution"), led to counter-revolution and repression throughout Italy. The liberal establishment, fearing a Soviet-style revolution, started to endorse the small [National Fascist Party](/wiki/National_Fascist_Party "National Fascist Party"), led by Mussolini. In October 1922, the [Blackshirts](/wiki/Blackshirts "Blackshirts") of the National Fascist Party organised a [mass demonstration](/wiki/Mass_demonstration "Mass demonstration") and the "[March on Rome](/wiki/March_on_Rome "March on Rome")" coup. King [Victor Emmanuel III](/wiki/Victor_Emmanuel_III "Victor Emmanuel III") appointed Mussolini as [prime minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Italy "Prime Minister of Italy"), transferring power to the fascists without armed conflict.[[82]](#cite_note-86)
Mussolini banned political parties and curtailed personal liberties, establishing a dictatorship. These actions attracted international attention and inspired similar dictatorships in [Nazi Germany](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany") and [Imperial Japan](/wiki/Imperial_Japan "Imperial Japan").

[Fascism](/wiki/Fascism "Fascism") was based upon Italian nationalism and imperialism, seeking to expand Italian possessions via [irredentist claims](/wiki/Irredentism "Irredentism") based on the legacy of the Roman and [Venetian empires](/wiki/Venetian_empire "Venetian empire").[[83]](#cite_note-87)
For this reason the fascists engaged in [interventionist foreign policy](/wiki/Interventionist_foreign_policy "Interventionist foreign policy"). In 1935, Mussolini [invaded Ethiopia](/wiki/Second_Italo-Abyssinian_War "Second Italo-Abyssinian War") and founded [Italian East Africa](/wiki/Italian_East_Africa "Italian East Africa"), resulting in international isolation. Italy withdrew from the [League of Nations](/wiki/League_of_Nations "League of Nations"). Italy then [allied with Nazi Germany](/wiki/Pact_of_Steel "Pact of Steel") and the [Empire of Japan](/wiki/Tripartite_Pact "Tripartite Pact"), and strongly supported [Francisco Franco](/wiki/Francisco_Franco "Francisco Franco") in the [Spanish Civil War](/wiki/Spanish_Civil_War "Spanish Civil War"). In April 1939, Italy [invaded Albania](/wiki/Italian_invasion_of_Albania "Italian invasion of Albania").

Italy entered World War II on 10 June 1940. At this date, France practically had lost the [Battle of France](/wiki/Battle_of_France "Battle of France").
At different times, Italians advanced in [British Somaliland](/wiki/Italian_conquest_of_British_Somaliland "Italian conquest of British Somaliland"), [Egypt](/wiki/Italian_invasion_of_Egypt "Italian invasion of Egypt"), the [Balkans](/wiki/Balkan_Campaign_(World_War_II) "Balkan Campaign (World War II)"), and eastern fronts. They were, however, [defeated on the Eastern Front](/wiki/Italian_participation_on_the_Eastern_Front "Italian participation on the Eastern Front") as well as in the [East African](/wiki/East_African_campaign_(World_War_II) "East African campaign (World War II)") and [North African](/wiki/North_African_campaign "North African campaign") campaigns, losing their territories in Africa and the Balkans. [Italian war crimes](/wiki/Italian_war_crimes "Italian war crimes") included [extrajudicial killings](/wiki/Extrajudicial_killing "Extrajudicial killing") and [ethnic cleansing](/wiki/Ethnic_cleansing "Ethnic cleansing")[[84]](#cite_note-88) by deportation of about 25,000 people – mainly Yugoslavs – to [Italian concentration camps](/wiki/Italian_concentration_camps "Italian concentration camps") and elsewhere. [Yugoslav Partisans](/wiki/Yugoslav_Partisans "Yugoslav Partisans") perpetrated their own crimes against the ethnic Italian population during and after the war, including the [foibe massacres](/wiki/Foibe_massacres "Foibe massacres"). An [Allied invasion of Sicily](/wiki/Allied_invasion_of_Sicily "Allied invasion of Sicily") began in July 1943, leading to the [collapse of the Fascist regime](/wiki/Fall_of_the_Fascist_regime_in_Italy "Fall of the Fascist regime in Italy") on 25 July. Mussolini was deposed and arrested by order of King [Victor Emmanuel III](/wiki/Victor_Emmanuel_III "Victor Emmanuel III"). On 3 September, Italy signed the [Armistice of Cassibile](/wiki/Armistice_of_Cassibile "Armistice of Cassibile"), ending its war with UK and USA. The Germans, with the assistance of Italian fascists, succeeded in taking control of north and central Italy ([Operation Achse](/wiki/Operation_Achse "Operation Achse")). The country remained a battlefield, with the Allies moving up from the south.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a6/01_partigiani_a_milano1.jpg/250px-01_partigiani_a_milano1.jpg)](/wiki/File:01_partigiani_a_milano1.jpg)

[Italian partisans](/wiki/Italian_resistance_movement "Italian resistance movement") in Milan during the final insurrection leading to the [liberation of Italy](/wiki/Liberation_of_Italy "Liberation of Italy") in April 1945

In the north, the Germans set up the [Italian Social Republic](/wiki/Italian_Social_Republic "Italian Social Republic") (RSI), a Nazi [puppet state](/wiki/Puppet_state "Puppet state") and [collaborationist](/wiki/Collaborationist "Collaborationist") regime with Mussolini installed as leader after he was [rescued](/wiki/Gran_Sasso_raid "Gran Sasso raid") by German paratroopers. What remained of the Italian troops was organised into the [Italian Co-belligerent Army](/wiki/Italian_Co-belligerent_Army "Italian Co-belligerent Army"), which fought alongside the Allies, while other Italian forces, loyal to Mussolini, opted to fight alongside the Germans in the [National Republican Army](/wiki/National_Republican_Army "National Republican Army"). German troops, with RSI collaboration, committed massacres and deported thousands of Jews to death camps. The post-armistice period saw the emergence of the [Italian Resistance](/wiki/Italian_Resistance "Italian Resistance"), who fought a guerrilla war against the [Nazi German occupiers](/wiki/Operation_Achse "Operation Achse") and collaborators.[[85]](#cite_note-89)
An aspect of this period was the [Italian civil war](/wiki/Italian_civil_war "Italian civil war") due to fighting between partisans and fascist RSI forces.[[86]](#cite_note-90)[[87]](#cite_note-91) In April 1945, with defeat looming, Mussolini attempted to escape north,[[88]](#cite_note-92) but was captured and [summarily executed](/wiki/Death_of_Benito_Mussolini "Death of Benito Mussolini") by partisans.[[89]](#cite_note-93)

Hostilities ended on 29 April 1945, [when the German forces in Italy surrendered](/wiki/Surrender_of_Caserta "Surrender of Caserta"). Nearly half a million Italians died in the war,[[90]](#cite_note-94) society was divided, and the economy all but destroyed – per capita income in 1944 was at its lowest point since 1900.[[91]](#cite_note-95)
In the aftermath of the war there was a revival of Italian republicanism, leading to the [1946 Italian institutional referendum](/wiki/1946_Italian_institutional_referendum "1946 Italian institutional referendum").[[92]](#cite_note-96)

### Republican era

Main article: [History of the Italian Republic](/wiki/History_of_the_Italian_Republic "History of the Italian Republic")

Italy became a republic after the 1946 referendum[[93]](#cite_note-97) held on 2 June, a day celebrated since as *[Festa della Repubblica](/wiki/Festa_della_Repubblica "Festa della Repubblica")*. This was the first time women voted nationally.[[94]](#cite_note-98) Victor Emmanuel III's son, [Umberto II](/wiki/Umberto_II "Umberto II"), was forced to abdicate. The [Republican Constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_Italy "Constitution of Italy") was approved in 1948. Under the [Treaty of Paris between Italy and the Allied Powers](/wiki/Treaty_of_Paris_between_Italy_and_the_Allied_Powers "Treaty of Paris between Italy and the Allied Powers"), areas next to the [Adriatic Sea](/wiki/Adriatic_Sea "Adriatic Sea") were annexed by [Yugoslavia](/wiki/Socialist_Federal_Republic_of_Yugoslavia "Socialist Federal Republic of Yugoslavia"), resulting in the [Istrian-Dalmatian exodus](/wiki/Istrian-Dalmatian_exodus "Istrian-Dalmatian exodus"), which involved the emigration of around 300,000 [Istrian](/wiki/Istrian_Italians "Istrian Italians") and [Dalmatian Italians](/wiki/Dalmatian_Italians "Dalmatian Italians").[[95]](#cite_note-99) Italy lost all colonial possessions, ending the [Italian Empire](/wiki/Italian_Empire "Italian Empire").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b7/Alcide_de_Gasperi_2.jpg/250px-Alcide_de_Gasperi_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Alcide_de_Gasperi_2.jpg)

[Alcide De Gasperi](/wiki/Alcide_De_Gasperi "Alcide De Gasperi"), [first](/wiki/List_of_Prime_Ministers_of_Italy "List of Prime Ministers of Italy") republican [prime minister of Italy](/wiki/Prime_minister_of_Italy "Prime minister of Italy") and one of the [Founding fathers of the European Union](/wiki/Founding_fathers_of_the_European_Union "Founding fathers of the European Union")

Fears of a Communist takeover proved crucial in [1948](/wiki/Italian_general_election,_1948 "Italian general election, 1948"), when the [Christian Democrats](/wiki/Christian_Democracy_(Italy) "Christian Democracy (Italy)"), under [Alcide De Gasperi](/wiki/Alcide_De_Gasperi "Alcide De Gasperi"), won a landslide victory.[[96]](#cite_note-100) Consequently, in 1949 Italy became a member of [NATO](/wiki/NATO "NATO"). The [Marshall Plan](/wiki/Marshall_Plan "Marshall Plan") revived the economy, which, until the late 1960s, enjoyed a period called the [Italian economic miracle](/wiki/Italian_economic_miracle "Italian economic miracle"). In the 1950s, Italy became a founding country of the [European Communities](/wiki/European_Communities "European Communities"), a forerunner of the European Union. From the late 1960s until the early 1980s, the country experienced the *[Years of lead](/wiki/Years_of_Lead_(Italy) "Years of Lead (Italy)")*, characterised by economic difficulties, especially after the [1973 oil crisis](/wiki/1973_oil_crisis "1973 oil crisis"); social conflicts; and terrorist massacres.[[97]](#cite_note-101)

The economy recovered and Italy became the world's fifth-largest industrial nation after it gained entry into the [G7](/wiki/G7 "G7") in the 1970s. However, national debt skyrocketed past 100% of GDP. Between 1992 and 1993, Italy faced terror attacks perpetrated by the [Sicilian Mafia](/wiki/Sicilian_Mafia "Sicilian Mafia") as a consequence of new anti-mafia measures by the government.[[98]](#cite_note-102) Voters – disenchanted with political paralysis, massive public debt, and extensive corruption uncovered by the [Clean Hands](/wiki/Mani_pulite "Mani pulite") investigation – demanded radical reform. The Christian Democrats, who had ruled for almost 50 years, underwent a crisis and disbanded, splitting into factions.[[99]](#cite_note-103) The Communists reorganised as a [social-democratic](/wiki/Social-democratic "Social-democratic") force. During the 1990s and 2000s, [centre-right](/wiki/Centre-right_coalition_(Italy) "Centre-right coalition (Italy)") (dominated by media magnate [Silvio Berlusconi](/wiki/Silvio_Berlusconi "Silvio Berlusconi")) and [centre-left](/wiki/Centre-left_coalition_(Italy) "Centre-left coalition (Italy)") coalitions (led by professor [Romano Prodi](/wiki/Romano_Prodi "Romano Prodi")) alternately governed.

In the early 21st century, Italy experienced a prolonged period of political and economic instability, strongly influenced by international crises. The effects of the [Great Recession](/wiki/Great_Recession "Great Recession") that began in 2008 had a significant impact on the country's economy and public finances, leading to the adoption of [austerity measures](/wiki/Austerity_measures "Austerity measures") and an increased reliance on [technocratic](/wiki/Technocratic "Technocratic") or broad coalition governments aimed at ensuring stability and international credibility.[[100]](#cite_note-104)

During the 2010s, efforts were made by new prime minister [Matteo Renzi](/wiki/Matteo_Renzi "Matteo Renzi") to introduce institutional reforms intended to streamline the political system and strengthen the executive branch, though these initiatives were rejected by [a referendum](/wiki/Italian_constitutional_referendum,_2016 "Italian constitutional referendum, 2016") in 2016.[[101]](#cite_note-105) At the same time, the [European migrant crisis](/wiki/European_migrant_crisis "European migrant crisis") placed Italy in a central role as a major point of entry into the European Union via the Mediterranean,[[102]](#cite_note-106) resulting in a large inflow of migrants and asylum seekers, particularly from sub-Saharan Africa.[[103]](#cite_note-107) This development had notable social and political consequences, contributing to growing public debate over immigration and leading to a surge in support for [populist](/wiki/Populist "Populist") parties.[[104]](#cite_note-108)[[105]](#cite_note-109)

The [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_Italy "COVID-19 pandemic in Italy"), which began in 2020, severely affected the country's public health and economic performance, exacerbating pre-existing structural weaknesses.[[106]](#cite_note-110) In response, [extraordinary measures were adopted](/wiki/COVID-19_lockdowns_in_Italy "COVID-19 lockdowns in Italy") to support the economy, many of them coordinated at the European level.[[107]](#cite_note-111)[[108]](#cite_note-112) In 2022, [Giorgia Meloni](/wiki/Giorgia_Meloni "Giorgia Meloni") was sworn in as Italy's first female prime minister.[[109]](#cite_note-113)

## Geography

Main article: [Geography of Italy](/wiki/Geography_of_Italy "Geography of Italy")

Further information: [Geology of Italy](/wiki/Geology_of_Italy "Geology of Italy"), [Volcanism of Italy](/wiki/Volcanism_of_Italy "Volcanism of Italy"), [List of rivers of Italy](/wiki/List_of_rivers_of_Italy "List of rivers of Italy"), [List of lakes of Italy](/wiki/List_of_lakes_of_Italy "List of lakes of Italy"), [List of islands of Italy](/wiki/List_of_islands_of_Italy "List of islands of Italy"), and [Italy (geographical region)](/wiki/Italy_(geographical_region) "Italy (geographical region)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/79/Italy_relief_location_map.jpg/250px-Italy_relief_location_map.jpg)](/wiki/File:Italy_relief_location_map.jpg)

Topographic map of Italy

Italy, whose territory largely coincides with the [eponymous geographical region](/wiki/Italy_(geographical_region) "Italy (geographical region)"),[[110]](#cite_note-Treccani-114) is located in Southern Europe[[111]](#cite_note-115) (and is also considered part of Western Europe[[c]](#cite_note-WE-15)) between latitudes [35°](/wiki/35th_parallel_north "35th parallel north") and [47° N](/wiki/47%C2%B0_N "47° N"), and longitudes [6°](/wiki/6th_meridian_east "6th meridian east") and [19° E](/wiki/19th_meridian_east "19th meridian east"). To the north, from west to east, Italy borders France, Switzerland, Austria, and Slovenia, and is roughly delimited by the [Alpine watershed](/wiki/Alps "Alps"), enclosing the [Po Valley](/wiki/Po_Valley "Po Valley") and the [Venetian Plain](/wiki/Venetian_Plain "Venetian Plain"). It consists of the entirety of the [Italian Peninsula](/wiki/Italian_Peninsula "Italian Peninsula"), Sicily and Sardinia (the [biggest islands of the Mediterranean](/wiki/Mediterranean_islands#By_area "Mediterranean islands")), and [many smaller islands](/wiki/List_of_islands_of_Italy "List of islands of Italy").[[110]](#cite_note-Treccani-114) Some of Italy's territory extends beyond the Alpine basin, and some islands are located outside the [Eurasian](/wiki/Eurasia "Eurasia") continental shelf.

The country's area is 301,230 square kilometres (116,306 sq mi), of which 294,020 km2 (113,522 sq mi) is land and 7,210 km2 (2,784 sq mi) is water.[[112]](#cite_note-Area-116) Including the islands, Italy has a coastline of 7,600 kilometres (4,722 miles) on the [Mediterranean Sea](/wiki/Mediterranean_Sea "Mediterranean Sea"), the [Ligurian](/wiki/Ligurian_Sea "Ligurian Sea") and [Tyrrhenian](/wiki/Tyrrhenian_Sea "Tyrrhenian Sea") seas,[[113]](#cite_note-117) the [Ionian Sea](/wiki/Ionian_Sea "Ionian Sea"),[[114]](#cite_note-118) and the [Adriatic Sea](/wiki/Adriatic_Sea "Adriatic Sea").[[115]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECushman-RoisinGačićPoulain20011–2-119) Its border with France runs for 488 km (303 mi); Switzerland, 740 km (460 mi); Austria, 430 km (267 mi); and Slovenia, 232 km (144 mi). The sovereign states of [San Marino](/wiki/San_Marino "San Marino") and [Vatican City](/wiki/Vatican_City "Vatican City") (the smallest country in the world and headquarters of the worldwide [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") under the governance of the [Holy See](/wiki/Holy_See "Holy See")) are [enclaves](/wiki/Enclaves "Enclaves") within Italy,[[116]](#cite_note-120) while [Campione d'Italia](/wiki/Campione_d%27Italia "Campione d'Italia") is an Italian [exclave](/wiki/Exclave "Exclave") in Switzerland.[[117]](#cite_note-121) The border with San Marino is 39 km (24 mi) long; that with Vatican City is 3.2 km (2.0 mi).[[112]](#cite_note-Area-116)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ee/Monte_Bianco_DSF1243-m.jpg/250px-Monte_Bianco_DSF1243-m.jpg)](/wiki/File:Monte_Bianco_DSF1243-m.jpg)

[Mont Blanc](/wiki/Mont_Blanc "Mont Blanc") (*Monte Bianco*) in [Aosta Valley](/wiki/Aosta_Valley "Aosta Valley"), the highest point in the European Union

Over 35% of Italian territory is mountainous.[[118]](#cite_note-eug92-122) The [Apennine Mountains](/wiki/Apennine_Mountains "Apennine Mountains") form the peninsula's backbone, and the Alps form most of its northern boundary, where Italy's highest point is located on the summit of [Mont Blanc](/wiki/Mont_Blanc "Mont Blanc") (*Monte Bianco*) at 4,810 m (15,780 ft). Other well-known mountains include the [Matterhorn](/wiki/Matterhorn "Matterhorn") (*Monte Cervino*) in the western Alps, and the [Dolomites](/wiki/Dolomites "Dolomites") in the eastern Alps. Many parts of [Italy are of volcanic origin](/wiki/Volcanism_of_Italy "Volcanism of Italy"). Most small islands and archipelagos in the south are [volcanic islands](/wiki/Volcanic_island "Volcanic island"). There are active volcanoes: [Mount Etna](/wiki/Mount_Etna "Mount Etna") in Sicily (the largest in Europe), [Vulcano](/wiki/Vulcano "Vulcano"), [Stromboli](/wiki/Stromboli "Stromboli"), and [Vesuvius](/wiki/Vesuvius "Vesuvius").

Most [rivers of Italy](/wiki/Rivers_of_Italy "Rivers of Italy") drain into the Adriatic or Tyrrhenian Sea.[[119]](#cite_note-123) The longest is the [Po](/wiki/Po_(river) "Po (river)"), which flows from the Alps on the western border, and crosses the [Padan plain](/wiki/Padan_plain "Padan plain") to the Adriatic.[[120]](#cite_note-124) The Po Valley is the largest plain, with 46,000 km2 (18,000 sq mi), and contains over 70% of the country's [lowlands](/wiki/Upland_and_lowland "Upland and lowland").[[118]](#cite_note-eug92-122) The largest lakes are, in descending size: [Garda](/wiki/Lake_Garda "Lake Garda") (367.94 km2 or 142 sq mi), [Maggiore](/wiki/Lake_Maggiore "Lake Maggiore") (212.51 km2 or 82 sq mi), and [Como](/wiki/Lake_Como "Lake Como") (145.9 km2 or 56 sq mi).[[121]](#cite_note-125)

### Climate

Main article: [Climate of Italy](/wiki/Climate_of_Italy "Climate of Italy")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8e/Italy_K%C3%B6ppen.svg/250px-Italy_K%C3%B6ppen.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Italy_K%C3%B6ppen.svg)

[Köppen-Geiger climate classification](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification") map of Italy[[122]](#cite_note-126)

Italy's climate is influenced by the seas that surround the country on every side except the north, which constitute a reservoir of heat and humidity. Within the southern temperate zone, they determine a Mediterranean climate with local differences.[[123]](#cite_note-127) Because of the length of the peninsula and the mostly mountainous hinterland, the climate is highly diverse. In most inland northern and central regions, the climate ranges from [humid subtropical](/wiki/Humid_subtropical "Humid subtropical") to [humid continental](/wiki/Humid_continental "Humid continental") and [oceanic](/wiki/Oceanic_climate "Oceanic climate"). The Po Valley is mostly humid subtropical, with cool winters and hot summers.[[124]](#cite_note-128) The coastal areas of [Liguria](/wiki/Liguria "Liguria"), Tuscany, and most of the south generally fit the Mediterranean climate stereotype, as in the [Köppen climate classification](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification").

Conditions on the coast are different from those in the interior, particularly during winter when the higher altitudes tend to be cold, wet, and often snowy. The coastal regions have mild winters, and hot and generally dry summers; lowland valleys are hot in summer. Winter temperatures vary from 0 °C (32 °F) in the Alps to 12 °C (54 °F) in Sicily; so, average summer temperatures range from 20 °C (68 °F) to over 25 °C (77 °F). Winters can vary widely with lingering cold, foggy, and snowy periods in the north, and milder, sunnier conditions in the south. Summers are hot across the country, except at high altitude, particularly in the south. Northern and central areas can experience strong thunderstorms from spring to autumn.[[125]](#cite_note-129)

### Biodiversity

Main articles: [Fauna of Italy](/wiki/Fauna_of_Italy "Fauna of Italy") and [Flora of Italy](/wiki/Flora_of_Italy "Flora of Italy")

Further information: [Italian garden](/wiki/Italian_garden "Italian garden")

Italy's varied geography, including the [Alps](/wiki/Alps "Alps"), [Apennines](/wiki/Apennines "Apennines"), central Italian woodlands, and southern Italian [Garigue](/wiki/Garigue "Garigue") and [Maquis shrubland](/wiki/Maquis_shrubland "Maquis shrubland"), contribute to habitat diversity. As the peninsula is in the centre of the Mediterranean, forming a corridor between Central Europe and North Africa, and having 8,000 km (5,000 mi) of coastline, Italy has received species from the [Balkans](/wiki/Balkans "Balkans"), Eurasia, and the Middle East. Italy has probably the highest level of [faunal](/wiki/Fauna "Fauna") [biodiversity](/wiki/Biodiversity "Biodiversity") in Europe, with over 57,000 species recorded, representing more than a third of all European fauna,[[126]](#cite_note-130) and the highest level of biodiversity of animal and plant species within the EU.[[127]](#cite_note-131)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a0/C._l._italicus_in_MNP.jpg/250px-C._l._italicus_in_MNP.jpg)](/wiki/File:C._l._italicus_in_MNP.jpg)

The [Italian wolf](/wiki/Italian_wolf "Italian wolf"), the national animal of Italy

The fauna of Italy includes 4,777 [endemic](/wiki/Endemic "Endemic") animal species,[[128]](#cite_note-132) which include the [Sardinian long-eared bat](/wiki/Sardinian_long-eared_bat "Sardinian long-eared bat"), [Sardinian red deer](/wiki/Corsican_red_deer "Corsican red deer"), [spectacled salamander](/wiki/Spectacled_salamander "Spectacled salamander"), [brown cave salamander](/wiki/Brown_cave_salamander "Brown cave salamander"), [Italian newt](/wiki/Italian_newt "Italian newt"), [Italian frog](/wiki/Italian_stream_frog "Italian stream frog"), [Apennine yellow-bellied toad](/wiki/Apennine_yellow-bellied_toad "Apennine yellow-bellied toad"), [Italian wall lizard](/wiki/Italian_wall_lizard "Italian wall lizard"), and [Sicilian pond turtle](/wiki/Sicilian_pond_turtle "Sicilian pond turtle"). There are 119 [mammals species](/wiki/List_of_mammals_of_Italy "List of mammals of Italy"),[[129]](#cite_note-133) 550 [bird species](/wiki/List_of_birds_of_Italy "List of birds of Italy"),[[130]](#cite_note-134) 69 [reptile species](/wiki/List_of_reptiles_of_Italy "List of reptiles of Italy"),[[131]](#cite_note-135) 39 [amphibian species](/wiki/List_of_amphibians_of_Italy "List of amphibians of Italy"),[[132]](#cite_note-136) 623 fish species,[[133]](#cite_note-137) and 56,213 invertebrate species, of which 37,303 are insect species.[[134]](#cite_note-138)

The flora of Italy was traditionally estimated to comprise about 5,500 [vascular plant](/wiki/Vascular_plant "Vascular plant") species.[[135]](#cite_note-139) However, as of 2005[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Italy&action=edit), 6,759 species are recorded in the *Data bank of Italian vascular flora*.[[136]](#cite_note-140) Italy has 1,371 endemic plant species and subspecies,[[137]](#cite_note-141) which include [Sicilian fir](/wiki/Sicilian_fir "Sicilian fir"), [Barbaricina columbine](/wiki/Barbaricina_columbine "Barbaricina columbine"), [Sea marigold](/wiki/Sea_marigold "Sea marigold"), [Lavender cotton](/wiki/Santolina_pinnata "Santolina pinnata"), and [Ucriana violet](/wiki/Viola_ucriana "Viola ucriana"). Italy is a signatory to the [Berne Convention on the Conservation of European Wildlife and Natural Habitats](/wiki/Berne_Convention_on_the_Conservation_of_European_Wildlife_and_Natural_Habitats "Berne Convention on the Conservation of European Wildlife and Natural Habitats") and the [Habitats Directive](/wiki/Habitats_Directive "Habitats Directive").

Italy has many botanical and historic gardens.[[138]](#cite_note-142) The Italian garden is stylistically based on symmetry, axial geometry, and the principle of imposing order on nature. It influenced the [history of gardening](/wiki/History_of_gardening "History of gardening"), especially [French](/wiki/French_garden "French garden") and [English](/wiki/English_garden "English garden") gardens.[[139]](#cite_note-143) The Italian garden was influenced by [Roman](/wiki/Roman_garden "Roman garden") and [Italian Renaissance](/wiki/Italian_Renaissance_garden "Italian Renaissance garden") gardens.

The [Italian wolf](/wiki/Italian_wolf "Italian wolf") is the national animal of Italy,[[140]](#cite_note-144) while the national tree is the [strawberry tree](/wiki/Arbutus_unedo "Arbutus unedo").[[141]](#cite_note-altovastese-145) The reasons for this are that the Italian wolf, which inhabits the [Apennine Mountains](/wiki/Apennine_Mountains "Apennine Mountains") and the Western Alps, features prominently in Latin and Italian cultures, such as the legend of the [founding of Rome](/wiki/Founding_of_Rome "Founding of Rome"),[[142]](#cite_note-146) while the green leaves, white flowers, and red berries of the strawberry tree, native to the Mediterranean, recall the colours of the flag.[[141]](#cite_note-altovastese-145) The national bird is the [Italian sparrow](/wiki/Italian_sparrow "Italian sparrow"),[[143]](#cite_note-147) while the national flower is the flower of the strawberry tree.[[144]](#cite_note-148)

### Environment

See also: [List of national parks of Italy](/wiki/List_of_national_parks_of_Italy "List of national parks of Italy"), [List of regional parks of Italy](/wiki/List_of_regional_parks_of_Italy "List of regional parks of Italy"), and [List of Marine Protected Areas of Italy](/wiki/List_of_Marine_Protected_Areas_of_Italy "List of Marine Protected Areas of Italy")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3b/National_and_Regional_Parks_of_Italy.svg/250px-National_and_Regional_Parks_of_Italy.svg.png)](/wiki/File:National_and_Regional_Parks_of_Italy.svg)

National and regional parks in Italy

After its quick industrial growth, Italy took time to address its environmental problems. After improvements, Italy now ranks 84th in the world for ecological sustainability.[[145]](#cite_note-149) The total area protected by national parks, regional parks, and nature reserves covers about 11% of Italian territory,[[146]](#cite_note-150) and 12% of Italy's coastline is protected.[[147]](#cite_note-151)

Italy has been one of the world's leading producers of [renewable energy](/wiki/Renewable_energy_in_Italy "Renewable energy in Italy"), in 2010 ranking as the fourth largest provider of installed [solar energy](/wiki/Solar_energy "Solar energy") capacity[[148]](#cite_note-152) and sixth largest of [wind power](/wiki/Wind_power "Wind power") capacity.[[149]](#cite_note-153) Renewable energy provided approximately 37% Italy's energy consumption in 2020.[[150]](#cite_note-154)

The country operated nuclear reactors between 1963 and 1990 but, after the [Chernobyl disaster](/wiki/Chernobyl_disaster "Chernobyl disaster") and [referendums](/wiki/1987_Italian_referendums "1987 Italian referendums"), the nuclear programme was terminated, a decision overturned by the government in 2008, with plans to build up to four nuclear power plants. This was in turn struck down by a referendum following the [Fukushima nuclear accident](/wiki/Fukushima_nuclear_accident "Fukushima nuclear accident").[[151]](#cite_note-155)

Air pollution remains severe, especially in the industrialised north. Italy is the [twelfth-largest carbon dioxide producer](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_carbon_dioxide_emissions "List of countries by carbon dioxide emissions").[[152]](#cite_note-156) Extensive traffic and congestion in large cities continue to cause environmental and health issues, even if smog levels have decreased since the 1970s and 1980s, with smog becoming an increasingly rare phenomenon and levels of [sulphur dioxide](/wiki/Sulfur_dioxide "Sulfur dioxide") decreasing.[[153]](#cite_note-157)

Deforestation, illegal building, and poor land-management policies have led to significant erosion in Italy's mountainous regions, leading to ecological disasters such as the 1963 [Vajont Dam](/wiki/Vajont_Dam "Vajont Dam") flood, the 1998 [Sarno](/wiki/Sarno "Sarno"),[[154]](#cite_note-158) and the 2009 [Messina mudslides](/wiki/2009_Messina_floods_and_mudslides "2009 Messina floods and mudslides").

## Politics

Main article: [Politics of Italy](/wiki/Politics_of_Italy "Politics of Italy")

Italy has been a unitary [parliamentary republic](/wiki/Parliamentary_republic "Parliamentary republic") since 1946, when the monarchy [was abolished](/wiki/1946_Italian_institutional_referendum "1946 Italian institutional referendum"). The [president of Italy](/wiki/President_of_Italy "President of Italy"), [Sergio Mattarella](/wiki/Sergio_Mattarella "Sergio Mattarella") since 2015, is Italy's head of state. The president is elected for a single seven-year term by the [Italian Parliament](/wiki/Italian_Parliament "Italian Parliament") and regional voters in joint session. Italy has [a written democratic constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_Italy "Constitution of Italy") that resulted from a [Constituent Assembly](/wiki/Constituent_Assembly_of_Italy "Constituent Assembly of Italy") formed by representatives of the [anti-fascist](/wiki/Anti-fascist "Anti-fascist") forces that contributed to the defeat of Nazi and Fascist forces during the liberation of Italy, in World War II.[[155]](#cite_note-159)

Italy plays a major role in several economic, military, cultural, and political affairs, and is one of the [EU big three](/wiki/EU_big_three "EU big three"). It is widely considered to be a [regional power](/wiki/Regional_power "Regional power"),[[156]](#cite_note-160) while its [great power](/wiki/Great_power "Great power") status[[157]](#cite_note-161) is [a subject of debate among scholars and political analysts](/wiki/Least_of_the_great_powers "Least of the great powers").

According to [International IDEA](/wiki/International_Institute_for_Democracy_and_Electoral_Assistance "International Institute for Democracy and Electoral Assistance")'s Global State of Democracy (GSoD) Indices and Democracy Tracker, Italy performs in the high range on overall democratic measures, with particular weaknesses in [rule of law](/wiki/Rule_of_law "Rule of law").[[158]](#cite_note-162)

### Government

Main article: [Government of Italy](/wiki/Government_of_Italy "Government of Italy")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2c/Sergio_Mattarella_Official_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Sergio_Mattarella_Official_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sergio_Mattarella_Official_(cropped).jpg)

[Sergio Mattarella](/wiki/Sergio_Mattarella "Sergio Mattarella")  
[President](/wiki/President_of_Italy "President of Italy")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b6/Giorgia_Meloni_Official_2023_crop.jpg/250px-Giorgia_Meloni_Official_2023_crop.jpg)](/wiki/File:Giorgia_Meloni_Official_2023_crop.jpg)

[Giorgia Meloni](/wiki/Giorgia_Meloni "Giorgia Meloni")  
[Prime Minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Italy "Prime Minister of Italy")
