Italy has a parliamentary government based on a mixed [proportional](/wiki/Proportional_representation "Proportional representation") and majoritarian voting system. The parliament is perfectly [bicameral](/wiki/Bicameral "Bicameral"); each house has the same powers. The two houses: the [Chamber of Deputies](/wiki/Chamber_of_Deputies_of_Italy "Chamber of Deputies of Italy") meets in [Palazzo Montecitorio](/wiki/Palazzo_Montecitorio "Palazzo Montecitorio"), and the [Senate of the Republic](/wiki/Senate_of_Italy "Senate of Italy") in [Palazzo Madama](/wiki/Palazzo_Madama,_Rome "Palazzo Madama, Rome"). A peculiarity of the [Italian Parliament](/wiki/Italian_Parliament "Italian Parliament") is the representation given to [Italian citizens](/wiki/Italian_nationality_law "Italian nationality law") permanently living abroad: 8 Deputies and 4 Senators are elected in four distinct [overseas constituencies](/wiki/Parliament_of_Italy#Overseas_constituency "Parliament of Italy"). There are [senators for life](/wiki/Senators_for_life "Senators for life"), appointed by the president "for outstanding patriotic merits in the social, scientific, artistic or literary field". Former presidents are *[ex officio](/wiki/Ex_officio_member "Ex officio member")* life senators.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cb/Palazzo_Madama_%28Roma%29.jpg/250px-Palazzo_Madama_%28Roma%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Palazzo_Madama_(Roma).jpg)

[Palazzo Madama](/wiki/Palazzo_Madama,_Rome "Palazzo Madama, Rome") in Rome, seat of the [Senate of the Republic](/wiki/Senate_of_the_Republic_(Italy) "Senate of the Republic (Italy)"), the upper house of the [Italian Parliament](/wiki/Italian_Parliament "Italian Parliament")

The [prime minister of Italy](/wiki/Prime_minister_of_Italy "Prime minister of Italy") is head of government and has executive authority, but must receive a vote of approval from the Council of Ministers to execute most policies. The prime minister and cabinet are appointed by the president, and confirmed by a vote of confidence in parliament. To remain as prime minister, one has to pass votes of confidence. The role of prime minister is similar to most other [parliamentary systems](/wiki/Parliamentary_system "Parliamentary system"), but they are not authorised to dissolve parliament. Another difference is that the political responsibility for intelligence is with the prime minister, who has exclusive power to coordinate intelligence policies, determine financial resources, strengthen cybersecurity, apply and protect State secrets, and authorise agents to carry out operations, in Italy or abroad.[[159]](#cite_note-163)

The major political parties are the [Brothers of Italy](/wiki/Brothers_of_Italy "Brothers of Italy"), [Democratic Party](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(Italy) "Democratic Party (Italy)"), and [Five Star Movement](/wiki/Five_Star_Movement "Five Star Movement"). During the 2022 general election, these three and their coalitions won 357 of the 400 seats in the Chamber of Deputies, and 187 of 200 in the Senate. The centre-right coalition, which included Giorgia Meloni's Brothers of Italy, [Matteo Salvini](/wiki/Matteo_Salvini "Matteo Salvini")'s League, Silvio Berlusconi's [Forza Italia](/wiki/Forza_Italia_(2013) "Forza Italia (2013)"), and [Maurizio Lupi](/wiki/Maurizio_Lupi "Maurizio Lupi")'s [Us Moderates](/wiki/Us_Moderates "Us Moderates"), won most seats in parliament. The rest were taken by the centre-left coalition, which included the Democratic Party, the [Greens and Left Alliance](/wiki/Greens_and_Left_Alliance "Greens and Left Alliance"), [Aosta Valley](/wiki/Aosta_Valley_(political_coalition) "Aosta Valley (political coalition)"), [More Europe](/wiki/More_Europe "More Europe"), [Civic Commitment](/wiki/Civic_Commitment "Civic Commitment"), the Five Star Movement, [Action – Italia Viva](/wiki/Action_%E2%80%93_Italia_Viva "Action – Italia Viva"), [South Tyrolean People's Party](/wiki/South_Tyrolean_People%27s_Party "South Tyrolean People's Party"), [South calls North](/wiki/South_calls_North "South calls North"), and the [Associative Movement of Italians Abroad](/wiki/Associative_Movement_of_Italians_Abroad "Associative Movement of Italians Abroad").

### Law and criminal justice

Main articles: [Law of Italy](/wiki/Law_of_Italy "Law of Italy"), [Judiciary of Italy](/wiki/Judiciary_of_Italy "Judiciary of Italy"), and [Law enforcement in Italy](/wiki/Law_enforcement_in_Italy "Law enforcement in Italy")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a0/Roma_2011_08_07_Palazzo_di_Giustizia.jpg/250px-Roma_2011_08_07_Palazzo_di_Giustizia.jpg)](/wiki/File:Roma_2011_08_07_Palazzo_di_Giustizia.jpg)

The [Supreme Court of Cassation](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_Cassation_(Italy) "Supreme Court of Cassation (Italy)"), Rome

The law of Italy has several sources. These are hierarchical: the law or regulation from a lower source cannot conflict with the rule of an upper source (hierarchy of sources).[[160]](#cite_note-164) The [Constitution of 1948](/wiki/Constitution_of_Italy "Constitution of Italy") is the highest source.[[161]](#cite_note-165) The [Constitutional Court of Italy](/wiki/Constitutional_Court_of_Italy "Constitutional Court of Italy") rules on the conformity of laws with the constitution. The judiciary bases their decisions on [Roman law](/wiki/Roman_law "Roman law") modified by the [Napoleonic Code](/wiki/Napoleonic_Code "Napoleonic Code") and later statutes. The [Supreme Court of Cassation](/wiki/Court_of_Cassation_(Italy) "Court of Cassation (Italy)") is the highest court for both criminal and civil appeals.

Italy lags behind other Western European nations in [LGBT rights](/wiki/LGBT_rights_in_Italy "LGBT rights in Italy").[[162]](#cite_note-166) Italy's law prohibiting torture is considered behind international standards.[[163]](#cite_note-167)

Law enforcement is complex with multiple police forces.[[164]](#cite_note-Walters-168) The national policing agencies are the [Polizia di Stato](/wiki/Polizia_di_Stato "Polizia di Stato") ('State Police'), the [Carabinieri](/wiki/Carabinieri "Carabinieri"), the [Guardia di Finanza](/wiki/Guardia_di_Finanza "Guardia di Finanza") ('Financial Police'), and the [Polizia Penitenziaria](/wiki/Polizia_Penitenziaria "Polizia Penitenziaria") ('Prison Police'),[[165]](#cite_note-BuonannoMastrobuoni-169) as well as the [Guardia Costiera](/wiki/Guardia_Costiera "Guardia Costiera") ('[Coast Guard Police](/wiki/Water_police "Water police")').[[164]](#cite_note-Walters-168) Although policing is primarily provided on a national basis,[[165]](#cite_note-BuonannoMastrobuoni-169) there are also the [provincial](/wiki/Provincial_police "Provincial police") and [municipal](/wiki/Municipal_police_(Italy) "Municipal police (Italy)") police.[[164]](#cite_note-Walters-168)

Since their appearance in the middle of the 19th century, [Italian organised crime](/wiki/Organized_crime_in_Italy "Organized crime in Italy") and criminal organisations have infiltrated the social and economic life of many regions in southern Italy; the most notorious is the Sicilian Mafia, which expanded into foreign countries, including the US. Mafia receipts may reach 9%[[166]](#cite_note-170) of GDP.[[167]](#cite_note-171) A 2009 report identified 610 [*comuni*](/wiki/Comune "Comune") which have a strong Mafia presence, where 13 million Italians live and 15% of GDP is produced.[[168]](#cite_note-172) The Calabrian ['Ndrangheta](/wiki/%27Ndrangheta "'Ndrangheta"), probably the most powerful crime syndicate of Italy, accounts alone for 3% of GDP.[[169]](#cite_note-173)

At 0.013 per 1,000 people, Italy has the 47th highest murder rate,[[170]](#cite_note-174) compared to 61 countries, and the 43rd highest number of rapes per 1,000 people, compared to 64 countries in the world. These are relatively low figures among developed countries.

### Foreign relations

Main article: [Foreign relations of Italy](/wiki/Foreign_relations_of_Italy "Foreign relations of Italy")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ef/G7_Summit_2024_-_Family_photo_-_02.jpg/250px-G7_Summit_2024_-_Family_photo_-_02.jpg)](/wiki/File:G7_Summit_2024_-_Family_photo_-_02.jpg)

Group photo of the [G7](/wiki/Group_of_Seven "Group of Seven") leaders at the [50th G7 summit](/wiki/50th_G7_summit "50th G7 summit") in [Apulia](/wiki/Apulia "Apulia")

Italy is a founding member of the [European Economic Community](/wiki/European_Economic_Community "European Economic Community") (EEC), now the European Union (EU), and of [NATO](/wiki/NATO "NATO"). Italy was admitted to the United Nations in 1955, and is a member and strong supporter of international organisations, such as the [OECD](/wiki/OECD "OECD"), the [General Agreement on Tariffs and Trade](/wiki/General_Agreement_on_Tariffs_and_Trade "General Agreement on Tariffs and Trade")/[World Trade Organization](/wiki/World_Trade_Organization "World Trade Organization") (GATT/WTO), the [Organization for Security and Co-operation in Europe](/wiki/Organization_for_Security_and_Co-operation_in_Europe "Organization for Security and Co-operation in Europe") (OSCE), the [Council of Europe](/wiki/Council_of_Europe "Council of Europe"), and the [Central European Initiative](/wiki/Central_European_Initiative "Central European Initiative"). Its turns in the rotating presidencies of international organisations include the [Organization for Security and Co-operation in Europe](/wiki/Organization_for_Security_and_Co-operation_in_Europe "Organization for Security and Co-operation in Europe") in 2018, [G7](/wiki/G7 "G7") in 2017, and the [EU Council](/wiki/Presidency_of_the_Council_of_the_European_Union "Presidency of the Council of the European Union") in 2014. Italy is a recurrent [non-permanent member](/wiki/List_of_members_of_the_United_Nations_Security_Council "List of members of the United Nations Security Council") of the [UN Security Council](/wiki/UN_Security_Council "UN Security Council").

Italy strongly supports multilateral international politics, endorsing the UN and its international security activities. In 2013, Italy had 5,296 troops deployed abroad, engaged in 33 UN and NATO missions in 25 countries.[[171]](#cite_note-175) Italy deployed troops in support of UN peacekeeping missions in [Somalia](/wiki/UNITAF "UNITAF"), [Mozambique](/wiki/United_Nations_Operation_in_Mozambique "United Nations Operation in Mozambique"), and [East Timor](/wiki/United_Nations_Integrated_Mission_in_East_Timor "United Nations Integrated Mission in East Timor"). Italy provides support for NATO and UN operations in [Bosnia](/wiki/IFOR "IFOR"), [Kosovo](/wiki/Kosovo_Force "Kosovo Force"), and [Albania](/wiki/Operation_Sunrise_(Albania) "Operation Sunrise (Albania)"), and deployed over 2,000 troops to Afghanistan in support of [Operation Enduring Freedom](/wiki/Operation_Enduring_Freedom "Operation Enduring Freedom") (OEF) from 2003.

Italy supported international efforts to reconstruct and stabilise Iraq, but it had withdrawn its [military contingent](/wiki/Multi-National_Force_%E2%80%93_Iraq#2006_withdrawals "Multi-National Force – Iraq") of 3,200 troops by 2006. In August 2006, Italy deployed about 2,450 troops for the [United Nations Interim Force in Lebanon](/wiki/United_Nations_Interim_Force_in_Lebanon "United Nations Interim Force in Lebanon").[[172]](#cite_note-176) Italy is one of the largest financiers of the [Palestinian Authority](/wiki/Palestinian_Authority "Palestinian Authority"), contributing €60 million in 2013 alone.[[173]](#cite_note-177)

### Military

Main articles: [Italian Armed Forces](/wiki/Italian_Armed_Forces "Italian Armed Forces") and [Military history of Italy](/wiki/Military_history_of_Italy "Military history of Italy")

See also: [List of wars involving Italy](/wiki/List_of_wars_involving_Italy "List of wars involving Italy")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b6/Cavour_%28550%29.jpg/250px-Cavour_%28550%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cavour_(550).jpg)

Aircraft carrier *[Cavour](/wiki/Italian_aircraft_carrier_Cavour "Italian aircraft carrier Cavour")*, the [flagship](/wiki/Flagship "Flagship") of the [Italian Navy](/wiki/Italian_Navy "Italian Navy")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/70/Centauro01.JPEG/250px-Centauro01.JPEG)](/wiki/File:Centauro01.JPEG)

An Italian Army [Centauro](/wiki/Centauro_(Tank_destroyer) "Centauro (Tank destroyer)") tank destroyer during a patrol in [Bosnia-Herzegovina](/wiki/Bosnia-Herzegovina "Bosnia-Herzegovina") as part of [IFOR](/wiki/IFOR "IFOR")

The [military history of Italy](/wiki/Military_history_of_Italy "Military history of Italy") chronicles a vast time period, lasting from the military conflicts fought by the [ancient peoples of Italy](/wiki/Ancient_peoples_of_Italy "Ancient peoples of Italy"), most notably the conquest of the Mediterranean world by the [ancient Romans](/wiki/Ancient_Romans "Ancient Romans"), through the expansion of the Italian [city-states](/wiki/Italian_city-states "Italian city-states") and [maritime republics](/wiki/Maritime_republics "Maritime republics") during the medieval period and the involvement of the [historical Italian states](/wiki/List_of_historical_states_of_Italy "List of historical states of Italy") in the [Italian Wars](/wiki/Italian_Wars "Italian Wars") and the [wars of succession](/wiki/Wars_of_succession "Wars of succession"), to the Napoleonic period, the [Italian unification](/wiki/Italian_unification "Italian unification"), the campaigns of the [colonial empire](/wiki/Italian_Empire "Italian Empire"), the two [world wars](/wiki/World_wars "World wars"), and into the modern day, with world [peacekeeping](/wiki/Peacekeeping "Peacekeeping") operations under the aegis of [NATO](/wiki/NATO "NATO"), the EU or the UN.

The [Italian Army](/wiki/Italian_Army "Italian Army"), [Navy](/wiki/Italian_Navy "Italian Navy"), [Air Force](/wiki/Italian_Air_Force "Italian Air Force"), and [Carabinieri](/wiki/Carabinieri "Carabinieri") collectively form the Italian Armed Forces, under the command of the [High Council of Defence](/wiki/High_Council_of_Defence_(Italy) "High Council of Defence (Italy)"), presided over by the president, per the [Constitution of Italy](/wiki/Constitution_of_Italy "Constitution of Italy"). According to Article 78, the [Parliament](/wiki/Italian_Parliament "Italian Parliament") has the authority to declare a state of war and vest the necessary war-making powers in the government.

Despite not being a branch of the armed forces, the [Guardia di Finanza](/wiki/Guardia_di_Finanza "Guardia di Finanza") has military status and is organised along military lines.[[e]](#cite_note-178) Since 2005, military service has been voluntary.[[174]](#cite_note-179) In 2010, the Italian military had 293,202 personnel on active duty,[[175]](#cite_note-180) of which 114,778 are Carabinieri.[[176]](#cite_note-181) As part of NATO's [nuclear sharing](/wiki/Nuclear_sharing "Nuclear sharing") strategy, Italy hosts 90 US [B61 nuclear bombs](/wiki/B61_nuclear_bomb "B61 nuclear bomb") located at the [Ghedi](/wiki/Ghedi_Air_Base "Ghedi Air Base") and [Aviano](/wiki/Aviano_Air_Base "Aviano Air Base") air bases.[[177]](#cite_note-182)

The Army is the national ground defence force. It was formed in 1946, when Italy became a republic, from what remained of the "[Royal Italian Army](/wiki/Royal_Italian_Army "Royal Italian Army")". Its best-known combat vehicles are the [Dardo](/wiki/Dardo_IFV "Dardo IFV") [infantry fighting vehicle](/wiki/Infantry_fighting_vehicle "Infantry fighting vehicle"), the [B1 Centauro](/wiki/B1_Centauro "B1 Centauro") [tank destroyer](/wiki/Tank_destroyer "Tank destroyer"), and the [Ariete](/wiki/Ariete "Ariete") [tank](/wiki/Tank "Tank"), and among its aircraft are the [Mangusta](/wiki/Agusta_A129_Mangusta "Agusta A129 Mangusta") [attack helicopter](/wiki/Attack_helicopter "Attack helicopter"), deployed on EU, NATO, and UN missions. It has at its disposal [Leopard 1](/wiki/Leopard_1 "Leopard 1") and [M113](/wiki/M113 "M113") armoured vehicles.

The Italian Navy is a [blue-water navy](/wiki/Blue-water_navy "Blue-water navy"). It was also formed in 1946 from what remained of the *[Regia Marina](/wiki/Regia_Marina "Regia Marina")* (the 'Royal Navy'). As a member of the EU and NATO, the Navy has participated in coalition peacekeeping operations worldwide. In 2014, the Navy operated 154 vessels in service, including minor auxiliary vessels.[[178]](#cite_note-183)

The Italian Air Force was founded as an independent service arm in 1923 by King Victor Emmanuel III as the *[Regia Aeronautica](/wiki/Regia_Aeronautica "Regia Aeronautica")* ('Royal Air Force'). After World War II, it was renamed as the *Aeronautica Militare*. In 2021, the Italian Air Force operated 219 combat jets. A transport capability is guaranteed by a fleet of 27 [C-130Js](/wiki/Lockheed_Martin_C-130J_Super_Hercules "Lockheed Martin C-130J Super Hercules") and [C-27J Spartan](/wiki/C-27J_Spartan "C-27J Spartan"). The acrobatic display team is the *[Frecce Tricolori](/wiki/Frecce_Tricolori "Frecce Tricolori")* ('Tricolour Arrows').

An autonomous corps of the military, the Carabinieri are the [gendarmerie](/wiki/Gendarmerie "Gendarmerie") and [military police](/wiki/Military_police "Military police") of Italy, policing the military and civilian population alongside [Italy's other police forces](/wiki/Law_enforcement_in_Italy "Law enforcement in Italy"). While different branches of the Carabinieri report to separate ministries, the corps reports to the Ministry of Internal Affairs when maintaining public order and security.[[179]](#cite_note-184)

## Economy

Main article: [Economy of Italy](/wiki/Economy_of_Italy "Economy of Italy")

See also: [List of largest Italian companies](/wiki/List_of_largest_Italian_companies "List of largest Italian companies")

Italy has an advanced[[181]](#cite_note-186) [mixed economy](/wiki/Mixed_economy "Mixed economy") that is the third-largest in the [eurozone](/wiki/Eurozone "Eurozone") and [11th-largest](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_GDP_(PPP) "List of countries by GDP (PPP)") in the world by [purchasing power parity](/wiki/Purchasing_power_parity "Purchasing power parity")-adjusted GDP.[[7]](#cite_note-IMFWEO.IT-7) It possesses the [ninth-largest national wealth](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_total_wealth "List of countries by total wealth") and ranks [third in central bank gold reserves](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_gold_reserve_holdings "List of countries by gold reserve holdings"). As a founding member of the [G7](/wiki/G7 "G7"), the eurozone, and the [OECD](/wiki/OECD "OECD"), it is one of the most industrialised countries and a major [trading nation](/wiki/Trading_nation "Trading nation") in Europe;[[182]](#cite_note-187) as of 2024, it exported €612 billion in goods and had a trade surplus of €46 billion.[[183]](#cite_note-188) A [developed country](/wiki/Developed_country "Developed country") ranked [30th on the Human Development Index](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_Human_Development_Index "List of countries by Human Development Index"), Italy performs well in [life expectancy](/wiki/Life_expectancy "Life expectancy"), [healthcare](/wiki/Healthcare_in_Italy "Healthcare in Italy"),[[184]](#cite_note-189) and [education](/wiki/Education_Index "Education Index"). It is well known for its creative and innovative businesses,[[185]](#cite_note-190) a competitive agricultural sector,[[186]](#cite_note-191) and its influential and high-quality automobile, machinery, food, design, and fashion industries.[[187]](#cite_note-192)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/12/Milan_skyline_skyscrapers_of_Porta_Nuova_business_district.jpg/250px-Milan_skyline_skyscrapers_of_Porta_Nuova_business_district.jpg)](/wiki/File:Milan_skyline_skyscrapers_of_Porta_Nuova_business_district.jpg)

[Milan](/wiki/Milan "Milan") is the economic capital of Italy[[188]](#cite_note-193) and a global [financial centre](/wiki/Financial_centre "Financial centre") and [fashion capital](/wiki/Fashion_capital "Fashion capital").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/56/Siena%2C_Piazza_Salimbeni_%28Bank_Monte_dei_Paschi_di_Siena%29_%2838588876202%29.jpg/250px-Siena%2C_Piazza_Salimbeni_%28Bank_Monte_dei_Paschi_di_Siena%29_%2838588876202%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Siena,_Piazza_Salimbeni_(Bank_Monte_dei_Paschi_di_Siena)_(38588876202).jpg)

[Banca Monte dei Paschi di Siena](/wiki/Banca_Monte_dei_Paschi_di_Siena "Banca Monte dei Paschi di Siena"), founded in 1472, is the world's [oldest or second oldest bank in continuous operation](/wiki/List_of_oldest_banks "List of oldest banks").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/86/The_Eni_building%2C_Quartiere_XXXII_Europa%2C_Roma%2C_Lazio%2C_Italy_-_panoramio.jpg/250px-The_Eni_building%2C_Quartiere_XXXII_Europa%2C_Roma%2C_Lazio%2C_Italy_-_panoramio.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Eni_building,_Quartiere_XXXII_Europa,_Roma,_Lazio,_Italy_-_panoramio.jpg)

[Eni](/wiki/Eni "Eni") is considered one of the world's oil and gas [supermajors](/wiki/Supermajors "Supermajors").[[189]](#cite_note-194)

Italy is the world's [eight-largest manufacturing country](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_manufacturing_output "List of countries by manufacturing output") and the second largest in Europe,[[190]](#cite_note-195) characterised by fewer multinational corporations than other economies of comparable size and many dynamic [small- and medium-sized enterprises](/wiki/Small_and_medium-sized_enterprises "Small and medium-sized enterprises"), clustered in industrial districts, which are the backbone of Italian industry. This has produced a niche-markets manufacturing sector often focused on the export of luxury products. While less capable of competing on quantity, it can compete with Asian economies that have lower labour costs through higher-quality products.[[191]](#cite_note-196) Italy was the world's [9th-largest exporter](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_exports "List of countries by exports") in 2023. [Its closest trade ties](/wiki/List_of_the_largest_trading_partners_of_Italy "List of the largest trading partners of Italy") are with other EU countries, and its largest export partners in 2019 were Germany (12%), France (11%), and the US (10%).[[192]](#cite_note-cia.gov-197)

The [Italian automotive industry](/wiki/Italian_automotive_industry "Italian automotive industry") is a significant part of the country's manufacturing sector, with over 144,000 firms and almost 485,000 employees in 2015,[[193]](#cite_note-198) contributing 9% to GDP.[[194]](#cite_note-199)[[195]](#cite_note-200) The country boasts a wide range of vehicles, from [mass market](/wiki/Mass_market "Mass market")-oriented brands such as [Fiat](/wiki/Fiat "Fiat") and [premium](/wiki/Premium_product "Premium product") brands like [Alfa Romeo](/wiki/Alfa_Romeo "Alfa Romeo") and [Maserati](/wiki/Maserati "Maserati") to luxury [supercars](/wiki/Supercars "Supercars") such as [Pagani](/wiki/Pagani_(company) "Pagani (company)"), [Lamborghini](/wiki/Lamborghini "Lamborghini"), and [Ferrari](/wiki/Ferrari "Ferrari").

The [Banca Monte dei Paschi di Siena](/wiki/Banca_Monte_dei_Paschi_di_Siena "Banca Monte dei Paschi di Siena") is the world's oldest or second oldest bank in continuous operation, depending on the definition, and the fourth-largest Italian commercial and retail bank.[[196]](#cite_note-201) Italy has a strong [cooperative](/wiki/Cooperative "Cooperative") sector with the largest share in the EU of the population (4.5%) employed by a cooperative.[[197]](#cite_note-202) The [Val d'Agri](/wiki/Val%27d_Agri_oil_field "Val'd Agri oil field") area, Basilicata, hosts the largest [onshore](/wiki/Onshore_(hydrocarbons) "Onshore (hydrocarbons)") [hydrocarbon field](/wiki/Hydrocarbon_field "Hydrocarbon field") in Europe.[[198]](#cite_note-203) Moderate natural gas reserves, mainly in the [Po Valley](/wiki/Po_Valley "Po Valley") and offshore under the Adriatic, have been discovered and constitute the country's most important mineral resource. Italy is one of the world's leading producers of [pumice](/wiki/Pumice "Pumice"), [pozzolana](/wiki/Pozzolana "Pozzolana"), and [feldspar](/wiki/Feldspar "Feldspar").[[199]](#cite_note-204) Another notable resource is marble, especially the famous white [Carrara marble](/wiki/Carrara_marble "Carrara marble") from Tuscany.

Italy is part of a monetary union, the eurozone, which represents around 330 million citizens, and of the [European single market](/wiki/European_single_market "European single market"), which represents more than 500 million consumers. Several domestic commercial policies are determined by agreements among EU members and EU legislation. Italy joined the common European currency, the [euro](/wiki/Euro "Euro"), in 2002.[[200]](#cite_note-205) Its monetary policy is set by the [European Central Bank](/wiki/European_Central_Bank "European Central Bank").

Italy was hit by the [2008 financial crisis](/wiki/2008_financial_crisis "2008 financial crisis"), which exacerbated structural problems.[[201]](#cite_note-206) After strong GDP growth of 5–6% per year from the 1950s to the early 1970s,[[202]](#cite_note-207) and a progressive slowdown in the 1980–90s, the country stagnated in the 2000s.[[203]](#cite_note-208) Political efforts to revive growth with massive government spending produced a severe rise in [public debt](/wiki/Public_debt "Public debt"), which stood at over 132% of GDP in 2017;[[204]](#cite_note-209) the second highest in the EU, after Greece.[[205]](#cite_note-210) The largest portion of [Italian public debt](/wiki/Italian_government_debt "Italian government debt") is owned by national subjects, a major difference between Italy and Greece,[[206]](#cite_note-211) and the level of [household debt](/wiki/Household_debt "Household debt") is much lower than the OECD average.[[207]](#cite_note-212)

[A gaping north–south divide](/wiki/Southern_question "Southern question") is a major factor of socio-economic weakness;[[208]](#cite_note-213) there is a huge difference in official income between northern and southern regions and municipalities.[[209]](#cite_note-214) The richest province, [Alto Adige-South Tyrol](/wiki/Alto_Adige-South_Tyrol "Alto Adige-South Tyrol"), earns 152% of the national GDP per capita, while the poorest region, Calabria, earns 61%.[[210]](#cite_note-215) The unemployment rate (11%) is above the eurozone average,[[211]](#cite_note-216) but the disaggregated figure is 7% in the north and 19% in the south.[[212]](#cite_note-217) The [youth unemployment rate](/wiki/Youth_unemployment_in_Italy "Youth unemployment in Italy") (32% in 2018) is extremely high.

### Agriculture

Main article: [Agriculture in Italy](/wiki/Agriculture_in_Italy "Agriculture in Italy")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6f/Vineyards_in_Piemonte%2C_Italy.jpg/250px-Vineyards_in_Piemonte%2C_Italy.jpg)](/wiki/File:Vineyards_in_Piemonte,_Italy.jpg)

[Vineyards in Langhe and Montferrat, Piedmont](/wiki/Vineyard_Landscape_of_Piedmont:_Langhe-Roero_and_Monferrato "Vineyard Landscape of Piedmont: Langhe-Roero and Monferrato"). Italy is the world's [largest wine producer](/wiki/List_of_wine-producing_countries "List of wine-producing countries"), and has the widest variety of indigenous [grapevines](/wiki/Grapevine "Grapevine").[[213]](#cite_note-218)

According to the last agricultural census, there were 1.6 million farms in 2010 (−32% since 2000) covering 12,700,000 ha or 31,382,383 acres (63% are in south Italy).[[214]](#cite_note-agrocensus-219) 99% are family-operated and small, averaging only 8 ha (20 acres).[[214]](#cite_note-agrocensus-219) Of the area in agricultural use, grain fields take up 31%, [olive](/wiki/Olive "Olive") orchards 8%, [vineyards](/wiki/Vineyard "Vineyard") 5%, [citrus](/wiki/Citrus "Citrus") orchards 4%, [sugar beets](/wiki/Sugar_beet "Sugar beet") 2%, and horticulture 2%. The remainder is primarily dedicated to pastures (26%) and feed grains (12%).[[214]](#cite_note-agrocensus-219)

Italy is the world's [largest wine producer](/wiki/List_of_wine-producing_countries "List of wine-producing countries"),[[215]](#cite_note-220)[[216]](#cite_note-221) and a leading producer of [olive oil](/wiki/Olive_oil "Olive oil"), fruits (apples, olives, grapes, oranges, lemons, pears, apricots, hazelnuts, peaches, cherries, plums, strawberries, and kiwifruits), and vegetables (especially artichokes and tomatoes). The most famous [Italian wines](/wiki/Italian_wine "Italian wine") are the [Tuscan](/wiki/Tuscany_(wine) "Tuscany (wine)") [Chianti](/wiki/Chianti "Chianti") and the [Piedmontese](/wiki/Piedmont_(wine) "Piedmont (wine)") [Barolo](/wiki/Barolo "Barolo"). Other famous wines are [Barbaresco](/wiki/Barbaresco "Barbaresco"), [Barbera d'Asti](/wiki/Barbera_d%27Asti "Barbera d'Asti"), [Brunello di Montalcino](/wiki/Brunello_di_Montalcino "Brunello di Montalcino"), [Frascati](/wiki/Frascati_DOC "Frascati DOC"), [Montepulciano d'Abruzzo](/wiki/Montepulciano_d%27Abruzzo "Montepulciano d'Abruzzo"), [Morellino di Scansano](/wiki/Morellino_di_Scansano "Morellino di Scansano"), and the [sparkling wines](/wiki/Sparkling_wine "Sparkling wine") [Franciacorta](/wiki/Franciacorta_DOCG "Franciacorta DOCG") and [Prosecco](/wiki/Prosecco "Prosecco").

Quality goods in which Italy specialises, particularly wines and [regional cheeses](/wiki/List_of_Italian_DOP_cheeses "List of Italian DOP cheeses"), are often protected under the quality assurance labels [DOC/DOP](/wiki/Denominazione_di_origine_controllata "Denominazione di origine controllata"). This [geographical indication certificate](/wiki/Geographical_indications_and_traditional_specialities_in_the_European_Union "Geographical indications and traditional specialities in the European Union"), accredited by the EU, is considered important to avoid confusion with [ersatz goods](/wiki/Ersatz_good "Ersatz good").

### Transport

Main article: [Transport in Italy](/wiki/Transport_in_Italy "Transport in Italy")

See also: [Railway stations in Italy](/wiki/Railway_stations_in_Italy "Railway stations in Italy")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ee/A8-A26_Besnate.jpg/250px-A8-A26_Besnate.jpg)](/wiki/File:A8-A26_Besnate.jpg)

The Autostrada dei Laghi ('Lakes Motorway'; part of the [A8](/wiki/Autostrada_A8_(Italy) "Autostrada A8 (Italy)") and [A9](/wiki/Autostrada_A9_(Italy) "Autostrada A9 (Italy)")), the first motorway built in the world[[217]](#cite_note-independent-222)

Italy was the first country to build motorways, the *[autostrade](/wiki/Autostrade "Autostrade")*, reserved for fast traffic and motor vehicles.[[217]](#cite_note-independent-222) In 2002 there were 668,721 km (415,524 mi) of serviceable [roads in Italy](/wiki/Roads_in_Italy "Roads in Italy"), including 6,487 km (4,031 mi) of motorways, state-owned but privately operated by [Atlantia](/wiki/Atlantia_(company) "Atlantia (company)"). In 2005, about 34,667,000 cars (590 per 1,000 people) and 4,015,000 goods vehicles circulated on the network.[[218]](#cite_note-European_Commission-223)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/94/Etr500.JPG/250px-Etr500.JPG)](/wiki/File:Etr500.JPG)

An [ETR 500](/wiki/ETR_500 "ETR 500") train on the [Florence–Rome high-speed line](/wiki/Florence%E2%80%93Rome_high-speed_railway "Florence–Rome high-speed railway"), the first high-speed railway built in Europe[[219]](#cite_note-224)

The [railway network](/wiki/Rail_transport_in_Italy "Rail transport in Italy"), state-owned and operated by [Rete Ferroviaria Italiana](/wiki/Ferrovie_dello_Stato "Ferrovie dello Stato") (FSI), in 2024 totalled 16,879 km (10,488 mi), of which 12,277 km (7,629 mi) is electrified,[[220]](#cite_note-225) and on which 4,802 locomotives and railcars run. The main public operator of high-speed trains is [Trenitalia](/wiki/Trenitalia "Trenitalia"), part of FSI. High-speed trains are in three categories: [Frecciarossa](/wiki/Frecciarossa "Frecciarossa") ('red arrow') trains operate at a maximum 300 km/h on dedicated high-speed tracks; [Frecciargento](/wiki/Frecciargento "Frecciargento") ('silver arrow') operate at a maximum 250 km/h on high-speed and mainline tracks; and [Frecciabianca](/wiki/Frecciabianca "Frecciabianca") ('white arrow') operate on high-speed regional lines at a maximum 200 km/h. Italy has 11 rail border crossings over the Alpine mountains with neighbouring countries.

Italy is fifth in Europe by number of passengers using air transport, with about 148 million passengers, or about 10% of the European total in 2011.[[221]](#cite_note-226) In 2022, there were 45 civil airports, including the hubs of [Milan Malpensa Airport](/wiki/Milan_Malpensa_Airport "Milan Malpensa Airport") and [Rome Fiumicino Airport](/wiki/Rome_Fiumicino_Airport "Rome Fiumicino Airport").[[222]](#cite_note-227) Since 2021, Italy's flag carrier has been [ITA Airways](/wiki/ITA_Airways "ITA Airways"), which took over from [Alitalia](/wiki/Alitalia "Alitalia").[[223]](#cite_note-228)

In 2004, there were 43 major seaports, including [Genoa](/wiki/Genoa "Genoa"), the country's largest and second-largest in the Mediterranean. In 2005, Italy maintained a civilian air fleet of about 389,000 units and a merchant fleet of 581 ships.[[218]](#cite_note-European_Commission-223) The national inland waterways network had a length of 2,400 km (1,491 mi) for commercial traffic in 2012.[[192]](#cite_note-cia.gov-197) North Italian ports, such as the deep-water port of Trieste, with its extensive rail connections to Central and Eastern Europe, are the destination of subsidies and significant foreign investment.[[224]](#cite_note-229)

In August 2025, the planned [Strait of Messina Bridge](/wiki/Strait_of_Messina_Bridge "Strait of Messina Bridge") was given final approval by the Meloni government, with construction set to commence in the autumn of 2025. It will connect [Calabria](/wiki/Calabria "Calabria") with [Sicily](/wiki/Sicily "Sicily") when it opens in 2032, and it will become the [longest suspension bridge in the world](/wiki/List_of_longest_suspension_bridge_spans "List of longest suspension bridge spans").[[225]](#cite_note-230)

### Energy

Main article: [Energy in Italy](/wiki/Energy_in_Italy "Energy in Italy")

Further information: [Renewable energy in Italy](/wiki/Renewable_energy_in_Italy "Renewable energy in Italy") and [Electricity sector in Italy](/wiki/Electricity_sector_in_Italy "Electricity sector in Italy")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/53/Pannelli_solari_Unicoop_Tirreno.JPG/250px-Pannelli_solari_Unicoop_Tirreno.JPG)](/wiki/File:Pannelli_solari_Unicoop_Tirreno.JPG)

Solar panels in [Piombino](/wiki/Piombino "Piombino"), Tuscany. Italy is one of the world's largest producers of renewable energy.[[226]](#cite_note-legambiente2015-231)

Italy has become one of the world's [largest producers of renewable energy](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_electricity_production_from_renewable_sources "List of countries by electricity production from renewable sources"), ranking as the second largest producer in the EU and the ninth in the world. [Wind power](/wiki/Wind_power_in_Italy "Wind power in Italy"), [hydroelectricity](/wiki/Hydroelectricity_in_Italy "Hydroelectricity in Italy"), and [geothermal power](/wiki/Geothermal_power_in_Italy "Geothermal power in Italy") are significant [sources of electricity in the country](/wiki/Electricity_sector_in_Italy "Electricity sector in Italy"). [Renewable sources](/wiki/Renewable_energy_in_Italy "Renewable energy in Italy") account for 28% of all electricity produced, with hydro alone reaching 13%, followed by solar at 6%, wind at 4%, bioenergy at 3.5%, and geothermal at 1.6%.[[227]](#cite_note-gse-232) The rest of the national demand is supplied by fossil fuels (natural gas 38%, coal 13%, oil 8%) and imports.[[227]](#cite_note-gse-232) [Eni](/wiki/Eni "Eni"), operating in 79 countries, is one of the seven "[Big Oil](/wiki/Big_Oil "Big Oil")" companies, and one of the world's largest industrial companies.[[228]](#cite_note-233)

[Solar energy](/wiki/Solar_power_in_Italy "Solar power in Italy") production alone accounted for 9% of electricity in 2014, making Italy the country with the highest contribution from solar energy in the world.[[226]](#cite_note-legambiente2015-231) The [Montalto di Castro Photovoltaic Power Station](/wiki/Montalto_di_Castro_Photovoltaic_Power_Station "Montalto di Castro Photovoltaic Power Station"), completed in 2010, is the largest photovoltaic (PV) power station in Italy.[[229]](#cite_note-234) Italy was the first country to exploit [geothermal energy](/wiki/Geothermal_power_in_Italy "Geothermal power in Italy") to produce electricity.[[230]](#cite_note-235) [Nuclear power in Italy](/wiki/Nuclear_power_in_Italy "Nuclear power in Italy") was abandoned after [1987 referendums](/wiki/1987_Italian_referendums "1987 Italian referendums") (in the wake of the 1986 [Chernobyl disaster](/wiki/Chernobyl_disaster "Chernobyl disaster")), although Italy still imports nuclear energy from Italy-owned reactors in foreign territories.

### Science and technology

Main article: [Science and technology in Italy](/wiki/Science_and_technology_in_Italy "Science and technology in Italy")

See also: [List of Italian inventions and discoveries](/wiki/List_of_Italian_inventions_and_discoveries "List of Italian inventions and discoveries")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d4/Justus_Sustermans_-_Portrait_of_Galileo_Galilei%2C_1636.jpg/250px-Justus_Sustermans_-_Portrait_of_Galileo_Galilei%2C_1636.jpg)](/wiki/File:Justus_Sustermans_-_Portrait_of_Galileo_Galilei,_1636.jpg)

[Galileo Galilei](/wiki/Galileo_Galilei "Galileo Galilei"), widely considered the father of modern science, physics and astronomy

Through the centuries, Italy has fostered a scientific community that produced major discoveries the sciences. [Galileo Galilei](/wiki/Galileo_Galilei "Galileo Galilei") played a major role in the [Scientific Revolution](/wiki/Scientific_Revolution "Scientific Revolution") and is widely considered the father of [observational astronomy](/wiki/Observational_astronomy "Observational astronomy"),[[231]](#cite_note-236) modern physics,[[232]](#cite_note-237) and the [scientific method](/wiki/Scientific_method "Scientific method").[[233]](#cite_note-238)[[234]](#cite_note-239)

The [Laboratori Nazionali del Gran Sasso](/wiki/Laboratori_Nazionali_del_Gran_Sasso "Laboratori Nazionali del Gran Sasso") (LNGS) is the largest underground research centre in the world.[[235]](#cite_note-240) Trieste has the highest percentage of researchers in Europe, in relation to the population.[[236]](#cite_note-241) Italy was ranked 28th in the [Global Innovation Index](/wiki/Global_Innovation_Index "Global Innovation Index") in 2025.[[237]](#cite_note-242)[[238]](#cite_note-243) There are [technology parks](/wiki/Technology_park "Technology park") in Italy such as the Science and Technology Parks Kilometro Rosso (Bergamo), the [AREA Science Park](/wiki/AREA_Science_Park "AREA Science Park") (Trieste), The VEGA-Venice Gateway for Science and Technology (Venezia), the Toscana Life Sciences (Siena), the Technology Park of Lodi Cluster (Lodi), and the Technology Park of Navacchio (Pisa),[[239]](#cite_note-244) as well as [science museums](/wiki/Science_museum "Science museum") such as the [Museo Nazionale Scienza e Tecnologia Leonardo da Vinci](/wiki/Museo_Nazionale_Scienza_e_Tecnologia_Leonardo_da_Vinci "Museo Nazionale Scienza e Tecnologia Leonardo da Vinci") in Milan.

The north–south large difference in income leads to a "[digital divide](/wiki/Digital_divide "Digital divide")".[[240]](#cite_note-245)[[241]](#cite_note-246)

### Tourism

Main article: [Tourism in Italy](/wiki/Tourism_in_Italy "Tourism in Italy")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bc/Positano_-_Fornillo_Beach.jpg/250px-Positano_-_Fornillo_Beach.jpg)](/wiki/File:Positano_-_Fornillo_Beach.jpg)

The [Amalfi Coast](/wiki/Amalfi_Coast "Amalfi Coast") is one of Italy's major tourist destinations.[[242]](#cite_note-247)

People have visited Italy for centuries, yet the first to [visit the peninsula for tourism](/wiki/Tourism_in_Italy "Tourism in Italy") were aristocrats during the [Grand Tour](/wiki/Grand_Tour "Grand Tour"), which began in the 17th century, and flourished in the 18th and the 19th centuries.[[243]](#cite_note-grand-tour-248) This was a period in which European aristocrats, many of whom were British, visited parts of Europe, with Italy as a key destination.[[243]](#cite_note-grand-tour-248) For Italy, this was to study ancient architecture, local culture, and admire its natural beauty.[[244]](#cite_note-249)

Italy is the [fifth-most visited country](/wiki/World_Tourism_rankings "World Tourism rankings"), with a total of 57 million arrivals in 2024.[[245]](#cite_note-250) In 2014, the income from travel and tourism was EUR163 billion (10% of GDP) and 1,082,000 jobs were directly related to it (5% of employment).[[246]](#cite_note-251)

Tourist interest is mainly in [culture](/wiki/Culture_of_Italy "Culture of Italy"), [cuisine](/wiki/Italian_cuisine "Italian cuisine"), [history](/wiki/History_of_Italy "History of Italy"), [architecture](/wiki/Architecture_of_Italy "Architecture of Italy"), [art](/wiki/Italian_art "Italian art"), religious sites and routes, wedding tourism, naturalistic beauties, nightlife, underwater sites, and spas.[[247]](#cite_note-252) Winter and summer tourism are present in locations in the Alps and the [Apennines](/wiki/Apennines "Apennines"),[[248]](#cite_note-253) while seaside tourism is widespread among locations along the Mediterranean.[[249]](#cite_note-254) Italy is the leading cruise tourism destination in the Mediterranean.[[250]](#cite_note-255) Small, historical, and artistic villages are promoted through the association [I Borghi più belli d'Italia](/wiki/I_Borghi_pi%C3%B9_belli_d%27Italia "I Borghi più belli d'Italia") (lit. 'The most beautiful villages of Italy').

The most visited regions are Veneto, Tuscany, Lombardy, Emilia-Romagna, and Lazio.[[251]](#cite_note-256) Rome is the third most visited city in Europe, and 12th in the world, with 9.4 million arrivals in 2017.[[252]](#cite_note-257) Venice and Florence are among the world's top 100 destinations.

Italy has the most [World Heritage Sites](/wiki/World_Heritage_Sites "World Heritage Sites") of any country: [61](/wiki/List_of_World_Heritage_Sites_in_Italy "List of World Heritage Sites in Italy"),[[253]](#cite_note-258) of which 55 are cultural and 6 natural.[[254]](#cite_note-259)

## Demographics

Main article: [Demographics of Italy](/wiki/Demographics_of_Italy "Demographics of Italy")

See also: [Italians](/wiki/Italians "Italians"), [Internal migration in Italy](/wiki/Internal_migration_in_Italy "Internal migration in Italy"), [Italian diaspora](/wiki/Italian_diaspora "Italian diaspora"), [Genetic history of Italy](/wiki/Genetic_history_of_Italy "Genetic history of Italy"), and [List of cities in Italy](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_Italy "List of cities in Italy")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/53/Map_of_population_density_in_Italy_%282011_census%29_alt_colours.jpg/250px-Map_of_population_density_in_Italy_%282011_census%29_alt_colours.jpg)](/wiki/File:Map_of_population_density_in_Italy_(2011_census)_alt_colours.jpg)

Map of Italy's population density (2011 census)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7d/Map_of_the_Italian_Diaspora_in_the_World.svg/250px-Map_of_the_Italian_Diaspora_in_the_World.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Map_of_the_Italian_Diaspora_in_the_World.svg)

[Italian diaspora](/wiki/Italian_diaspora "Italian diaspora") in the world

As of 2025, Italy has 58,915,561 inhabitants.[[6]](#cite_note-population-6) Its population density of 195 inhabitants per square kilometre (510/sq mi) is higher than most Western European countries. However, distribution is uneven: the most densely populated areas are the Po Valley (almost half the population) and the metropolitan areas of Rome and Naples, while vast regions such as the Alps and Apennine highlands, the plateaus of Basilicata, and the island of Sardinia, as well as much of Sicily, are sparsely populated.

Italy's population almost doubled during the 20th century, but the pattern of growth was uneven because of large-scale [internal migration from the rural south to the industrial north](/wiki/Internal_migration_in_Italy "Internal migration in Italy"), a consequence of the [Italian economic miracle](/wiki/Italian_economic_miracle "Italian economic miracle") of the 1950–1960s. High fertility rates persisted until the 1970s, after which they started to decline; the [total fertility rate](/wiki/Total_fertility_rate "Total fertility rate") (TFR) reached an all-time low of 1.2 children per woman in 1995, well below the replacement rate of 2.1 and considerably below the high of 5 in 1883.[[255]](#cite_note-260) Since 2008, when the rate climbed slightly to 1.4,[[256]](#cite_note-261)[[257]](#cite_note-262) the number of births has consistently declined every year, reaching a record low of 379,000 in 2023 – the fewest since 1861.[[258]](#cite_note-www.reuters.com-263) In 2024, it stood at 1.2.[[259]](#cite_note-264)

As a result of these trends, Italy's population is rapidly aging and gradually shrinking. Nearly one in four Italians is over 65,[[258]](#cite_note-www.reuters.com-263) and the country has the [fourth oldest population in the world](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_median_age "List of countries by median age"), with a median age of 48 and an average age of 46.6.[[192]](#cite_note-cia.gov-197)[[260]](#cite_note-265) The overall population has been falling steadily since 2014 and is estimated to have fallen just below 59 million in 2024, representing a cumulative loss of more than 1.36 million people in one decade.[[261]](#cite_note-266)

From the late 19th century to the 1960s, Italy was a country of mass emigration. Between 1898 and 1914, the peak years of [Italian diaspora](/wiki/Italian_diaspora "Italian diaspora"), approximately 750,000 Italians emigrated annually.[[262]](#cite_note-267) The diaspora included more than 25 million Italians and is considered the greatest mass migration of recent times.[[263]](#cite_note-268)
