### Immigration

Main articles: [Immigration to Italy](/wiki/Immigration_to_Italy "Immigration to Italy") and [2015 European migrant crisis](/wiki/2015_European_migrant_crisis "2015 European migrant crisis")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fc/Italy%2C_foreign_residents_as_a_percentage_of_the_total_population%2C_2011.svg/250px-Italy%2C_foreign_residents_as_a_percentage_of_the_total_population%2C_2011.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Italy,_foreign_residents_as_a_percentage_of_the_total_population,_2011.svg)

Foreign residents as a percentage of the regional population (2011 census)

In the 1980s, until then a linguistically and culturally homogeneous society, Italy began to attract substantial flows of immigrants.[[265]](#cite_note-270) After the [fall of the Berlin Wall](/wiki/Fall_of_the_Berlin_Wall "Fall of the Berlin Wall") and of the [Iron Curtain](/wiki/Iron_Curtain#Fall "Iron Curtain"), waves of migration originated from many former socialist countries of East Europe. The EU enlarged [in 2004](/wiki/2004_enlargement_of_the_European_Union "2004 enlargement of the European Union"), in 2007 (Romania and Bulgaria), and in 2013 ([Croatia](/wiki/Croatia "Croatia")).

Other sources of immigration have been neighbouring North Africa, the Asia-Pacific region,[[266]](#cite_note-271) the [Philippines](/wiki/Philippines "Philippines") and Latin America.

In 2010, the foreign-born population was from the following regions: Europe (54%), Africa (22%), Asia (16%), the Americas (8%), and Oceania (0.06%). The distribution of the foreign population is geographically varied: in 2020, 61% of foreign citizens lived in the north, 24% in the centre, 11% in the south, and 4% on the islands.[[267]](#cite_note-272)

In 2021, Italy had about 5.2 million foreign residents,[[1]](#cite_note-id2020-1)[[268]](#cite_note-273) making up 9% of the population. The figures include more than half a million children born in Italy to foreign nationals, but exclude foreign nationals who have subsequently acquired Italian citizenship;[[269]](#cite_note-274) in 2016, about 201,000 people became Italian citizens.[[270]](#cite_note-275) The official figures also do not include [illegal immigrants](/wiki/Illegal_immigrants "Illegal immigrants"), which was estimated to be 670,000 in 2008.[[271]](#cite_note-276) About one million [Romanian](/wiki/Romanian_diaspora "Romanian diaspora") citizens are registered as living in Italy, representing the largest migrant population.

### Languages

Main articles: [Languages of Italy](/wiki/Languages_of_Italy "Languages of Italy"), [Italian language](/wiki/Italian_language "Italian language"), [Regional Italian](/wiki/Regional_Italian "Regional Italian"), and [Geographical distribution of Italian speakers](/wiki/Geographical_distribution_of_Italian_speakers "Geographical distribution of Italian speakers")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ae/Linguistic_map_of_Italy.png/250px-Linguistic_map_of_Italy.png)](/wiki/File:Linguistic_map_of_Italy.png)

Map of the [languages spoken in Italy](/wiki/Languages_of_Italy "Languages of Italy")

Italy's official language is Italian.[[272]](#cite_note-lang-277)[[273]](#cite_note-278)
There are an estimated 64 million native Italian speakers around the world,[[274]](#cite_note-279) and another 21 million use it as a second language.[[275]](#cite_note-280)
Italian is often natively spoken as a [regional dialect](/wiki/Regional_Italian "Regional Italian"), not to be confused with Italy's regional and minority languages;[[276]](#cite_note-281) however, during the 20th century, the establishment of a national education system led to a decrease in regional dialects. Standardisation was further expanded in the 1950s and 1960s, due to economic growth and the rise of [mass media](/wiki/Media_of_Italy "Media of Italy") and television.

Twelve "historical minority languages" are formally recognised: Albanian, [Catalan](/wiki/Catalan_language "Catalan language"), German, Greek, Slovene, Croatian, French, Franco-Provençal, Friulian, [Ladin](/wiki/Ladin_language "Ladin language"), [Occitan](/wiki/Occitan_language "Occitan language"), and Sardinian.[[272]](#cite_note-lang-277) Four of these enjoy co-official status in their respective regions: French in the Aosta Valley;[[277]](#cite_note-282) German in [South Tyrol](/wiki/South_Tyrol "South Tyrol"), and Ladin as well in some parts of the same province and in parts of the neighbouring Trentino;[[278]](#cite_note-283) and [Slovene](/wiki/Slovene_language "Slovene language") in the provinces of [Trieste](/wiki/Province_of_Trieste "Province of Trieste"), [Gorizia](/wiki/Province_of_Gorizia "Province of Gorizia"), and [Udine](/wiki/Province_of_Udine "Province of Udine").[[279]](#cite_note-284) Other Ethnologue, ISO, and UNESCO languages are not recognised under Italian law. Like France, Italy has signed the [European Charter for Regional or Minority Languages](/wiki/European_Charter_for_Regional_or_Minority_Languages "European Charter for Regional or Minority Languages"), but has not ratified it.[[280]](#cite_note-285)[[281]](#cite_note-286)

Due to recent immigration, Italy has sizeable populations whose native language is not Italian, nor a regional language. According to the [Italian National Institute of Statistics](/wiki/Italian_National_Institute_of_Statistics "Italian National Institute of Statistics"), in 2012 Romanian was the most common mother tongue among foreign residents: almost 800,000 people speak Romanian as their first language (22% of foreign residents aged 6 and over). Other prevalent mother tongues were Arabic (spoken by over 475,000; 13% of foreign residents), Albanian (380,000), and Spanish (255,000).[[282]](#cite_note-287)

### Religion

Main articles: [Religion in Italy](/wiki/Religion_in_Italy "Religion in Italy") and [Catholic Church in Italy](/wiki/Catholic_Church_in_Italy "Catholic Church in Italy")

See also: [List of cathedrals in Italy](/wiki/List_of_cathedrals_in_Italy "List of cathedrals in Italy")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8b/PonteSantAngeloRom.jpg/330px-PonteSantAngeloRom.jpg)](/wiki/File:PonteSantAngeloRom.jpg)

[St. Peter's Basilica](/wiki/St._Peter%27s_Basilica "St. Peter's Basilica") viewed from the [Tiber](/wiki/Tiber "Tiber"); the [Vatican Hill](/wiki/Vatican_Hill "Vatican Hill") in the back and [Castel Sant'Angelo](/wiki/Castel_Sant%27Angelo "Castel Sant'Angelo") in Rome to the right. Both the basilica and the hill are part of the [sovereign state](/wiki/United_Nations_General_Assembly_observers "United Nations General Assembly observers") of [Vatican City](/wiki/Vatican_City "Vatican City"), the [Holy See](/wiki/Holy_See "Holy See") of the [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church").

The [Holy See](/wiki/Holy_See "Holy See"), the [episcopal jurisdiction of Rome](/wiki/Diocese_of_Rome "Diocese of Rome"), contains the government of [Vatican City](/wiki/Vatican_City "Vatican City") and the worldwide [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church"). It is recognised as a [sovereign](/wiki/Sovereignty "Sovereignty") entity, headed by the pope, who is also the [Bishop of Rome](/wiki/Bishop_of_Rome "Bishop of Rome"), with which diplomatic relations can be maintained.[[283]](#cite_note-288)[[f]](#cite_note-289)

Italy has historically been dominated by Catholicism.[[284]](#cite_note-Dell'orto-290)
Most Catholics are nominal; [Associated Press](/wiki/Associated_Press "Associated Press") described [Italian Catholicism](/wiki/Catholic_Church_in_Italy "Catholic Church in Italy") as "nominally embraced but rarely lived".[[284]](#cite_note-Dell'orto-290) Around 2010, Italy had the world's [fifth-largest Catholic population](/wiki/Catholic_Church_by_country "Catholic Church by country") and the largest in Europe.[[285]](#cite_note-291)[[286]](#cite_note-292) Since 1985, Catholicism is no longer the [state religion](/wiki/State_religion "State religion").[[287]](#cite_note-293)

In 2011, minority Christian faiths included an estimated 1.5 million Orthodox Christians, while [Protestantism](/wiki/Protestantism "Protestantism") has been growing.[[288]](#cite_note-294) Italy has for centuries welcomed Jews expelled from other countries, notably Spain. However, about 20% of Italian Jews were killed during [the Holocaust](/wiki/The_Holocaust "The Holocaust").[[289]](#cite_note-295) This, together with emigration before and after World War II, has left around 28,000 Jews.[[290]](#cite_note-296) There are 120,000 Hindus[[291]](#cite_note-297) and 70,000 Sikhs.[[292]](#cite_note-298)

The state devolves shares of income tax to recognised religious communities, under a regime known as [eight per thousand](/wiki/Eight_per_thousand "Eight per thousand"). Donations are allowed to Christian, Jewish, Buddhist, and Hindu communities; however, Islam remains excluded, as no Muslim communities have signed a concordat.[[293]](#cite_note-299) Taxpayers who do not wish to fund a religion contribute their share to the welfare system.[[294]](#cite_note-300)

### Education

Main article: [Education in Italy](/wiki/Education_in_Italy "Education in Italy")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e7/Archiginnasio_ora_blu_Bologna.jpg/250px-Archiginnasio_ora_blu_Bologna.jpg)](/wiki/File:Archiginnasio_ora_blu_Bologna.jpg)

[Bologna University](/wiki/Bologna_University "Bologna University"), established in 1088 AD, is the world's [oldest university in continuous operation](/wiki/List_of_oldest_universities_in_continuous_operation "List of oldest universities in continuous operation").

Education is mandatory and free from ages six to sixteen,[[295]](#cite_note-301) and consists of five stages: kindergarten, primary school, lower secondary school, upper secondary school, and university.[[296]](#cite_note-302)

Primary school lasts eight years. Students are given a basic education in Italian, English, mathematics, natural sciences, history, geography, social studies, physical education, and visual and musical arts. Secondary school lasts for five years and includes three traditional types of schools focused on different academic levels: the *[liceo](/wiki/Secondary_education_in_Italy#Liceo "Secondary education in Italy")* prepares students for university studies with a classical or scientific curriculum, while the *[istituto tecnico](/wiki/Istituto_tecnico "Istituto tecnico")* and the *[istituto professionale](/wiki/Istituto_professionale "Istituto professionale")* prepare pupils for vocations.

In 2018, secondary education was evaluated as being below the average among [OECD](/wiki/OECD "OECD") countries.[[297]](#cite_note-oecd.org-303) Italy scored below the OECD average in reading and science, and near the OECD average in mathematics.[[297]](#cite_note-oecd.org-303) A wide gap exists between northern schools, which perform near average, and the south, which had much poorer results.[[298]](#cite_note-304)

Tertiary education is divided between [public universities](/wiki/List_of_universities_in_Italy "List of universities in Italy"), private universities, and the prestigious and selective [superior graduate schools](/wiki/Superior_Graduate_Schools_in_Italy "Superior Graduate Schools in Italy"), such as the [Scuola Normale Superiore di Pisa](/wiki/Scuola_Normale_Superiore_di_Pisa "Scuola Normale Superiore di Pisa"). 33 Italian universities were ranked among the world's top 500 in 2019.[[299]](#cite_note-305) [Bologna University](/wiki/Bologna_University "Bologna University"), founded in 1088, is the [oldest university](/wiki/Oldest_university "Oldest university") still in operation,[[300]](#cite_note-306) and one of the leading academic institutions in Europe.[[301]](#cite_note-307) [Bocconi University](/wiki/Bocconi_University "Bocconi University"), the [Università Cattolica del Sacro Cuore](/wiki/Universit%C3%A0_Cattolica_del_Sacro_Cuore "Università Cattolica del Sacro Cuore"), [LUISS](/wiki/LUISS "LUISS"), the [Polytechnic University of Turin](/wiki/Polytechnic_University_of_Turin "Polytechnic University of Turin"), the [Polytechnic University of Milan](/wiki/Polytechnic_University_of_Milan "Polytechnic University of Milan"), the [Sapienza University of Rome](/wiki/Sapienza_University_of_Rome "Sapienza University of Rome"), and the [University of Milan](/wiki/University_of_Milan "University of Milan") are also ranked among the best.[[302]](#cite_note-308)

### Health

Main article: [Health in Italy](/wiki/Health_in_Italy "Health in Italy")

See also: [Healthcare in Italy](/wiki/Healthcare_in_Italy "Healthcare in Italy")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f6/Oil-1383546_1920.jpg/250px-Oil-1383546_1920.jpg)](/wiki/File:Oil-1383546_1920.jpg)

[Olive oil](/wiki/Olive_oil "Olive oil") and vegetables are central to the Mediterranean diet.[[303]](#cite_note-309)

Italy's life expectancy in 2015 was 80.5 years for men and 84.8 for women, placing the country [5th in the world](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_life_expectancy "List of countries by life expectancy").[[304]](#cite_note-310) Compared to other Western countries, Italy has a low rate of adult obesity (below 10%[[305]](#cite_note-311)), as the health benefits of the [Mediterranean diet](/wiki/Mediterranean_diet "Mediterranean diet") are very significant.[[306]](#cite_note-312) In 2013, [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO"), prompted by Italy, added the Mediterranean diet to the [Representative List of the Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity](/wiki/Representative_List_of_the_Intangible_Cultural_Heritage_of_Humanity "Representative List of the Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity") of Italy, Morocco, Spain, Portugal, Greece, Cyprus, and Croatia.[[307]](#cite_note-313)

The proportion of daily smokers was 22% in 2012, down from 24% in 2000 but above the OECD average.[[308]](#cite_note-314) Since 2005, smoking in public places has been restricted to "specially ventilated rooms".[[309]](#cite_note-315)

Since 1978, the state has run a universal public healthcare system.[[310]](#cite_note-316) However, healthcare is provided to all citizens and residents by a mixed public-private system. The public part is the [Servizio Sanitario Nazionale](/wiki/Servizio_Sanitario_Nazionale "Servizio Sanitario Nazionale"), which is organised under the Ministry of Health and administered on a devolved regional basis. Healthcare spending accounted for 10% of GDP in 2020. Italy's healthcare system has been consistently ranked among the best in the world;[[311]](#cite_note-317) according to research by the [World Health Organization](/wiki/World_Health_Organization "World Health Organization") (WHO) dating back to 2000, Italy had the second best healthcare system in the world in terms of spending efficiency and access to public care for citizens, after France.[[312]](#cite_note-318)

## Culture

Main article: [Culture of Italy](/wiki/Culture_of_Italy "Culture of Italy")

Italy is one of the primary birthplaces of [Western civilisation](/wiki/Western_civilisation "Western civilisation") and a [cultural superpower](/wiki/Cultural_superpower "Cultural superpower").[[313]](#cite_note-319) Its culture has been shaped by a multitude of regional customs and local centres of power and patronage.[[314]](#cite_note-320) The country has made substantial contributions to the [cultural](/wiki/Culture_of_Europe "Culture of Europe") and [historical](/wiki/History_of_Europe "History of Europe") heritage of Europe.[[315]](#cite_note-321)

### Architecture

Main article: [Italian architecture](/wiki/Italian_architecture "Italian architecture")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b0/Reggia_di_Caserta_-_panoramio_-_Carlo_Pelagalli_%282%29.jpg/250px-Reggia_di_Caserta_-_panoramio_-_Carlo_Pelagalli_%282%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Reggia_di_Caserta_-_panoramio_-_Carlo_Pelagalli_(2).jpg)

The [Royal Palace of Caserta](/wiki/Royal_Palace_of_Caserta "Royal Palace of Caserta") is the largest former royal residence in the world.[[316]](#cite_note-322)[[317]](#cite_note-323)

Italy is known for its architectural achievements,[[318]](#cite_note-324) such as the construction of arches, domes, and similar structures by ancient Rome, the founding of the [Renaissance architectural movement](/wiki/Renaissance_architecture "Renaissance architecture") in the late 14th to 16th centuries, and as the home of [Palladianism](/wiki/Palladianism "Palladianism"), a style that inspired movements such as [Neoclassical architecture](/wiki/Neoclassical_architecture "Neoclassical architecture") and influenced designs of country houses all over the world, notably in the UK and US during the late 17th to early 20th centuries.

The first to begin a recognised sequence of designs were the Greeks and the Etruscans, progressing to classical Roman,[[319]](#cite_note-325) then the revival of the classical Roman era during the Renaissance, and evolving into the Baroque era. The Christian concept of the basilica, a style that came to dominate in the Middle Ages, was invented in Rome.[[320]](#cite_note-326) [Romanesque architecture](/wiki/Romanesque_architecture "Romanesque architecture"), which flourished from approximately 800 to 1100 AD, was one of the most fruitful and creative periods in Italian architecture, when masterpieces, such as the [Leaning Tower of Pisa](/wiki/Leaning_Tower_of_Pisa "Leaning Tower of Pisa") and the [Basilica of Sant'Ambrogio](/wiki/Basilica_of_Sant%27Ambrogio "Basilica of Sant'Ambrogio") in Milan, were built. It was known for its usage of Roman arches, stained glass windows, and curved columns. The main innovation of Italian Romanesque architecture was the [vault](/wiki/Vault_(architecture) "Vault (architecture)"), which had never been seen in Western architecture.[[321]](#cite_note-327)

Italian architecture significantly evolved during the [Renaissance](/wiki/Renaissance "Renaissance"). [Filippo Brunelleschi](/wiki/Filippo_Brunelleschi "Filippo Brunelleschi") contributed to architectural design with his dome for the [Cathedral of Florence](/wiki/Florence_Cathedral "Florence Cathedral"), a feat of engineering not seen since antiquity.[[322]](#cite_note-328)
A popular achievement of Italian Renaissance architecture was [St. Peter's Basilica](/wiki/St._Peter%27s_Basilica "St. Peter's Basilica"), designed by [Donato Bramante](/wiki/Donato_Bramante "Donato Bramante") in the early 16th century. [Andrea Palladio](/wiki/Andrea_Palladio "Andrea Palladio") influenced architects throughout Western Europe with the villas and palaces he designed.[[323]](#cite_note-329)

The [Baroque period](/wiki/Baroque_architecture "Baroque architecture") produced outstanding Italian architects. The most original work of late Baroque and [Rococo](/wiki/Rococo "Rococo") architecture is the [Palazzina di caccia of Stupinigi](/wiki/Palazzina_di_caccia_of_Stupinigi "Palazzina di caccia of Stupinigi").[[324]](#cite_note-330) In 1752, [Luigi Vanvitelli](/wiki/Luigi_Vanvitelli "Luigi Vanvitelli") began the construction of the [Royal Palace of Caserta](/wiki/Royal_Palace_of_Caserta "Royal Palace of Caserta").[[325]](#cite_note-331) In the late 18th and early 19th centuries, Italy was influenced by the [Neoclassical architectural](/wiki/Neoclassical_architecture "Neoclassical architecture") movement. Villas, palaces, gardens, interiors, and art began again to be based on ancient Roman and Greek themes.[[326]](#cite_note-332)

During the [Fascist period](/wiki/Italian_fascism "Italian fascism"), the supposedly "[Novecento movement](/wiki/Novecento_movement "Novecento movement")" flourished, based on the rediscovery of imperial Rome. [Marcello Piacentini](/wiki/Marcello_Piacentini "Marcello Piacentini"), responsible for the urban transformations of cities, devised a form of simplified Neoclassicism.[[327]](#cite_note-333)

### Visual art

Main article: [Italian art](/wiki/Italian_art "Italian art")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/08/Leonardo_da_Vinci_%281452-1519%29_-_The_Last_Supper_%281495-1498%29.jpg/250px-Leonardo_da_Vinci_%281452-1519%29_-_The_Last_Supper_%281495-1498%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Leonardo_da_Vinci_(1452-1519)_-_The_Last_Supper_(1495-1498).jpg)

*[The Last Supper](/wiki/The_Last_Supper_(Leonardo_da_Vinci) "The Last Supper (Leonardo da Vinci)")* (1494–1499), [Leonardo da Vinci](/wiki/Leonardo_da_Vinci "Leonardo da Vinci"), [Church of Santa Maria delle Grazie](/wiki/Santa_Maria_delle_Grazie,_Milan "Santa Maria delle Grazie, Milan"), Milan

The history of Italian visual arts is significant to [Western painting](/wiki/Western_painting "Western painting"). [Roman art](/wiki/Roman_art "Roman art") was influenced by Greece and can be taken as a descendant of ancient Greek painting. The only surviving Roman paintings are wall paintings.[[328]](#cite_note-334) These may contain the first examples of [trompe-l'œil](/wiki/Trompe-l%27%C5%93il "Trompe-l'œil"), pseudo-perspective, and pure landscape.[[329]](#cite_note-335)

The Italian Renaissance is considered to be the [golden age](/wiki/Golden_Age_(metaphor) "Golden Age (metaphor)") of painting, spanning from the 14th through the mid-17th centuries and having significant influence outside Italy. Artists such as [Masaccio](/wiki/Masaccio "Masaccio"), [Filippo Lippi](/wiki/Filippo_Lippi "Filippo Lippi"), [Tintoretto](/wiki/Tintoretto "Tintoretto"), [Sandro Botticelli](/wiki/Sandro_Botticelli "Sandro Botticelli"), [Leonardo da Vinci](/wiki/Leonardo_da_Vinci "Leonardo da Vinci"), [Michelangelo](/wiki/Michelangelo "Michelangelo"), [Raphael](/wiki/Raphael "Raphael"), and [Titian](/wiki/Titian "Titian") took painting to a higher level through the use of [perspective](/wiki/Perspective_(graphical) "Perspective (graphical)"). Michelangelo was also active as a sculptor; his works include masterpieces such as *[David](/wiki/David_(Michelangelo) "David (Michelangelo)")*, *[Pietà](/wiki/Piet%C3%A0_(Michelangelo) "Pietà (Michelangelo)")*, and *[Moses](/wiki/Moses_(Michelangelo) "Moses (Michelangelo)")*.

In the 15th and 16th centuries, the [High Renaissance](/wiki/High_Renaissance "High Renaissance") gave rise to a stylised art known as [Mannerism](/wiki/Mannerism "Mannerism"). In place of the balanced compositions and rational approach to perspective that characterised art at the dawn of the 16th century, the Mannerists sought instability, artifice, and doubt. The unperturbed faces and gestures of [Piero della Francesca](/wiki/Piero_della_Francesca "Piero della Francesca") and the calm Virgins of Raphael were replaced by the troubled expressions of [Pontormo](/wiki/Pontormo "Pontormo") and emotional intensity of [El Greco](/wiki/El_Greco "El Greco").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0b/Sandro_Botticelli_-_La_nascita_di_Venere_-_Google_Art_Project_-_edited.jpg/250px-Sandro_Botticelli_-_La_nascita_di_Venere_-_Google_Art_Project_-_edited.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sandro_Botticelli_-_La_nascita_di_Venere_-_Google_Art_Project_-_edited.jpg)

*[The Birth of Venus](/wiki/The_Birth_of_Venus "The Birth of Venus")* (1484–1486), [Sandro Botticelli](/wiki/Sandro_Botticelli "Sandro Botticelli"), [Uffizi Gallery](/wiki/Uffizi_Gallery "Uffizi Gallery"), Florence

In the 17th century, among the greatest painters of [Italian Baroque](/wiki/Italian_Baroque "Italian Baroque") are [Caravaggio](/wiki/Caravaggio "Caravaggio"), [Artemisia Gentileschi](/wiki/Artemisia_Gentileschi "Artemisia Gentileschi"), [Carlo Saraceni](/wiki/Carlo_Saraceni "Carlo Saraceni"), and [Bartolomeo Manfredi](/wiki/Bartolomeo_Manfredi "Bartolomeo Manfredi"). In the 18th century, [Italian Rococo](/wiki/Italian_Rococo_art "Italian Rococo art") was mainly inspired by [French Rococo](/w/index.php?title=French_Rococo&action=edit&redlink=1 "French Rococo (page does not exist)"). Italian Neoclassical sculpture focused, with [Antonio Canova](/wiki/Antonio_Canova "Antonio Canova")'s nudes, on the idealist aspect of the movement.

In the 19th century, Romantic painters included [Francesco Hayez](/wiki/Francesco_Hayez "Francesco Hayez") and [Francesco Podesti](/wiki/Francesco_Podesti "Francesco Podesti"). [Impressionism](/wiki/Impressionism "Impressionism") was brought from France to Italy by the *[Macchiaioli](/wiki/Macchiaioli "Macchiaioli")*, and [realism](/wiki/Realism_(arts) "Realism (arts)") by [Gioacchino Toma](/wiki/Gioacchino_Toma "Gioacchino Toma") and [Giuseppe Pellizza da Volpedo](/wiki/Giuseppe_Pellizza_da_Volpedo "Giuseppe Pellizza da Volpedo"). In the 20th century, with [futurism](/wiki/Futurism "Futurism"), Italy rose again as a seminal country for evolution in painting and sculpture. Futurism was succeeded by the metaphysical paintings of [Giorgio de Chirico](/wiki/Giorgio_de_Chirico "Giorgio de Chirico"), who exerted an influence on the [surrealists](/wiki/Surrealists "Surrealists").[[330]](#cite_note-336)

### Literature

Main article: [Italian literature](/wiki/Italian_literature "Italian literature")

Formal Latin literature began in 240 BC, when the first stage play was performed in Rome.[[331]](#cite_note-337) Latin literature was, and is, highly influential, with numerous writers, poets, philosophers, and historians, such as [Pliny the Elder](/wiki/Pliny_the_Elder "Pliny the Elder"), [Pliny the Younger](/wiki/Pliny_the_Younger "Pliny the Younger"), [Virgil](/wiki/Virgil "Virgil"), [Horace](/wiki/Horace "Horace"), [Propertius](/wiki/Propertius "Propertius"), [Ovid](/wiki/Ovid "Ovid"), and [Livy](/wiki/Livy "Livy"). The Romans were famous for their oral tradition, poetry, drama, and epigrams.[[332]](#cite_note-338) In the early 13th century, [Francis of Assisi](/wiki/Francis_of_Assisi "Francis of Assisi") was the first Italian poet, with his religious song *[Canticle of the Sun](/wiki/Canticle_of_the_Sun "Canticle of the Sun")*.[[333]](#cite_note-339)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/ba/Dante03.jpg/250px-Dante03.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dante03.jpg)

[Dante Alighieri](/wiki/Dante_Alighieri "Dante Alighieri"), whose works helped establish modern Italian language, is considered one of the greatest poets of the [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages"). His epic poem *[Divine Comedy](/wiki/Divine_Comedy "Divine Comedy")* ranks among the finest works of [world literature](/wiki/World_literature "World literature").

At the court of [Emperor Frederick II](/wiki/Emperor_Frederick_II "Emperor Frederick II") in Sicily, in the 13th century, lyrics modelled on Provençal forms and themes were written in a refined version of the local vernacular. One of these poets was [Giacomo da Lentini](/wiki/Giacomo_da_Lentini "Giacomo da Lentini"), inventor of the [sonnet](/wiki/Sonnet "Sonnet") form; the most famous early sonneteer was [Petrarch](/wiki/Petrarch "Petrarch").[[334]](#cite_note-340)

[Guido Guinizelli](/wiki/Guido_Guinizelli "Guido Guinizelli") is the founder of the *[Dolce Stil Novo](/wiki/Dolce_Stil_Novo "Dolce Stil Novo")*, a school that added a philosophical dimension to love poetry. This new understanding of love, expressed in a smooth style, influenced the Florentine poet [Dante Alighieri](/wiki/Dante_Alighieri "Dante Alighieri"), who established the basis of modern Italian. Dante's work, *[Divine Comedy](/wiki/Divine_Comedy "Divine Comedy")*, is among the finest in literature.[[335]](#cite_note-Bloom-341) Petrarch and [Giovanni Boccaccio](/wiki/Giovanni_Boccaccio "Giovanni Boccaccio") sought and imitated the works of antiquity and cultivated their own artistic personalities. Petrarch achieved fame through his collection of poems, *[Il Canzoniere](/wiki/Il_Canzoniere "Il Canzoniere")*. Equally influential was Boccaccio's *[The Decameron](/wiki/The_Decameron "The Decameron")*, a very popular collection of short stories.[[336]](#cite_note-342)

Renaissance authors' works include [Niccolò Machiavelli](/wiki/Niccol%C3%B2_Machiavelli "Niccolò Machiavelli")'s *[The Prince](/wiki/The_Prince "The Prince")*, an essay on political science in which the "effectual truth" is taken to be more important than any abstract ideal. [Giovanni Francesco Straparola](/wiki/Giovanni_Francesco_Straparola "Giovanni Francesco Straparola") and [Giambattista Basile](/wiki/Giambattista_Basile "Giambattista Basile"), who wrote *[The Facetious Nights of Straparola](/wiki/The_Facetious_Nights_of_Straparola "The Facetious Nights of Straparola")* (1550–55) and the *[Pentamerone](/wiki/Pentamerone "Pentamerone")* (1634), respectively, printed some of the first known versions of fairy tales in Europe.[[337]](#cite_note-343) The Baroque period produced the clear scientific prose of [Galileo](/wiki/Galileo "Galileo"). In the 17th century, the [Arcadians](/wiki/Academy_of_Arcadia "Academy of Arcadia") began a movement to restore simplicity and classical restraint to poetry.[[338]](#cite_note-344)

Italian writers embraced Romanticism in the 19th century; it coincided with ideas of the [Risorgimento](/wiki/Risorgimento "Risorgimento"), the movement that brought Italian unification. Unification was heralded by the poets [Vittorio Alfieri](/wiki/Vittorio_Alfieri "Vittorio Alfieri"), [Ugo Foscolo](/wiki/Ugo_Foscolo "Ugo Foscolo"), and [Giacomo Leopardi](/wiki/Giacomo_Leopardi "Giacomo Leopardi"). Works by [Alessandro Manzoni](/wiki/Alessandro_Manzoni "Alessandro Manzoni"), the leading Italian Romantic, are a symbol of Italian unification for their patriotic message and because of his efforts in the development of modern, unified Italian.[[339]](#cite_note-345)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e2/Portrait_of_Niccol%C3%B2_Machiavelli_by_Santi_di_Tito.jpg/250px-Portrait_of_Niccol%C3%B2_Machiavelli_by_Santi_di_Tito.jpg)](/wiki/File:Portrait_of_Niccol%C3%B2_Machiavelli_by_Santi_di_Tito.jpg)

[Machiavelli](/wiki/Machiavelli "Machiavelli"), the founder of modern [political science](/wiki/Political_science "Political science")

In the late 19th century, a literary movement called *[verismo](/wiki/Verismo_(literature) "Verismo (literature)")*, which extolled realism, played a major role in Italian literature. [Emilio Salgari](/wiki/Emilio_Salgari "Emilio Salgari"), a writer of action-adventure [swashbucklers](/wiki/Swashbuckler "Swashbuckler") and a pioneer of science fiction, published his *[Sandokan](/wiki/Sandokan "Sandokan")* series.[[340]](#cite_note-346) In 1883, [Carlo Collodi](/wiki/Carlo_Collodi "Carlo Collodi") published *[The Adventures of Pinocchio](/wiki/The_Adventures_of_Pinocchio "The Adventures of Pinocchio")*, which became the most celebrated children's classic by an Italian author and one of the world's [most translated](/wiki/List_of_literary_works_by_number_of_translations "List of literary works by number of translations") non-religious books.[[341]](#cite_note-347) A movement called [futurism](/wiki/Futurism "Futurism") influenced literature in the early 20th century. [Filippo Tommaso Marinetti](/wiki/Filippo_Tommaso_Marinetti "Filippo Tommaso Marinetti") wrote *[Manifesto of Futurism](/wiki/Manifesto_of_Futurism "Manifesto of Futurism")* and called for the use of language and metaphors that glorified the speed, dynamism, and violence of the machine age.[[342]](#cite_note-348)

Modern literary figures and Nobel laureates are [Gabriele D'Annunzio](/wiki/Gabriele_D%27Annunzio "Gabriele D'Annunzio"), nationalist poet [Giosuè Carducci](/wiki/Giosu%C3%A8_Carducci "Giosuè Carducci") 1906 Nobel laureate, realist writer [Grazia Deledda](/wiki/Grazia_Deledda "Grazia Deledda") 1926 laureate, modern theatre author [Luigi Pirandello](/wiki/Luigi_Pirandello "Luigi Pirandello") in 1936, short story writer [Italo Calvino](/wiki/Italo_Calvino "Italo Calvino") in 1960, poets [Salvatore Quasimodo](/wiki/Salvatore_Quasimodo "Salvatore Quasimodo") in 1959 and [Eugenio Montale](/wiki/Eugenio_Montale "Eugenio Montale") in 1975, [Umberto Eco](/wiki/Umberto_Eco "Umberto Eco") in 1980, and satirist and theatre author [Dario Fo](/wiki/Dario_Fo "Dario Fo") in 1997.[[343]](#cite_note-349)

### Philosophy

Main article: [Italian philosophy](/wiki/Italian_philosophy "Italian philosophy")

Italian philosophy had an influence on [Western philosophy](/wiki/Western_philosophy "Western philosophy"), beginning with the Greeks and Romans, and Renaissance humanism, the [Age of Enlightenment](/wiki/Age_of_Enlightenment "Age of Enlightenment"), and [modern philosophy](/wiki/Modern_philosophy "Modern philosophy").[[344]](#cite_note-Garin-350) Formal philosophy was introduced to Italy by [Pythagoras](/wiki/Pythagoras "Pythagoras"), founder of the Italian school of philosophy in [Crotone](/wiki/Crotone "Crotone").[[345]](#cite_note-351) Italian philosophers of the Greek period include [Xenophanes](/wiki/Xenophanes "Xenophanes"), [Parmenides](/wiki/Parmenides "Parmenides"), and [Zeno](/wiki/Zeno_of_Elea "Zeno of Elea"). Roman philosophers include [Cicero](/wiki/Cicero "Cicero"), [Lucretius](/wiki/Lucretius "Lucretius"), [Seneca the Younger](/wiki/Seneca_the_Younger "Seneca the Younger"), [Plutarch](/wiki/Plutarch "Plutarch"), [Epictetus](/wiki/Epictetus "Epictetus"), [Marcus Aurelius](/wiki/Marcus_Aurelius "Marcus Aurelius"), and [Augustine of Hippo](/wiki/Augustine_of_Hippo "Augustine of Hippo").[[344]](#cite_note-Garin-350)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6b/Famous_Italian_philosophers.jpg/250px-Famous_Italian_philosophers.jpg)](/wiki/File:Famous_Italian_philosophers.jpg)

Clockwise from top left: [Aquinas](/wiki/Aquinas "Aquinas"), theologian;[[346]](#cite_note-352) [Bruno](/wiki/Giordano_Bruno "Giordano Bruno"), [cosmologist](/wiki/Cosmologist "Cosmologist");[[347]](#cite_note-353) [Beccaria](/wiki/Cesare_Beccaria "Cesare Beccaria"), [criminologist](/wiki/Criminologist "Criminologist");[[348]](#cite_note-Hostettler-2011-354) and [Montessori](/wiki/Maria_Montessori "Maria Montessori"), of [Montessori education](/wiki/Montessori_education "Montessori education")[[349]](#cite_note-Montessori-355)

Italian medieval philosophy was mainly Christian, and included theologians such as [Thomas Aquinas](/wiki/Thomas_Aquinas "Thomas Aquinas"), a classical proponent of [natural theology](/wiki/Natural_theology "Natural theology"), who reintroduced [Aristotelian philosophy](/wiki/Aristotelian_philosophy "Aristotelian philosophy") to Christianity.[[350]](#cite_note-356) Renaissance philosophers include: [Giordano Bruno](/wiki/Giordano_Bruno "Giordano Bruno"), a major scientific figure of the West; [Marsilio Ficino](/wiki/Marsilio_Ficino "Marsilio Ficino"), a humanist philosopher; and [Niccolò Machiavelli](/wiki/Niccol%C3%B2_Machiavelli "Niccolò Machiavelli"), a founder of modern [political science](/wiki/Political_science "Political science"). Machiavelli's most famous work is *[The Prince](/wiki/The_Prince "The Prince")*, whose contribution to political thought is the fundamental break between political [idealism](/wiki/Idealism "Idealism") and [realism](/wiki/Realism_(international_relations) "Realism (international relations)").[[351]](#cite_note-357)[[352]](#cite_note-358) University cities such as Padua, Bologna, and Naples remained centres of scholarship, with philosophers such as [Giambattista Vico](/wiki/Giambattista_Vico "Giambattista Vico").[[353]](#cite_note-maritain.nd.edu-359) [Cesare Beccaria](/wiki/Cesare_Beccaria "Cesare Beccaria") was a significant Enlightenment figure and a father of [classical criminal theory](/wiki/Classical_school_(criminology) "Classical school (criminology)") and [penology](/wiki/Penology "Penology").[[348]](#cite_note-Hostettler-2011-354)

Italy had a renowned philosophical movement in the 1800s, with [idealism](/wiki/Idealism "Idealism"), [sensism](/wiki/Sensism "Sensism"), and [empiricism](/wiki/Empiricism "Empiricism").[[353]](#cite_note-maritain.nd.edu-359) During the late 19th and 20th centuries, there were other movements that gained popularity, such as [Ontologism](/wiki/Ontologism "Ontologism"),[[354]](#cite_note-360) [anarchism](/wiki/Anarchism_in_Italy "Anarchism in Italy"), communism, socialism, futurism, fascism, and Christian democracy.[[355]](#cite_note-361) [Antonio Gramsci](/wiki/Antonio_Gramsci "Antonio Gramsci") remains a relevant philosopher within communist theory, credited with creating the theory of [cultural hegemony](/wiki/Cultural_hegemony "Cultural hegemony"). Italian philosophers were influential in development of the non-Marxist [liberal socialism](/wiki/Liberal_socialism "Liberal socialism") philosophy. In the 1960s, left-wing activists adopted the [anti-authoritarian](/wiki/Anti-authoritarian "Anti-authoritarian") pro-working class theories that became known as [autonomism](/wiki/Autonomism "Autonomism") and [workerism](/wiki/Workerism "Workerism").[[356]](#cite_note-362)

[Italian feminists](/wiki/Italian_feminists "Italian feminists") include [Sibilla Aleramo](/wiki/Sibilla_Aleramo "Sibilla Aleramo"), [Alaide Gualberta Beccari](/wiki/Alaide_Gualberta_Beccari "Alaide Gualberta Beccari"), and [Anna Maria Mozzoni](/wiki/Anna_Maria_Mozzoni "Anna Maria Mozzoni"), and proto-feminist philosophies had previously been touched upon by Italian writers. Italian educator [Maria Montessori](/wiki/Maria_Montessori "Maria Montessori") created the [philosophy of education that bears her name](/wiki/Montessori_education "Montessori education").[[349]](#cite_note-Montessori-355) [Giuseppe Peano](/wiki/Giuseppe_Peano "Giuseppe Peano") was a founder of analytic philosophy and the contemporary philosophy of mathematics. Analytic philosophers include [Carlo Penco](/wiki/Carlo_Penco "Carlo Penco"), [Gloria Origgi](/wiki/Gloria_Origgi "Gloria Origgi"), [Pieranna Garavaso](/wiki/Pieranna_Garavaso "Pieranna Garavaso"), and [Luciano Floridi](/wiki/Luciano_Floridi "Luciano Floridi").[[344]](#cite_note-Garin-350)

### Theatre

Main article: [Theatre of Italy](/wiki/Theatre_of_Italy "Theatre of Italy")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e2/Gem%C3%A4lde_des_Hieronymus_Francken_-_Die_Compagnia_dei_Comici_Gelosi_bei_einer_Auff%C3%BChrung_in_Paris.jpg/250px-Gem%C3%A4lde_des_Hieronymus_Francken_-_Die_Compagnia_dei_Comici_Gelosi_bei_einer_Auff%C3%BChrung_in_Paris.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gem%C3%A4lde_des_Hieronymus_Francken_-_Die_Compagnia_dei_Comici_Gelosi_bei_einer_Auff%C3%BChrung_in_Paris.jpg)

[Commedia dell'arte](/wiki/Commedia_dell%27arte "Commedia dell'arte") troupe [I Gelosi](/wiki/I_Gelosi "I Gelosi") performing, by [Hieronymus Francken I](/wiki/Hieronymus_Francken_I "Hieronymus Francken I"), c. 1590

Italian theatre came about in the Middle Ages, with its antecedents dating back to ancient Greek colonies in southern Italy ([Magna Graecia](/wiki/Magna_Graecia "Magna Graecia")),[[357]](#cite_note-363) as well as the theatre of the [Italic peoples](/wiki/Italic_peoples "Italic peoples")[[358]](#cite_note-364) and the [theatre of ancient Rome](/wiki/Theatre_of_ancient_Rome "Theatre of ancient Rome"). There were two main lines along which theatre developed. The first, dramatisation of Catholic liturgies, and the second, formed by pagan forms of spectacle, such as staging for city festivals, court preparations of jesters, and songs of the [troubadours](/wiki/Troubadour "Troubadour").[[359]](#cite_note-365) Renaissance theatre marked the beginning of modern theatre. Ancient theatrical texts were translated and staged at courts, and moved to public theatres. In the late 15th century, the cities of [Ferrara](/wiki/Ferrara "Ferrara") and Rome were important for the rediscovery and renewal of theatre.[[360]](#cite_note-366)

During the 16th into the 18th century, [commedia dell'arte](/wiki/Commedia_dell%27arte "Commedia dell'arte") was a form of [improvisational theatre](/wiki/Improvisational_theatre "Improvisational theatre"), and is still performed. Travelling troupes of players set up an outdoor stage and provided amusement in the form of [juggling](/wiki/Juggling "Juggling"), [acrobatics](/wiki/Acrobatics "Acrobatics"), and humorous plays. Plays did not originate from written drama, but scenarios called *[lazzi](/wiki/Lazzi "Lazzi")*, loose frameworks around which actors would improvise. The characters of the *commedia* usually represent fixed social types and stock characters, each of which has a distinct [costume](/wiki/Costumes_in_commedia_dell%27arte "Costumes in commedia dell'arte").[[361]](#cite_note-367) The first recorded commedia dell'arte performances came from Rome as early as 1551.[[362]](#cite_note-368) Female roles were played by women, documented as early as the 1560s, making them the first known professional actresses in Europe since antiquity. [Lucrezia Di Siena](/wiki/Lucrezia_Di_Siena "Lucrezia Di Siena"), named on a 1564 contract, has been referred to as the first Italian actress known by name, with [Vincenza Armani](/wiki/Vincenza_Armani "Vincenza Armani") and [Barbara Flaminia](/wiki/Barbara_Flaminia "Barbara Flaminia") as the first [prima donnas](/wiki/Prima_donna "Prima donna").[[363]](#cite_note-369)

Ballet originated in Italy during the Renaissance, as an outgrowth of court pageantry.[[364]](#cite_note-370)[[365]](#cite_note-371)

### Music

Main article: [Music of Italy](/wiki/Music_of_Italy "Music of Italy")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f6/Old_violin.jpg/120px-Old_violin.jpg)](/wiki/File:Old_violin.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a4/Pianoforte_Verticale.jpg/250px-Pianoforte_Verticale.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pianoforte_Verticale.jpg)

Instruments associated with classical music, including the violin and piano, were invented in Italy.[[366]](#cite_note-Erlich-372)

From [folk](/wiki/Italian_folk_music "Italian folk music") to [classical](/wiki/European_classical_music "European classical music"), music is an intrinsic part of Italian culture. Instruments associated with classical music, including the piano and violin, were invented in Italy,[[366]](#cite_note-Erlich-372) and many prevailing forms, such as the [symphony](/wiki/Symphony "Symphony"), concerto, and [sonata](/wiki/Sonata "Sonata"), trace their roots back to innovations in 16th- and 17th-century Italian music.

Italy's most famous composers include the Renaissance [Palestrina](/wiki/Giovanni_Pierluigi_da_Palestrina "Giovanni Pierluigi da Palestrina"), [Monteverdi](/wiki/Monteverdi "Monteverdi"), and [Gesualdo](/wiki/Carlo_Gesualdo "Carlo Gesualdo"); the [Baroque](/wiki/Baroque_music "Baroque music") [Scarlatti](/wiki/Domenico_Scarlatti "Domenico Scarlatti"), and [Vivaldi](/wiki/Vivaldi "Vivaldi"); the classical [Paganini](/wiki/Paganini "Paganini"), and [Rossini](/wiki/Rossini "Rossini"); and the Romantic [Verdi](/wiki/Verdi "Verdi") and [Puccini](/wiki/Puccini "Puccini"). Classical music has a strong hold in Italy, as evidenced by the fame of its opera houses, such as La Scala, and performers such as the pianist [Maurizio Pollini](/wiki/Maurizio_Pollini "Maurizio Pollini") and tenor [Luciano Pavarotti](/wiki/Luciano_Pavarotti "Luciano Pavarotti"). Italy is known as the birthplace of opera.[[367]](#cite_note-Kimbell,_David_R.B.-1994-373) [Italian opera](/wiki/Italian_opera "Italian opera") is believed to have been founded in the 17th century.[[367]](#cite_note-Kimbell,_David_R.B.-1994-373)

Introduced in the early 1920s, [jazz](/wiki/Jazz "Jazz") gained a strong foothold in Italy, and remained popular despite xenophobic policies of the fascists. Italy was represented in the [progressive rock](/wiki/Progressive_rock "Progressive rock") and pop movements of the 1970s, with bands such as [PFM](/wiki/Premiata_Forneria_Marconi "Premiata Forneria Marconi"), [Banco del Mutuo Soccorso](/wiki/Banco_del_Mutuo_Soccorso "Banco del Mutuo Soccorso"), [Le Orme](/wiki/Le_Orme "Le Orme"), [Goblin](/wiki/Goblin_(band) "Goblin (band)"), and [Pooh](/wiki/Pooh_(band) "Pooh (band)").[[368]](#cite_note-374) The same period saw diversification in the [cinema of Italy](/wiki/Cinema_of_Italy "Cinema of Italy"), and [Cinecittà](/wiki/Cinecitt%C3%A0 "Cinecittà") films included complex scores by composers including [Ennio Morricone](/wiki/Ennio_Morricone "Ennio Morricone"). In the 1980s, the first star to emerge from [Italian hip hop](/wiki/Italian_hip_hop "Italian hip hop") was singer [Jovanotti](/wiki/Jovanotti "Jovanotti").[[369]](#cite_note-375) Italian metal bands include [Rhapsody of Fire](/wiki/Rhapsody_of_Fire "Rhapsody of Fire"), [Lacuna Coil](/wiki/Lacuna_Coil "Lacuna Coil"), [Elvenking](/wiki/Elvenking_(band) "Elvenking (band)"), [Forgotten Tomb](/wiki/Forgotten_Tomb "Forgotten Tomb"), and [Fleshgod Apocalypse](/wiki/Fleshgod_Apocalypse "Fleshgod Apocalypse").[[370]](#cite_note-376)

Italy contributed to the development of [disco](/wiki/Disco "Disco") and [electronic music](/wiki/Electronic_music "Electronic music"), with [Italo disco](/wiki/Italo_disco "Italo disco"), known for its futuristic sound and prominent use of synthesisers and drum machines, one of the earliest electronic dance genres.[[371]](#cite_note-377) Producers such as [Giorgio Moroder](/wiki/Giorgio_Moroder "Giorgio Moroder"), who won three Academy Awards and four Golden Globes, were influential in the development of electronic dance music.[[372]](#cite_note-378) Italian pop is represented annually with the [Sanremo Music Festival](/wiki/Sanremo_Music_Festival "Sanremo Music Festival"), which served as inspiration for the [Eurovision Song Contest](/wiki/Eurovision_Song_Contest "Eurovision Song Contest").[[373]](#cite_note-379) [Gigliola Cinquetti](/wiki/Gigliola_Cinquetti "Gigliola Cinquetti"), [Toto Cutugno](/wiki/Toto_Cutugno "Toto Cutugno"), and [Måneskin](/wiki/M%C3%A5neskin "Måneskin") won Eurovision, in [1964](/wiki/Eurovision_Song_Contest_1964 "Eurovision Song Contest 1964"), [1990](/wiki/Eurovision_Song_Contest_1990 "Eurovision Song Contest 1990"), and [2021](/wiki/Eurovision_Song_Contest_2021 "Eurovision Song Contest 2021") respectively. Singers such as [Domenico Modugno](/wiki/Domenico_Modugno "Domenico Modugno"), [Mina](/wiki/Mina_(Italian_singer) "Mina (Italian singer)"), [Andrea Bocelli](/wiki/Andrea_Bocelli "Andrea Bocelli"), [Raffaella Carrà](/wiki/Raffaella_Carr%C3%A0 "Raffaella Carrà"), [Il Volo](/wiki/Il_Volo "Il Volo"), [Al Bano](/wiki/Al_Bano "Al Bano"), [Toto Cutugno](/wiki/Toto_Cutugno "Toto Cutugno"), [Nek](/wiki/Nek "Nek"), [Umberto Tozzi](/wiki/Umberto_Tozzi "Umberto Tozzi"), [Giorgia](/wiki/Giorgia_(singer) "Giorgia (singer)"), Grammy winner [Laura Pausini](/wiki/Laura_Pausini "Laura Pausini"), [Eros Ramazzotti](/wiki/Eros_Ramazzotti "Eros Ramazzotti"), [Tiziano Ferro](/wiki/Tiziano_Ferro "Tiziano Ferro"), Måneskin, and others have received international acclaim.[[374]](#cite_note-380)

### Fashion and design

Main articles: [Italian fashion](/wiki/Italian_fashion "Italian fashion") and [Italian design](/wiki/Italian_design "Italian design")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/31/Prada_milano.JPG/250px-Prada_milano.JPG)](/wiki/File:Prada_milano.JPG)

[Prada](/wiki/Prada "Prada") shop at [Galleria Vittorio Emanuele II](/wiki/Galleria_Vittorio_Emanuele_II "Galleria Vittorio Emanuele II") in Milan

Italian fashion has a long tradition. *Top Global Fashion Capital Rankings* (2013), by [Global Language Monitor](/wiki/Global_Language_Monitor "Global Language Monitor"), ranked Rome sixth and Milan twelfth.[[375]](#cite_note-381) Major Italian fashion labels – such as [Gucci](/wiki/Gucci "Gucci"), [Armani](/wiki/Armani "Armani"), [Prada](/wiki/Prada "Prada"), [Versace](/wiki/Versace "Versace"), [Valentino](/wiki/Valentino_SpA "Valentino SpA"), [Dolce & Gabbana](/wiki/Dolce_%26_Gabbana "Dolce & Gabbana") – are among the finest fashion houses in the world. Jewellers such as [Bulgari](/wiki/Bulgari "Bulgari"), [Damiani](/wiki/Damiani_(jewelry_company) "Damiani (jewelry company)"), and [Buccellati](/wiki/Buccellati "Buccellati") were founded in Italy. The fashion magazine *[Vogue Italia](/wiki/Vogue_Italia "Vogue Italia")* is one of the most prestigious fashion magazines in the world.[[376]](#cite_note-382)

Italy is prominent in the field of design, notably interior, architectural, industrial, and urban designs.[[377]](#cite_note-383)[[378]](#cite_note-384) Milan and Turin are the nation's leaders in architectural and industrial design. The city of Milan hosts [Fiera Milano](/wiki/Fiera_Milano "Fiera Milano"), Europe's largest design fair.[[379]](#cite_note-385) Milan hosts major design- and architecture-related events and venues, such as the *Fuori Salone* and the [Milan Furniture Fair](/wiki/Milan_Furniture_Fair "Milan Furniture Fair"), and has been home to the designers [Bruno Munari](/wiki/Bruno_Munari "Bruno Munari"), [Lucio Fontana](/wiki/Lucio_Fontana "Lucio Fontana"), [Enrico Castellani](/wiki/Enrico_Castellani "Enrico Castellani"), and [Piero Manzoni](/wiki/Piero_Manzoni "Piero Manzoni").[[380]](#cite_note-386)

### Cinema

Main article: [Cinema of Italy](/wiki/Cinema_of_Italy "Cinema of Italy")

Italian cinema began just after the [Lumière brothers](/wiki/Lumi%C3%A8re_brothers "Lumière brothers") introduced motion picture exhibitions.[[381]](#cite_note-387) The first Italian director is [Vittorio Calcina](/wiki/Vittorio_Calcina "Vittorio Calcina"), who filmed [Pope Leo XIII](/wiki/Pope_Leo_XIII "Pope Leo XIII") in 1896.[[382]](#cite_note-388) *[Cabiria](/wiki/Cabiria "Cabiria")*, from 1914, is the most famous Italian [silent film](/wiki/Silent_film "Silent film").[[383]](#cite_note-389)[[384]](#cite_note-390) The oldest European [avant-garde](/wiki/Avant-garde "Avant-garde") cinema movement, [Italian futurism](/wiki/Italian_Futurism_(cinema) "Italian Futurism (cinema)"), took place in the late 1910s.[[385]](#cite_note-391)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/97/Federico_Fellini_NYWTS_2.jpg/250px-Federico_Fellini_NYWTS_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Federico_Fellini_NYWTS_2.jpg)

[Federico Fellini](/wiki/Federico_Fellini "Federico Fellini"), considered one of the most influential and widely revered filmmakers of the 20th century[[386]](#cite_note-392)

After decline in the 1920s, the industry was revitalised in the 1930s with the arrival of [sound](/wiki/Sound_film "Sound film"). A popular Italian genre, the *[Telefoni Bianchi](/wiki/Telefoni_Bianchi "Telefoni Bianchi")*, consisted of comedies with glamorous backgrounds.[[387]](#cite_note-393) *[Calligrafismo](/wiki/Calligrafismo "Calligrafismo")* was a sharp contrast to the *Telefoni Bianchi*-American style comedies and is rather artistic, highly formalistic, expressive in complexity, and deals mainly with contemporary literary material.[[388]](#cite_note-394) Cinema was used by Mussolini, who founded Rome's renowned [Cinecittà studio](/wiki/Cinecitt%C3%A0 "Cinecittà"), for the production of [Fascist propaganda](/wiki/Fascist_propaganda "Fascist propaganda").[[389]](#cite_note-395)

After World War II, Italian film was widely recognised and exported until an artistic decline occurred in the 1980s.[[390]](#cite_note-396) [Italian film directors](/wiki/List_of_Italian_film_directors "List of Italian film directors") include [Federico Fellini](/wiki/Federico_Fellini "Federico Fellini"), [Sergio Leone](/wiki/Sergio_Leone "Sergio Leone"), [Pier Paolo Pasolini](/wiki/Pier_Paolo_Pasolini "Pier Paolo Pasolini"), [Duccio Tessari](/wiki/Duccio_Tessari "Duccio Tessari"), [Luchino Visconti](/wiki/Luchino_Visconti "Luchino Visconti"), [Vittorio De Sica](/wiki/Vittorio_De_Sica "Vittorio De Sica"), [Michelangelo Antonioni](/wiki/Michelangelo_Antonioni "Michelangelo Antonioni"), and [Roberto Rossellini](/wiki/Roberto_Rossellini "Roberto Rossellini"), recognised among the greatest of all time.[[391]](#cite_note-397) The mid-1940s to the early 1950s was the heyday of [Italian neorealism](/wiki/Italian_neorealism "Italian neorealism"), reflecting the poor condition of post-war Italy.[[392]](#cite_note-398)

As the country grew wealthier in the 1950s, a form of neorealism known as pink neorealism succeeded, and the *[commedia all'italiana](/wiki/Commedia_all%27italiana "Commedia all'italiana")* genre and other [film genres](/wiki/Film_genre "Film genre"), such as [sword-and-sandal](/wiki/Sword-and-sandal "Sword-and-sandal") and [spaghetti Westerns](/wiki/Spaghetti_Western "Spaghetti Western"), were popular in the 1960s and 70s.[[393]](#cite_note-399) Actresses such as [Sophia Loren](/wiki/Sophia_Loren "Sophia Loren") achieved international stardom. Erotic Italian thrillers, or *[gialli](/wiki/Gialli "Gialli")*, produced by directors such as [Dario Argento](/wiki/Dario_Argento "Dario Argento") in the 1970s, influenced horror.[[394]](#cite_note-400) Recently, the Italian scene has received only occasional attention, with movies such as *[Life Is Beautiful](/wiki/Life_Is_Beautiful "Life Is Beautiful")*, *[Cinema Paradiso](/wiki/Cinema_Paradiso "Cinema Paradiso")*, and *[Il Postino: The Postman](/wiki/Il_Postino:_The_Postman "Il Postino: The Postman")*.[[395]](#cite_note-401)

Cinecittà studio is the largest film and television production facility in Europe,[[396]](#cite_note-402) where many international box office hits were filmed. In the 1950s, the number of international productions made there led to Rome's being dubbed "[Hollywood on the Tiber](/wiki/Hollywood_on_the_Tiber "Hollywood on the Tiber")". More than 3,000 productions have been made on its lot, of which 90 received an [Academy Award](/wiki/Academy_Award "Academy Award") nomination, with 47 wins.[[397]](#cite_note-403) Italy is the most awarded country at the Academy Awards for [Best Foreign Language Film](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Foreign_Language_Film "Academy Award for Best Foreign Language Film"), with 14 wins, 3 [Special Awards](/wiki/Academy_Honorary_Award "Academy Honorary Award"), and 31 [nominations](/wiki/List_of_Italian_submissions_for_the_Academy_Award_for_Best_Foreign_Language_Film "List of Italian submissions for the Academy Award for Best Foreign Language Film").[[398]](#cite_note-404) As of 2016[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Italy&action=edit), Italian films have won 12 Palmes d'Or,[[399]](#cite_note-405) 11 [Golden Lions](/wiki/Golden_Lion "Golden Lion"),[[400]](#cite_note-406) and 7 [Golden Bears](/wiki/Golden_Bear "Golden Bear").[[401]](#cite_note-407)

### Cuisine

Main articles: [Italian cuisine](/wiki/Italian_cuisine "Italian cuisine"), [Italian meal structure](/wiki/Italian_meal_structure "Italian meal structure"), and [List of Italian foods and drinks](/wiki/List_of_Italian_foods_and_drinks "List of Italian foods and drinks")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3a/Salumi_e_vino_lucchese.JPG/250px-Salumi_e_vino_lucchese.JPG)](/wiki/File:Salumi_e_vino_lucchese.JPG)

[Italian wine](/wiki/Italian_wine "Italian wine") and *[salumi](/wiki/Salumi "Salumi")*

[Italian cuisine](/wiki/Italian_cuisine "Italian cuisine") is heavily influenced by [Etruscan](/wiki/Etruscan_civilization "Etruscan civilization"), [ancient Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek_cuisine "Ancient Greek cuisine"), [ancient Roman](/wiki/Ancient_Roman_cuisine "Ancient Roman cuisine"), [Byzantine](/wiki/Byzantine_cuisine "Byzantine cuisine"), [Arabic](/wiki/Arab_cuisine "Arab cuisine"), and [Jewish](/wiki/Jewish_cuisine "Jewish cuisine") cuisines.[[402]](#cite_note-408) Significant changes occurred with the discovery of the [New World](/wiki/New_World "New World"), with items such as potatoes, tomatoes, and maize becoming main ingredients from the 18th century.[[403]](#cite_note-409) The [Mediterranean diet](/wiki/Mediterranean_diet "Mediterranean diet") forms the basis of Italian cuisine, which is rich in [pasta](/wiki/Pasta "Pasta"), fish, fruits, and vegetables and characterised by its simplicity and variety, with many dishes having only four to eight ingredients.[[404]](#cite_note-410) Italian cuisine is noted for its regional diversity,[[405]](#cite_note-411) abundance of difference in taste, and as one of the most popular in the world,[[406]](#cite_note-412) wielding strong influence abroad.[[407]](#cite_note-413)[[408]](#cite_note-414)[[409]](#cite_note-415)

Italian cuisine relies heavily on traditional products; the country has a large number of traditional specialties protected under [EU law](/wiki/Geographical_indications_and_traditional_specialities_in_the_European_Union "Geographical indications and traditional specialities in the European Union").[[410]](#cite_note-416) Italy is home to 395 [Michelin star](/wiki/Michelin_star "Michelin star")-rated restaurants.[[411]](#cite_note-417) [Cheese](/wiki/List_of_Italian_cheeses "List of Italian cheeses"), [cold cuts](/wiki/Salumi "Salumi"), and [wine](/wiki/Italian_wine "Italian wine") are central to Italian cuisine, with regional declinations and [protected designation of origin](/wiki/Protected_designation_of_origin "Protected designation of origin") or [protected geographical indication](/wiki/Protected_geographical_indication "Protected geographical indication") labels, along with [pizza](/wiki/Pizza "Pizza") and coffee forming part of gastronomic culture.[[412]](#cite_note-418) Desserts have a long tradition of merging local flavours, such as citrus fruits, pistachio, and almonds, with sweet cheeses such as [mascarpone](/wiki/Mascarpone "Mascarpone") and [ricotta](/wiki/Ricotta "Ricotta") or exotic tastes such as cocoa, vanilla, and cinnamon. [Gelato](/wiki/Gelato "Gelato"),[[413]](#cite_note-419) [tiramisu](/wiki/Tiramisu "Tiramisu"),[[414]](#cite_note-420) and [cassata](/wiki/Cassata "Cassata") are among the most famous examples of Italian desserts.

The [Italian meal structure](/wiki/Italian_meal_structure "Italian meal structure") is typical of the Mediterranean region and differs from North, Central, and East European meal structures, although it still often consists of breakfast (*[colazione](/wiki/Italian_meal_structure#Breakfast_(colazione) "Italian meal structure")*), lunch (*[pranzo](/wiki/Italian_meal_structure#Lunch_(pranzo) "Italian meal structure")*), and dinner (*[cena](/wiki/Italian_meal_structure#Supper_(cena) "Italian meal structure")*).[[415]](#cite_note-421) However, much less emphasis is placed on breakfast, which is often skipped or involves lighter portions than are seen in non-Mediterranean Western countries.[[416]](#cite_note-422) Late-morning and mid-afternoon snacks, called *[merenda](/wiki/Italian_meal_structure#Mid-afternoon_snack_(merenda) "Italian meal structure")* (pl.: *merende*), are often included.[[417]](#cite_note-423)

### Sport

Main article: [Sport in Italy](/wiki/Sport_in_Italy "Sport in Italy")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/45/Italy_national_football_team_Euro_2012_final.jpg/250px-Italy_national_football_team_Euro_2012_final.jpg)](/wiki/File:Italy_national_football_team_Euro_2012_final.jpg)

[The *Azzurri*](/wiki/Italy_national_football_team "Italy national football team") in 2012. [Football](/wiki/Football_in_Italy "Football in Italy") is the most popular sport in Italy.

The most popular sport is [football](/wiki/Football_in_Italy "Football in Italy").[[418]](#cite_note-424) Italy's [team](/wiki/Italy_national_football_team "Italy national football team") is one of the most successful, with four [World Cup](/wiki/FIFA_World_Cup "FIFA World Cup") victories ([1934](/wiki/1934_FIFA_World_Cup "1934 FIFA World Cup"), [1938](/wiki/1938_FIFA_World_Cup "1938 FIFA World Cup"), [1982](/wiki/1982_FIFA_World_Cup "1982 FIFA World Cup"), and [2006](/wiki/2006_FIFA_World_Cup "2006 FIFA World Cup")) and two [UEFA Euro](/wiki/UEFA_Euro "UEFA Euro") victories ([1968](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_1968 "UEFA Euro 1968") and [2020](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2020 "UEFA Euro 2020")).[[419]](#cite_note-425) Italian clubs have won 48 major European trophies, making Italy the second most successful country in Europe, after Spain. Italy's top league is [Serie A](/wiki/Serie_A "Serie A") and is followed by millions of fans around the world.[[420]](#cite_note-426)

Other popular team sports include basketball, volleyball, and rugby.[[421]](#cite_note-sportface-427) Italy's male and female national volleyball teams are often featured among the world's best. The [men's team](/wiki/Italy_men%27s_national_volleyball_team "Italy men's national volleyball team") won three consecutive [World Championships](/wiki/FIVB_Volleyball_Men%27s_World_Championship "FIVB Volleyball Men's World Championship") (in 1990, 1994, and 1998). [Italy men's basketball team](/wiki/Italy_men%27s_national_basketball_team "Italy men's national basketball team")'s best results were gold at [EuroBasket 1983](/wiki/EuroBasket_1983 "EuroBasket 1983") and [1999](/wiki/EuroBasket_1999 "EuroBasket 1999"), and silver at the [2004 Olympics](/wiki/Basketball_at_the_2004_Summer_Olympics "Basketball at the 2004 Summer Olympics"). [Lega Basket Serie A](/wiki/Lega_Basket_Serie_A "Lega Basket Serie A") is one of the most competitive in Europe.[[422]](#cite_note-428) The [Italy national rugby union team](/wiki/Italy_national_rugby_union_team "Italy national rugby union team") competes in the [Six Nations Championship](/wiki/Six_Nations_Championship "Six Nations Championship"), and at the [Rugby World Cup](/wiki/Rugby_World_Cup "Rugby World Cup").

Among individual sports, bicycle racing is popular;[[423]](#cite_note-429) Italians have won the [UCI World Championships](/wiki/UCI_Road_World_Championships_%E2%80%93_Men%27s_road_race "UCI Road World Championships – Men's road race") [more than any other country](/wiki/UCI_Road_World_Championships_%E2%80%93_Men%27s_road_race#Medalists_by_nation "UCI Road World Championships – Men's road race"), except [Belgium](/wiki/Belgium "Belgium"). The [Giro d'Italia](/wiki/Giro_d%27Italia "Giro d'Italia") is a cycling race held every May and one of the three [Grand Tours](/wiki/Grand_Tours "Grand Tours"). Alpine skiing is a widespread sport, and the country is a popular skiing destination.[[424]](#cite_note-430) Italian skiers achieve good results in [Winter Olympic Games](/wiki/Winter_Olympic_Games "Winter Olympic Games") and the [Alpine Ski World Cup](/wiki/Alpine_Ski_World_Cup "Alpine Ski World Cup"). Tennis has a significant following: it is the fourth most practised sport.[[425]](#cite_note-431) The [Rome Masters](/wiki/Rome_Masters "Rome Masters"), founded in 1930, is one of the most prestigious tennis tournaments.[[426]](#cite_note-432) Italian players won the [Davis Cup](/wiki/Davis_Cup "Davis Cup") 4 times (1976, 2023, 2024 and 2025) and the [Billie Jean King Cup](/wiki/Billie_Jean_King_Cup "Billie Jean King Cup") 6 times (2006, 2009, 2010, 2013, 2024 and 2025).

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f9/Michael_Schumacher_2006_USA_2.jpg/250px-Michael_Schumacher_2006_USA_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Michael_Schumacher_2006_USA_2.jpg)

A [Ferrari 248 F1](/wiki/Ferrari_248_F1 "Ferrari 248 F1") by [Scuderia Ferrari](/wiki/Scuderia_Ferrari "Scuderia Ferrari"), the oldest surviving team in [Grand Prix](/wiki/Grand_Prix_motor_racing "Grand Prix motor racing") racing,[[427]](#cite_note-targaflorio-433) having competed since 1948, and statistically the [most successful Formula One team in history](/wiki/List_of_Formula_One_Grand_Prix_winners_(constructors) "List of Formula One Grand Prix winners (constructors)")

Motorsports are popular.[[421]](#cite_note-sportface-427) Italy has won, by far, the most MotoGP World Championships. Italian [Scuderia Ferrari](/wiki/Scuderia_Ferrari "Scuderia Ferrari") is the oldest surviving team in [Grand Prix](/wiki/Grand_Prix_motor_racing "Grand Prix motor racing") racing,[[427]](#cite_note-targaflorio-433) competing since 1948, and the most successful Formula One team with 232 wins. The [Italian Grand Prix](/wiki/Italian_Grand_Prix "Italian Grand Prix") of [Formula One](/wiki/Formula_One "Formula One") has been held since 1921[[428]](#cite_note-434) always at [Autodromo Nazionale Monza](/wiki/Autodromo_Nazionale_Monza "Autodromo Nazionale Monza") (except [1980](/wiki/1980_Italian_Grand_Prix "1980 Italian Grand Prix")).[[429]](#cite_note-435) Other successful Italian car manufacturers in motorsports are [Alfa Romeo](/wiki/Alfa_Romeo "Alfa Romeo"), [Lancia](/wiki/Lancia "Lancia"), [Maserati](/wiki/Maserati "Maserati"), and [Fiat](/wiki/Fiat "Fiat").[[430]](#cite_note-436)

Italy has been successful in the Olympics, taking part from the [first Olympiad](/wiki/1896_Summer_Olympics "1896 Summer Olympics") and in 47 Games out of 48 (not [1904](/wiki/1904_Summer_Olympics "1904 Summer Olympics")).[[431]](#cite_note-437) [Italians](/wiki/Italy_at_the_Olympics "Italy at the Olympics") have won 618 medals at the [Summer Olympic Games](/wiki/Summer_Olympic_Games "Summer Olympic Games"), and 141 at the Winter Olympics, with 259 golds, the sixth most successful for total medals. The country hosted the Summer Olympics in [1960](/wiki/1960_Summer_Olympics "1960 Summer Olympics"); and the [Winter Olympics](/wiki/Winter_Olympic_Games "Winter Olympic Games") in [1956](/wiki/1956_Winter_Olympics "1956 Winter Olympics"), [2006](/wiki/2006_Winter_Olympics "2006 Winter Olympics"), and is currently hosting the [2026 edition](/wiki/2026_Winter_Olympics "2026 Winter Olympics") in [Milan](/wiki/Milan "Milan") and [Cortina d'Ampezzo](/wiki/Cortina_d%27Ampezzo "Cortina d'Ampezzo").

### Public holidays, festivals and folklore

Main articles: [Public holidays in Italy](/wiki/Public_holidays_in_Italy "Public holidays in Italy"), [Traditions of Italy](/wiki/Traditions_of_Italy "Traditions of Italy"), and [Folklore of Italy](/wiki/Folklore_of_Italy "Folklore of Italy")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e4/Frecce_Tricolori_2022.jpg/250px-Frecce_Tricolori_2022.jpg)](/wiki/File:Frecce_Tricolori_2022.jpg)

The *[Frecce Tricolori](/wiki/Frecce_Tricolori "Frecce Tricolori")*, with the smoke trail representing the [national colours of Italy](/wiki/National_colours_of_Italy "National colours of Italy"), above the [Victor Emmanuel II Monument](/wiki/Victor_Emmanuel_II_Monument "Victor Emmanuel II Monument") in Rome during the celebrations of the *[Festa della Repubblica](/wiki/Festa_della_Repubblica "Festa della Repubblica")*

Public holidays include religious, national, and regional observances. Italy's National Day, the *[Festa della Repubblica](/wiki/Festa_della_Repubblica "Festa della Repubblica")* ('Republic Day'),[[432]](#cite_note-438) is celebrated on 2 June, with the main celebration taking place in Rome, and commemorates the birth of the Italian Republic in 1946.[[433]](#cite_note-Italian_Embassy_in_London-439) The ceremony includes deposition of a wreath as a tribute to the [Italian Unknown Soldier](/wiki/Tomb_of_the_Unknown_Soldier_(Italy) "Tomb of the Unknown Soldier (Italy)") and a military parade along [Via dei Fori Imperiali](/wiki/Via_dei_Fori_Imperiali "Via dei Fori Imperiali") in Rome.

[Saint Lucy's Day](/wiki/Saint_Lucy%27s_Day#Italy "Saint Lucy's Day"), on 13 December, is popular among children in some Italian regions, where she plays a role similar to Santa Claus.[[434]](#cite_note-440) The [Epiphany](/wiki/Epiphany_(holiday) "Epiphany (holiday)") is associated with the [folklore](/wiki/Italian_folklore "Italian folklore") figure of [Befana](/wiki/Befana "Befana"), a broomstick-riding old woman who, on the night of 5 January, brings good children gifts, and bad ones charcoal or bags of ashes.[[435]](#cite_note-441) The [Assumption of Mary](/wiki/Assumption_of_Mary "Assumption of Mary") coincides with *[Ferragosto](/wiki/Ferragosto "Ferragosto")* on 15 August, the summer vacation period.[[436]](#cite_note-442) The Italian national [patronal day](/wiki/Patronal_day "Patronal day"), on 4 October, celebrates [Saints Francis and Catherine](/wiki/Feast_of_Saints_Francis_and_Catherine "Feast of Saints Francis and Catherine"). Each city or town also celebrates a public holiday on the festival of the local patron saint.[[433]](#cite_note-Italian_Embassy_in_London-439) [Natale di Roma](/wiki/Natale_di_Roma "Natale di Roma") (lit. 'Birthday of Rome') is an annual festival held in [Rome](/wiki/Rome "Rome") on 21 April to celebrate the legendary [founding of the city](/wiki/Founding_of_Rome "Founding of Rome").[[437]](#cite_note-Plutarch12-443) According to [legend](/wiki/Legend "Legend"), [Romulus](/wiki/Romulus "Romulus") is said to have founded the city of Rome on 21 April 753 BC. It was celebrated for the first time in 47 AD.[[438]](#cite_note-penelope-444)

Festivals and festivities include the [Palio di Siena](/wiki/Palio_di_Siena "Palio di Siena") horse race, [Holy Week](/wiki/Holy_Week#Italy "Holy Week") rites, [Saracen Joust](/wiki/Saracen_Joust "Saracen Joust") of Arezzo, and the *[calcio storico fiorentino](/wiki/Calcio_storico_fiorentino "Calcio storico fiorentino")*. In 2013, [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO") included among the [intangible cultural heritage](/wiki/Intangible_cultural_heritage "Intangible cultural heritage") Italian festivals and *[pasos](/wiki/Paso_(float) "Paso (float)")*, such as the [Varia di Palmi](/wiki/Varia_di_Palmi "Varia di Palmi"), the [Macchina di Santa Rosa](/wiki/Macchina_di_Santa_Rosa "Macchina di Santa Rosa") in [Viterbo](/wiki/Viterbo "Viterbo"), and *faradda di li candareri* in [Sassari](/wiki/Sassari "Sassari").[[439]](#cite_note-445) Other festivals include [carnivals](/wiki/Carnival_in_Italy "Carnival in Italy") in [Venice](/wiki/Carnival_of_Venice "Carnival of Venice"), [Viareggio](/wiki/Carnival_of_Viareggio "Carnival of Viareggio"), [Ivrea](/wiki/Carnival_of_Ivrea "Carnival of Ivrea"), [Foiano della Chiana](/wiki/Carnival_of_Foiano_della_Chiana "Carnival of Foiano della Chiana"), and [Satriano di Lucania](/wiki/Carnival_of_Satriano_di_Lucania "Carnival of Satriano di Lucania"). The [Venice Film Festival](/wiki/Venice_Film_Festival "Venice Film Festival"), awarding the [Golden Lion](/wiki/Golden_Lion "Golden Lion") and held since 1932, is the oldest in the world and one of the "Big Three" European film festivals, alongside [Cannes](/wiki/Cannes_Film_Festival "Cannes Film Festival") and [Berlin](/wiki/Berlin_International_Film_Festival "Berlin International Film Festival").[[440]](#cite_note-446)
